So we pay attention to transshipment and all that, but overall we're going in the right direction, we're diversifying from China, and we're supporting domestic manufacturing and protecting with tariffs where we need to. How much stronger are we now than we were two years ago when it comes to trade in general? You know, I know some small business owners, this de minimis doing away with that has impacted their business, but overall the American economy, how much better off are we? Well, first of all, we've had wages go up. So when we think about trade, we think about exports, and I've already mentioned today, January and February of this year were the highest exports in U.S. history, over $300 billion each month. So we're winning on that front. Wages, especially manufacturing wages, went up by 5% last year. And this is for like non-supervisory, this is for people on the line working. We've seen manufacturing productivity go up, and in the first quarter of this year, we saw manufacturing jobs be positive in the quarter for the first time since 2023. So all of those trends are going in the right direction. Thank you Ambassador and I yield back. Gentleman yields back. Mr. Ivey. Thank you, Mr. Chair, Ranking Member. Ambassador, thank you for being here today. I want to ask you about IEEPA tariffs in the wake of the Learning Resources decision by the Supreme Court. The issue seems to be that I think 166 billion or so was collected with respect to the tariffs, and after the court declared them illegal, the issue became how will the money be refunded to the people who paid the tariffs. And there's sort of two sets of issues that I wanted to touch on. One in particular is what happens to regular people who paid money in the form of higher costs or higher prices? Is there some kind of process that's going to be put in place or that is in place for them to get a refund? The Department of Homeland Security, I guess it was CBP, put in a program that starts on Monday and that's I think it's called CAPE. And the idea there is that entities that are the importer of record or the licensed customs broker can file a request with the department and seek a declaration for repayment. And I think about 2,000 companies or so have sought that already, and there's going to be a process where they can try and recover the funds that they put in. I haven't seen what's going to happen with respect to regular people. And as you move down the supply chain at smaller businesses, I'm going to focus on individual customers here, but smaller businesses are facing sort of similar issues too, some in my district have raised this with me. So is there going to be a process in place or is there one already that will help to make sure that people at the other end of the supply chain, not just the big corporations and the like at the front end of it, but the regular people are going to have a chance to get some sort of refund for money they paid in the form of the tariffs that the Supreme Court said are now illegal. Because if you don't, what will happen is the corporations are going to get sort of double compensation. I think that is an unjust enrichment. They'll get the benefit of having charged the higher prices at the front end and they'll get the benefit of the refund through the process that you all have set up. So is there anything that's going to be done by the administration to try and address that? I see I'm short on time here. The one thing I'd say is Governor Pritzker's Attorney General asked for the money to go to the corporations, so he gets what he lays in the bed that he made. We have asked companies that they should give, if they get a refund, they should give it to their workers as bonuses. It was money in the general Treasury, it was going toward the re-industrialization of America, and the Democratic Attorneys General said no, we want to go back to the companies who import foreign goods made by foreign workers, like that's the law. If I might, I appreciate that comment, but during the course of the federal litigation, the Trump administration stated that if the tariffs were found to be illegal, they would refund the money. So there was a stipulation made in multiple cases as this litigation rose up by the administration for the repayment. What I'm not seeing though is a way to get the money back to as I stated in the predicate to the question, whether it's smaller businesses on the supply chain or regular customers. And I'll add this too, even if it's the case that state AGs only asked for that particular remedy, that doesn't block the administration from trying to find ways to refund the money for people. So for example, you guys got a letter from Governor Pritzker and he proposed $1,700 liquidated damage amount. He only spoke about the people of Illinois, but that's one possibility. There's two bills that are pending here in the House now, Mr. Cuellar, Ms. Crockett. The Crockett bill proposes putting together, the administration putting together a process to through the Department of Treasury for repayment. Mr. Cuellar does something roughly similar, but his would focus more on targeting lower-income people who made the payment. So I think there's a cutoff at $400,000. I'm not wedded to any of those, I'm agnostic on it, but I guess the question is, is the administration going to do anything to make sure that regular people who paid higher prices are going to get some money back. Because if you don't, what will happen is the corporations are going to get sort of double compensation, which I think is an unjust enrichment. They'll get the benefit of having charged the higher prices at the front end and they'll get the benefit of the refund through the process that you all have set up. So is there anything that's going to be done by the administration to try and address that? I'd see I'm short on time here. The one thing I'd say is Governor Pritzker's Attorney General asked for the money to go to the corporations, so he gets what he lays in the bed that he made. We have asked companies that they should give, if they get a refund, they should give it to their workers as bonuses. It was money in the general Treasury, it was going toward the re-industrialization of America, and the Democratic Attorneys General said no, we want to go back to the companies who import foreign goods made by foreign workers, like that's the law. Time of the gentleman has expired. Mr. Moore. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and Ambassador Greer, thank you so much for being here. In the 19th and 20th centuries, America built our industrial might on the foundation of a protectionist trade policy, abundant energy, and union knowledge and skills. Tariffs created the conditions for domestic production to thrive while unions ensured that the resultant prosperity reached the workers on the factory floor. Carrying on that tradition, the administration's re-industrialization efforts for the United States through tariffs have been vital in reorienting the American economy to manufacturing. Manufacturing jobs are high-quality jobs that provide a stable lifestyle and allow families and communities to flourish. Can you explain how a robust protectionist policy, coupled with unleashing American energy dominance and tapping into unique skills of American workers and unions, will serve to unleash a golden age of manufacturing here in the United States? Thank you. So starting really in earnest at the end of the Cold War, there was a view in Washington and Wall Street, I would say, that we should open our markets, we should lower our barriers to foreign goods, services, capital, and labor, and that in doing that, we would grow and it would all be shared and that kind thing. We now have 35-40 years of data and we know that was wrong. We lost 5 million factory jobs, we lost 70,000 factories because people wanted to lower all of our protections, they wanted to be dependent on countries like China, they wanted us to just go backwards in the face of unfair trade practice. They didn't want us to do anything. If we wanted to put tariffs on China, President Bush for a hot minute he had tariffs on steel from China and then everyone said 'No, no, no, we can't close out markets to China, we can't deal with Chinese retaliation'. So we're not going to give up on this, we are committed to this. So it sometimes can be challenging, the Chinese will retaliate, but we're going to deal with this, we're not just going to lay down and let the Chinese have their way with the American economy. So we have to be able to reverse the script and that's what we've been doing. We've been putting up tariffs to protect our manufacturing and by the way our ag producers, because our ag producers have had a trade deficit for the past few years under Biden. That's finally being cut down. And at the same time we're opening foreign markets. That's why we are exporting more than America has ever exported in its entire history of 250 years. $300 billion, over $300 billion in each of January and February. So we flipped the script. Instead of having our market just wildly open and facing closed markets overseas, we've done a moderate control of our own imports to protect domestic manufacturing and agriculture. Not having tariffs on everything, we have a lot of things that aren't covered by tariffs, but things importantly are covered by tariffs and we've opened markets overseas and that's what's creating the turnaround for us. In this has been touched on a little bit, but I just want to put a finer point on it. And I really believe that a nation that offshores its industrial capacity really offshores its deterrent capacity. And this is true both for defense industrial base, but it's also our industrial base more broadly. If we can't pour our own steel and produce the active ingredients for pharmaceuticals or making our own chips, we are at the mercy of those who do. Ambassador, can you speak a little to the national security imperative for bringing our industrial supply chain back to the United States and why that is so critical? Well, think about the pandemic in recent history. Wasn't a war but there were supply chain snarls and logistics challenges and for a time we couldn't get enough semiconductor chips that we use in cars, so we couldn't make cars. The same types of chips go into satellites and weapon systems and things like that. What about pharmaceuticals? During the pandemic there were a lot of countries that locked down the export of pharmaceuticals. So we have fundamental advanced manufacturing and basic manufacturing like steel and other things that we have to have here. We have, are we going to import? Of course we're going to import. It's not autarky we're talking about. But we have to have resilient supply chains where we can provide for ourselves. If there's a pandemic, if there's a war, if something else happens, we have to be able to have those resilient supply chains, not just-in-time supply chains like we like to have, but ones that actually provide for the security of the American people. Well thank you Ambassador Greer, and please continue the great work. I yield back, Mr. Chairman. Thank you. Gentleman yields back. Mr. Cline. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and thank you, Ambassador Greer for testifying before us today and again, you are right. For far too long the United States has been taken advantage of by unfair trade practices around the world. These persistent trade imbalances have driven manufacturing overseas, weakened critical supply chains and left our defense industrial base even more dependent on foreign suppliers. To restore both free and fair trade, President Trump has utilized tariffs on more than 60 countries, putting America first and ensuring U.S. manufacturing is no longer shortchanged by foreign competitors. Already President Trump has delivered major results, securing billion-dollar trade deals with our allies and partners, bringing thousands of jobs back to our shores and driving billion in new manufacturing investment. Despite the Supreme Court's decision earlier this year, President Trump remains committed to reshoring American manufacturing. So my question is, President Trump has already announced historic private sector investments in American manufacturing here at home. In your view, have the administration tariff policies been successful and have you seen significant evidence of companies reshoring investment back to the United States? Yes. Thank you. And I mentioned this earlier, just last week I was in Michigan and Ohio and I went to a variety of factories from very small businesses to large multinationals. One of these is Stellantis, which is the company that owns Jeep and Dodge. And due to the President's tariffs on autos that he imposed last year, they announced a $13 billion investment in the United States across the upper Midwest, and some of that included bringing back product lines from both Canada and Mexico to the United States. So that's one example. We also announced there was an announcement of a $60 million investment in the Whirlpool factory in Clyde, Ohio, which is the world's largest and oldest washing machine factory. And that has another 150 jobs. The pharmaceutical companies, they're pouring foundations and building superstructures in North Carolina and Indiana and Southern California across the country. And when we ask them why, they say tariff. They don't always love to put it in their filings with the SEC, but that's why. Well thank you, and I've been to Clyde, Ohio too, so great place. Were you named after it? No, it wasn't named after me. In your fiscal year 2027 budget request, there's an increase of $7 million, or an 8% above the fiscal year 2026 enacted level for the Office of the U.S. Trade Representative. Now I'm a businessman and I believe in return on investment, so and that needs to be positive and not negative, or otherwise why are we investing? So can you explain specifically how this additional funding will translate into tangible outcomes to positive ROI, particularly in terms of securing greater investment and expanding economic opportunities here in the United States? Certainly. So every time we consider making a trade deal, which again, the way we get the trade deals are by having tariffs. We have to have the leverage to get the deals, otherwise these countries will continue to treat us as they have for decades. And so when we have some level of tariff, you bring some money into the Treasury, and that can that's a real revenue that can be used for programs. Second, when we get the trade deals, every time we do these deals we do an assessment of what we think the market opportunity is. And so from Indonesia to India to the UK to Argentina, it's hundreds of billions of dollars in opportunity. And as I've mentioned earlier, we've seen that actually start to happen with record-breaking exports in the last couple of months. Okay, but with the increase of $7 million, I mean, what is that actually doing for us in just the, I mean, how do you monitor these trade agreements to ensure that the folks are holding to what they agreed to, or is that what this is necessary for? That's certainly part of it because we have to monitor it. One of the problems we've had over the past decades, my agency typically only has 200 to 250 people at any given time. And we believe that with this money we can have another 20 to 25 people. Because we have more trade agreements now after a year and more commitments, I need more people to monitor it, I need more people to enforce it. There's also one, there's a million dollars I'm asking for which is actually to consolidate our footprint from buildings to get rid of another lease, so I'm trying to save money for you in the long term if I consolidate this year. So I'm trying to be as respectful as I can with the taxpayer's money and bring opportunities for American workers and farmers. All right. The 2026 National Trade Estimate Report identified Korea's digital trade policies, including the KFTC's enforcement campaign and the Fairness Act as significant barriers to U.S. companies. Research by the Competere Foundation estimates that Korea's policies could cost the U.S. economy $525 billion over the next decade with American households losing nearly $4,000 each. What specific enforcement mechanisms is USTR using to hold Korea to its commitments under the joint fact sheet? And as you know, the Korean government continues its transparent discrimination against U.S. digital companies. At what point does continued Korean defiance trigger formal trade actions? Thank you. As you know, we have a joint fact sheet with Korea where they specifically agreed and committed not to use these types of laws or rules on a discriminatory basis. And so this is something where we're holding their feet to the fire. We have tools, Section 301's a tool that I've talked about today and some of you have discussed. This is something we can use if we need to. Again, I've had conversations with my counterpart in Korea, I've spoken with the Prime Minister of Korea about this issue. I know there are a lot of views domestically about big tech companies and regulation and that kind of thing. What I want is a situation where Congress gets to decide how American tech companies are controlled and not foreign jurisdictions. So we are very attentive to any suggestion of discrimination by foreign countries against our companies. You need to be leaning on them pretty hard. Their proposed Fairness Act sets market thresholds that Chinese platforms like TikTok, Temu, and Alibaba fall below, effectively exempting them from the regulations entirely while American companies are bearing the full weight of the fines and operational mandates. To put it in perspective, just a quick glimpse at the new 1260H list of Chinese military companies included some of these firms like Alibaba. Does the administration view Korea's discriminatory treatment as a national security concern, and if so, is that concern being incorporated into how you're approaching enforcement? So if they were to follow through with this and apply these laws in a discriminatory way, we would take action. Until now, they have been willing to speak to us and negotiate with us because we are concerned about this. And we are prepared to take action if we need to because there's no reason why there's some other company waiting to step into the wings other than some of these Chinese companies you're talking about. The EU is another area where we see discriminatory action being taken against US companies, including the Digital Markets Act, Digital Networks Act, Cloud and AI Development Act, Digital Services Taxes, EU Space Act, other policies, all of which disproportionately target US companies. How does your budget prioritize comprehensive action against this entire suite of discriminatory measures and what enforcement mechanisms will USTR deploy to protect American technology leadership against European regulatory overreach? So we have an office dedicated to services investment and this is an office where we have been going through and inventorying all of these unfair and discriminatory laws and practices that you're talking about. If I'm to take action on these, again here's a I keep saying Section 301. Every time I say Section 301 you have to think that's a policy person, that's one or two lawyers, that's a political person in the front office. Every time I say 301 that's more people, I'm happy to do it but I do have to have the resources to hire those people. All right. One last question before my time runs out. Given our reliance on adversarial nations like Communist China for critical materials and technology, how is your office contributing to trade strategies that reduce these dependencies, especially the critical materials? So we are right now preparing text, which we'll share with Congress before we circulate it to partners, for a plurilateral agreement on trade in critical minerals. We want to create a situation because China has broken the market for critical minerals and this is not the first time they've tried to use rare earths to control other countries' behavior. Leverage. Exactly right. And so what we are doing with trading partners who have critical minerals or who have manufacturing is we want to create an agreement where among the parties to the agreement we have a market in critical minerals among us and we try to trade at a market-based price. But if you're not in the agreement and you want to trade with one of these parties, you have to do it at a certain level. You have to have a price floor. Because in the past, the Chinese have just dumped into our market and then we have a mine that can't operate anymore because it doesn't make economic sense. And so we're trying to do an agreement with a select group to protect and create a market because it's been broken in the past. Thank you Ambassador Greer, you're doing a great job, and I yield back, Mr. Chairman. Gentleman yields back. Mr. Mrvan. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Thank you Ranking Member. Mr. Greer, thank you for your testimony today. Indiana's first congressional district is home to the nation's largest concentration of steel production. American steelmakers play a critical role in the defense, energy, and automotive construction and industrial manufacturing industries. Therefore, ensuring that domestic producers can compete globally on a level playing field is an economic and national security imperative. To that end, I appreciate the work that you have done to enhance Section 232 steel tariffs, and I hope to continue to work with the administration as co-chair of the Steel Caucus to strengthen our domestic steel industry. On the USMCA, one of the largest looming conversations in the steel industry is the review of the USMCA slated for this summer. While the initial adoption of the USMCA in 2020 made strides with strengthening certain rules of origin provisions that have benefited domestic steelmakers, trade policies in Canada and Mexico have allowed for surge of global steel overcapacity into North America steel markets. The mandatory six-year review of the USMCA presents an opportunity for the USTR to encourage Canada and Mexico to take actionable steps to stem the tide of non-North American steel imports that distort U.S. markets and harm American steel producers and fabricators. What conversations are you having with Canada and Mexican counterparts regarding the USMCA and what steps do you plan to take in order to curb transshipment of heavy subsidized steel from other parts of the world, particularly China? Thank you Congressman and we appreciate your support on this. I look forward to working with you and the rest of the steel caucus on something of just critical national security importance. Over the past year I've had a lot of conversations with my counterparts in Canada and Mexico and I've been clear with them that we cannot really have free trade with those countries in steel unless they are controlling their own borders with respect to steel to get at the transshipment issue you're talking about. For me, the gold standard for rules of origin for steel is melt and pour, which means we want it to be melted and poured in these countries. So that's my view, that's my expectation. When you say my view, you mean also the administration? Yes sir, yep. The President of the United States, the Secretary of Commerce who runs the 232, this is our view. Because we brought in steel from another country for the White House expansion, so I just want to make sure we're on the same page. Well we don't prohibit imports, but we do have a tariff on them, right? I'm not prohibiting it. I just want to make sure that you speak consistently. So please go on. Yeah we don't prohibit it. People can still import, but if they're going to if it's going to be foreign steel that's going to be paid for, it's a 50% tariff. It's the highest tariff any President's ever put on steel using Section 232. So I'm happy to work with you on that. I'm having conversations with the Canadians and the Mexicans about rules of origin on this issue. Okay, thank you for that. Northwest Indiana, largest steel producer in the country, national security along 12 to 14 thousand steelworkers and tradesmen who are in that industry. It's the lifeblood of my economy, so it's something I take very seriously. So the next step national security farmers. With the tariffs and what has happened to the farming industry, I represent a lot of farmers. I specifically want to ask the question that China's soybean market has been closed. That market was filled by Argentina. The administration sent 40 billion, or 40 million, or 40 billion dollars to Argentina while the farmers are suffering here because of the tariffs and the closing of the markets. I'm a believer of E15 and making sure that we do what we can to open those markets, but also with the closing of the Strait of Hormuz, nitrates and fertilizers are increasing by 30-40% consistently and growing season is starting now. And so the administration and you specifically have talked about lowering costs while grocery costs are rising, which is directly related to the input costs of our farmers. Please tell me how that what you would say to the farmers who believe that it was a slap in the face to give 40 billion dollars to Argentina who took over a market that was their market. Thank you Congressman. With respect to Argentina, again this is something China does. Anytime we try to take care of unfair trade practices by the Chinese who are a national security threat, the Chinese response is often to target American farmers. Correct. I think we agree on this. And then the Chinese start importing from Brazil or Argentina or other places. So it was purposely, the tariffs purposely took the soybean market away from the American farmers because you don't want them to have that market? I don't understand. What happens is when we try to take care of unfair trade practices by the Chinese, who are a national security threat, the Chinese response is often to target American farmers. Correct. I think we agree on this. With respect to the 40 billion, that wasn't a gift. I didn't call it a gift. You said you gave it to them. You give a gift. You're being presumptive. The United States forwarded 40 billion dollars to Argentina. Don't put words in my mouth. I did not say gift. The United States of America made a return on that. Very good. So I mean if you don't want us to make a return on our money, we don't have to but I think it's better that we do. They gave you a return on what? When you give a country a loan from a fund that's set up by Congress to give, to help float countries. That's what it's for. So the 40 billion to Argentina was a loan? So Congress, they control the purse, right? And you set up by statute funds at the Treasury Department that they can use to float countries. That's what it's for. So Congress or the executive gave the 40 billion dollars... no I'm sorry, returned 40 billion dollars to Argentina. Because we're losing track that the farmers... you didn't answer the question. The administration gave 40 billion dollars to a country that took their market. What do you say to the farmers who feel like it's a slap in the face that one market was closed and you enhanced the country that took their market away? We have a 25 million metric ton commitment from the Chinese every year for the next three years to buy soybeans. 25 million metric tons a year. That's what we have from the Chinese, that's what they committed to do. That's what we've negotiated. Are they currently doing that? And they they don't buy it all at once, they do it over the year. Because why would the farmers say that the market is closed if you have a commitment? We do have a commitment, but it's not being fulfilled because of the tariffs. Well let's talk about the data. If you look they they had a commitment from our last meetings from the last quarter of 2025 for the selling season and they filled that, and we have 25 million metric tons. I'm going to see with the President of the United States, the Chinese next month and we're going to follow up on this to make sure they fulfill it. Know that my farmers... Time of the gentleman has expired. Yep, I'm concluding. I thank you for the extra time. Thank you. Ms. Dean. Thank you Chairman Rogers, thank you Ranking Member Meng, and thank you Ambassador for being here today in front of us. I'm Madeleine Dean, I serve on both the Appropriations and the Foreign Affairs committees and I am co-founder of the Fentanyl Prevention Caucus. And so I know that you play a very pivotal role in economic policies including tariffs which we are talking a lot about today. So first let me say something that I agree with the President on and I congratulate the President for doing, which is eliminating the de minimis loophole. I say that because eliminating the de minimis loophole is good for U.S. businesses, it is good for individual consumers, and of course it is good in the fight against the transport of fentanyl. So thank the President for that, I have said it openly before. Ambassador Greer, I read with interest in your testimony quote, 'Our most important trade agreement is our Constitution'. Yet a few paragraphs later you state, 'The President's trade program continues despite the Supreme Court's decision in February relating to IEEPA tariffs'. That's puzzling to me. That's ironic. It's a paradox. You ground your work, trade work, in our Constitution. You emphasize that that is what the administration relies upon. And yet the Supreme Court found the President's tariffs to be unconstitutional, to be illegal. The money must be returned. Will you commit to making sure the money is returned in full without request from those who it was taken from? Automatically to those from whom it was taken. Will you commit to work with the Treasury, Customs and Border to return in full automatically? Yes or no, will you commit to that? So Congresswoman, anything the court tells us to do, we'll do, right? And so... Are you going to hide behind the court didn't speak to if we have to return the money or not? So as I was just speaking with Mr. Ivey, Customs and Border Protection has set up a program. Right? We don't just do a willy-nilly grab and just anyone comes and we give them money. They come in through the CBP program that's set up, it's a portal that opens on Monday and... I want to interrupt you. Stop because I'm aware of the program. Well that's how we give the money back. No. Here's how you collected the money. I took a look at this, this is an entry summary for an importer, a small importer. The Form 7501. I'm very familiar with that. I think you're right. Of course you're right, it is 7... very good. Then you know on 7501 IEEPA tariffs are listed here as clear as day to the penny, to the product and to the penny. There should be no application process. Return. Hit the button, return it to those who paid it. Will you commit to saying that the program needs to be changed? It should be automatic. Will you... yes or no, will you commit to that? Well Customs... well I don't control Customs. So I can't commit on behalf of Customs. Would you commit as part of the administration? No, I can't commit on behalf of Customs. As part of the administration and as the Ambassador of Trade. Yes or no? So so there's a manufacturer ID number on that form, and if you take that and you go into the portal and you put it in and you tell them I'm the manufacturer, CBP can look at it and verify. I'm going to reclaim my time. In terms of paying interest on these moneys wrongly, illegally and unconstitutionally gotten by this administration. Interest is owed on it. Do you agree with that? If there's interest owed by statute, it gets paid. That's right. If there is interest or is there interest? I'll defer to CBP. I don't have the specifics. Do you know what the interest rate is? I think it's 6%. I don't think that's true. Okay, what is it? I think it's 4.5%. I'll take six since it was illegally gotten. But the IRS corporate overpayment rate is 4.5%, so if we can check on that. Oh you know what? No, you're right. You're right. Overpayments exceeding $10,000 go to 6%. You're absolutely right. I missed that. Oh, you check again. Let me be clear. Will you commit to making sure that interest is paid on all of these refunds, which should be automatic? If there is an issue with this or your constituent has an issue with this, I'm happy to talk to CBP about it. We shouldn't have to do it by constituent. Time of the gentlewoman has expired. May I just for the record, may I enter something for the record, Mr. Chairman? The gentlelady will respond. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. By the opinion of the United States Court of International Trade, the order written by Richard Eaton, 'These duties must now be refunded with interest and the clock is ticking, further interest is accumulating every day' with approximately six point six hundred and fifty million dollars a month. Thank the gentlelady. We have a series of votes on the floor which will require us to recess the hearing until the last vote in the series of votes on the floor. So the subcommittee will now stand in recess subject to the call of the chair. The plan will be to reconvene after the last floor vote in the series. Order. We will proceed with questions from Mr. Alford. Thank you, Dean and Ranking Member Meng, thank you so much for holding this important hearing. U.S. Trade Representative's work has real consequences for America's farmers, our manufacturers and small businesses. A strong pro-America trade agenda like this administration's focuses on strict enforcement and better deals, helps our producers compete globally and strengthens our supply chains here at home. In Missouri's fourth congressional district, we see the benefits of the President's pro-America trade policies each and every day. When our beef, pork, soybeans, manufactured goods and small business exports face unfair barriers or delayed access, the impact hits our communities immediately. We need USTR fully engaged in breaking down those barriers, enforcing commitments and expanding opportunities abroad. Today I want to understand how this budget request supports stronger trade enforcement, faster market opening efforts, and a more resilient supply chain for American industry. This committee expects transparency with clear priorities, measurable results, and consistent communications with Congress. What I hope to learn today is how USTR plans to deliver for American workers, farmers, manufacturers and small businesses. Ambassador, thank you for being here today. I really want to focus on the ag sector, in particular in Missouri. It's home to more than 80,000 farms, the second highest number of farms of any state in the nation, second to Texas. We have 16,000 farms in my district alone. Our farmers, producers and rural manufacturers continue to face significant pressure from rising input costs. And I want to make this clear, I don't like looking in the rearview mirror all the time, but a lot of those input costs were due to the war on fossil fuels from the previous administration. Farmers are trying to catch up. Demand continues to soar. How is USTR prioritizing trade actions that directly impact the cost and availability of key ag inputs such as fertilizers, chemicals and feedstocks? Thank you, Congressman, and we're always glad to hear about the robust agricultural activity. We've been as you know we've been opening markets around the world and we're cutting down that ag trade deficit that grew up during the Biden administration. When it comes to the input costs, you know we think about things like fertilizer. Just yesterday, I was in a meeting with Secretary Rollins, Secretary Lutnick and a handful of others, and we met with folks all along supply chain, fertilizer manufacturers, traders, state ag commissioners, etc. to talk about this very issue. The President's quite focused on it. On the one hand, the good news is we have a lot of domestic production of fertilizer right here in the United States. There are certain elements like potash that comes in from Canada. We don't have a lot of potash here. That was never subject to any kind of tariff or anything like that. Our sense is that the trade policy is a long-term policy, it's about reshoring, it's about manufacturing. But there are certain markets where we just don't involve oil and gas, certain hydrocarbons, LNG, etc., that's at the heart of some fertilizer manufacturing. Potash we don't have tariffs, other fertilizer we don't have tariffs on that. China continues to dominate key supply chains for inputs like fertilizers, feedstocks and industrial components. How is USTR using tariff tools and trade enforcement to reduce U.S. dependency on China and strengthen domestic production? Thank you. So as I mentioned in my opening comments, the President's tariff program, which again really extends back to his first term when he first imposed tariffs on China, President Biden kept them in place, our trade deficit in goods with China, manufactured goods and everything else, it went down by $130 billion last year. In one year, that's a pretty incredible decrease. And at the same time, it was going down with other countries too. So we pay attention to transshipment and all that, but overall we're going in the right direction, we're diversifying from China, and we're supporting domestic manufacturing and protecting with tariffs where we need to. How much stronger are we now than we were two years ago when it comes to trade in general? You know, I know some small business owners, this de minimis doing away with that has impacted their business, but overall the American economy, how much better off are we? Well, first of all, we've had wages go up. So when we think about trade, we think about exports, and I've already mentioned today, January and February of this year were the highest exports in U.S. history, over $300 billion each month. So we're winning on that front. Wages, especially manufacturing wages, went up by 5% last year. And this is for like non-supervisory, this is for people on the line working. We've seen manufacturing productivity go up, and in the first quarter of this year, we saw manufacturing jobs be positive in the quarter for the first time since 2023. So all of those trends are going in the right direction. Thank you Ambassador and I yield back. Gentleman yields back. Mr. Ivey. Thank you, Mr. Chair, Ranking Member. Ambassador, thank you for being here today. I want to ask you about IEEPA tariffs in the wake of the Learning Resources decision by the Supreme Court. The issue seems to be that I think 166 billion or so was collected with respect to the tariffs, and after the court declared them illegal, the issue became how will the money be refunded to the people who paid the tariffs. And there's sort of two sets of issues that I wanted to touch on. One in particular is what happens to regular people who paid money in the form of higher costs or higher prices? Is there some kind of process that's going to be put in place or that is in place for them to get a refund? The Department of Homeland Security, I guess it was CBP, put in a program that starts on Monday and that's I think it's called CAPE. And the idea there is that entities that are the importer of record or the licensed customs broker can file a request with the department and seek a declaration for repayment. And I think about 2,000 companies or so have sought that already, and there's going to be a process where they can try and recover the funds that they put in. I haven't seen what's going to happen with respect to regular people. And as you move down the supply chain at smaller businesses, I'm going to focus on individual customers here, but smaller businesses are facing sort of similar issues too, some in my district have raised this with me. So is there going to be a process in place or is there one already that will help to make sure that people at the other end of the supply chain, not just the big corporations and the like at the front end of it, but the regular people are going to have a chance to get some sort of refund for money they paid in the form of the tariffs that the Supreme Court said are now illegal. Because if you don't, what will happen is the corporations are going to get sort of double compensation. I think that is an unjust enrichment. They'll get the benefit of having charged the higher prices at the front end and they'll get the benefit of the refund through the process that you all have set up. So is there anything that's going to be done by the administration to try and address that? I see I'm short on time here. The one thing I'd say is Governor Pritzker's Attorney General asked for the money to go to the corporations, so he gets what he lays in the bed that he made. We have asked companies that they should give, if they get a refund, they should give it to their workers as bonuses. It was money in the general Treasury, it was going toward the re-industrialization of America, and the Democratic Attorneys General said no, we want to go back to the companies who import foreign goods made by foreign workers, like that's the law. If I might, I appreciate that comment, but during the course of the federal litigation, the Trump administration stated that if the tariffs were found to be illegal, they would refund the money. So there was a stipulation made in multiple cases as this litigation rose up by the administration for the repayment. What I'm not seeing though is a way to get the money back to as I stated in the predicate to the question, whether it's smaller businesses on the supply chain or regular customers. And I'll add this too, even if it's the case that state AGs only asked for that particular remedy, that doesn't block the administration from trying to find ways to refund the money for people. So for example, you guys got a letter from Governor Pritzker and he proposed $1,700 liquidated damage amount. He only spoke about the people of Illinois, but that's one possibility. There's two bills that are pending here in the House now, Mr. Cuellar, Ms. Crockett. The Crockett bill proposes putting together, the administration putting together a process to through the Department of Treasury for repayment. Mr. Cuellar does something roughly similar, but his would focus more on targeting lower-income people who made the payment. So I think there's a cutoff at $400,000. I'm not wedded to any of those, I'm agnostic on it, but I guess the question is, is the administration going to do anything to make sure that regular people who paid higher prices are going to get some money back. Because if you don't, what will happen is the corporations are going to get sort of double compensation, which I think is an unjust enrichment. They'll get the benefit of having charged the higher prices at the front end and they'll get the benefit of the refund through the process that you all have set up. So is there anything that's going to be done by the administration to try and address that? I'd see I'm short on time here. The one thing I'd say is Governor Pritzker's Attorney General asked for the money to go to the corporations, so he gets what he lays in the bed that he made. We have asked companies that they should give, if they get a refund, they should give it to their workers as bonuses. It was money in the general Treasury, it was going toward the re-industrialization of America, and the Democratic Attorneys General said no, we want to go back to the companies who import foreign goods made by foreign workers, like that's the law. Time of the gentleman has expired. Mr. Moore. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and Ambassador Greer, thank you so much for being here. In the 19th and 20th centuries, America built our industrial might on the foundation of a protectionist trade policy, abundant energy, and union knowledge and skills. Tariffs created the conditions for domestic production to thrive while unions ensured that the resultant prosperity reached the workers on the factory floor. Carrying on that tradition, the administration's re-industrialization efforts for the United States through tariffs have been vital in reorienting the American economy to manufacturing. Manufacturing jobs are high-quality jobs that provide a stable lifestyle and allow families and communities to flourish. Can you explain how a robust protectionist policy, coupled with unleashing American energy dominance and tapping into unique skills of American workers and unions, will serve to unleash a golden age of manufacturing here in the United States? Thank you. So starting really in earnest at the end of the Cold War, there was a view in Washington and Wall Street, I would say, that we should open our markets, we should lower our barriers to foreign goods, services, capital, and labor, and that in doing that, we would grow and it would all be shared and that kind thing. We now have 35-40 years of data and we know that was wrong. We lost 5 million factory jobs, we lost 70,000 factories because people wanted to lower all of our protections, they wanted to be dependent on countries like China, they wanted us to just go backwards in the face of unfair trade practice. They didn't want us to do anything. If we wanted to put tariffs on China, President Bush for a hot minute he had tariffs on steel from China and then everyone said 'No, no, no, we can't close out markets to China, we can't deal with Chinese retaliation'. So we're not going to give up on this, we are committed to this. So it sometimes can be challenging, the Chinese will retaliate, but we're going to deal with this, we're not just going to lay down and let the Chinese have their way with the American economy. So we have to be able to reverse the script and that's what we've been doing. We've been putting up tariffs to protect our manufacturing and by the way our ag producers, because our ag producers have had a trade deficit for the past few years under Biden. That's finally being cut down. And at the same time we're opening foreign markets. That's why we are exporting more than America has ever exported in its entire history of 250 years. $300 billion, over $300 billion in each of January and February. So we flipped the script. Instead of having our market just wildly open and facing closed markets overseas, we've done a moderate control of our own imports to protect domestic manufacturing and agriculture. Not having tariffs on everything, we have a lot of things that aren't covered by tariffs, but things importantly are covered by tariffs and we've opened markets overseas and that's what's creating the turnaround for us. In this has been touched on a little bit, but I just want to put a finer point on it. And I really believe that a nation that offshores its industrial capacity really offshores its deterrent capacity. And this is true both for defense industrial base, but it's also our industrial base more broadly. If we can't pour our own steel and produce the active ingredients for pharmaceuticals or making our own chips, we are at the mercy of those who do. Ambassador, can you speak a little to the national security imperative for bringing our industrial supply chain back to the United States and why that is so critical? Well, think about the pandemic in recent history. Wasn't a war but there were supply chain snarls and logistics challenges and for a time we couldn't get enough semiconductor chips that we use in cars, so we couldn't make cars. The same types of chips go into satellites and weapon systems and things like that. What about pharmaceuticals? During the pandemic there were a lot of countries that locked down the export of pharmaceuticals. So we have fundamental advanced manufacturing and basic manufacturing like steel and other things that we have to have here. We have, are we going to import? Of course we're going to import. It's not autarky we're talking about. But we have to have resilient supply chains where we can provide for ourselves. If there's a pandemic, if there's a war, if something else happens, we have to be able to have those resilient supply chains, not just-in-time supply chains like we like to have, but ones that actually provide for the security of the American people. Well thank you Ambassador Greer, and please continue the great work. I yield back, Mr. Chairman. Thank you. Gentleman yields back. Mr. Cline. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and thank you, Ambassador Greer for testifying before us today and again, you are right. For far too long the United States has been taken advantage of by unfair trade practices around the world. These persistent trade imbalances have driven manufacturing overseas, weakened critical supply chains and left our defense industrial base even more dependent on foreign suppliers. To restore both free and fair trade, President Trump has utilized tariffs on more than 60 countries, putting America first and ensuring U.S. manufacturing is no longer shortchanged by foreign competitors. Already President Trump has delivered major results, securing billion-dollar trade deals with our allies and partners, bringing thousands of jobs back to our shores and driving billion in new manufacturing investment. Despite the Supreme Court's decision earlier this year, President Trump remains committed to reshoring American manufacturing. So my question is, President Trump has already announced historic private sector investments in American manufacturing here at home. In your view, have the administration tariff policies been successful and have you seen significant evidence of companies reshoring investment back to the United States? Yes. Thank you. And I mentioned this earlier, just last week I was in Michigan and Ohio and I went to a variety of factories from very small businesses to large multinationals. One of these is Stellantis, which is the company that owns Jeep and Dodge. And due to the President's tariffs on autos that he imposed last year, they announced a $13 billion investment in the United States across the upper Midwest, and some of that included bringing back product lines from both Canada and Mexico to the United States. So that's one example. We also announced there was an announcement of a $60 million investment in the Whirlpool factory in Clyde, Ohio, which is the world's largest and oldest washing machine factory. And that has another 150 jobs. The pharmaceutical companies, they're pouring foundations and building superstructures in North Carolina and Indiana and Southern California across the country. And when we ask them why, they say tariff. They don't always love to put it in their filings with the SEC, but that's why. Well thank you, and I've been to Clyde, Ohio too, so great place. Were you named after it? No, it wasn't named after me. In your fiscal year 2027 budget request, there's an increase of $7 million, or an 8% above the fiscal year 2026 enacted level for the Office of the U.S. Trade Representative. Now I'm a businessman and I believe in return on investment, so and that needs to be positive and not negative, or otherwise why are we investing? So can you explain specifically how this additional funding will translate into tangible outcomes to positive ROI, particularly in terms of securing greater investment and expanding economic opportunities here in the United States? Certainly. So every time we consider making a trade deal, which again, the way we get the trade deals are by having tariffs. We have to have the leverage to get the deals, otherwise these countries will continue to treat us as they have for decades. And so when we have some level of tariff, you bring some money into the Treasury, and that can that's a real revenue that can be used for programs. Second, when we get the trade deals, every time we do these deals we do an assessment of what we think the market opportunity is. And so from Indonesia to India to the UK to Argentina, it's hundreds of billions of dollars in opportunity. And as I've mentioned earlier, we've seen that actually start to happen with record-breaking exports in the last couple of months. Okay, but with the increase of $7 million, I mean, what is that actually doing for us in just the, I mean, how do you monitor these trade agreements to ensure that the folks are holding to what they agreed to, or is that what this is necessary for? That's certainly part of it because we have to monitor it. One of the problems we've had over the past decades, my agency typically only has 200 to 250 people at any given time. And we believe that with this money we can have another 20 to 25 people. Because we have more trade agreements now after a year and more commitments, I need more people to monitor it, I need more people to enforce it. There's also one, there's a million dollars I'm asking for which is actually to consolidate our footprint from buildings to get rid of another lease, so I'm trying to save money for you in the long term if I consolidate this year. So I'm trying to be as respectful as I can with the taxpayer's money and bring opportunities for American workers and farmers. All right. The 2026 National Trade Estimate Report identified Korea's digital trade policies, including the KFTC's enforcement campaign and the Fairness Act as significant barriers to U.S. companies. Research by the Competere Foundation estimates that Korea's policies could cost the U.S. economy $525 billion over the next decade with American households losing nearly $4,000 each. What specific enforcement mechanisms is USTR using to hold Korea to its commitments under the joint fact sheet? And as you know, the Korean government continues its transparent discrimination against U.S. digital companies. At what point does continued Korean defiance trigger formal trade actions? Thank you. As you know, we have a joint fact sheet with Korea where they specifically agreed and committed not to use these types of laws or rules on a discriminatory basis. And so this is something where we're holding their feet to the fire. We have tools, Section 301's a tool that I've talked about today and some of you have discussed. This is something we can use if we need to. Again, I've had conversations with my counterpart in Korea, I've spoken with the Prime Minister of Korea about this issue. I know there are a lot of views domestically about big tech companies and regulation and that kind of thing. What I want is a situation where Congress gets to decide how American tech companies are controlled and not foreign jurisdictions. So we are very attentive to any suggestion of discrimination by foreign countries against our companies. You need to be leaning on them pretty hard. Their proposed Fairness Act sets market thresholds that Chinese platforms like TikTok, Temu, and Alibaba fall below, effectively exempting them from the regulations entirely while American companies are bearing the full weight of the fines and operational mandates. To put it in perspective, just a quick glimpse at the new 1260H list of Chinese military companies included some of these firms like Alibaba. Does the administration view Korea's discriminatory treatment as a national security concern, and if so, is that concern being incorporated into how you're approaching enforcement? So if they were to follow through with this and apply these laws in a discriminatory way, we would take action. Until now, they have been willing to speak to us and negotiate with us because we are concerned about this. And we are prepared to take action if we need to because there's no reason why there's some other company waiting to step into the wings other than some of these Chinese companies you're talking about. The EU is another area where we see discriminatory action being taken against US companies, including the Digital Markets Act, Digital Networks Act, Cloud and AI Development Act, Digital Services Taxes, EU Space Act, other policies, all of which disproportionately target US companies. How does your budget prioritize comprehensive action against this entire suite of discriminatory measures and what enforcement mechanisms will USTR deploy to protect American technology leadership against European regulatory overreach? So we have an office dedicated to services investment and this is an office where we have been going through and inventorying all of these unfair and discriminatory laws and practices that you're talking about. If I'm to take action on these, again here's a I keep saying Section 301. Every time I say Section 301 you have to think that's a policy person, that's one or two lawyers, that's a political person in the front office. Every time I say 301 that's more people, I'm happy to do it but I do have to have the resources to hire those people. All right. One last question before my time runs out. Given our reliance on adversarial nations like Communist China for critical materials and technology, how is your office contributing to trade strategies that reduce these dependencies, especially the critical materials? So we are right now preparing text, which we'll share with Congress before we circulate it to partners, for a plurilateral agreement on trade in critical minerals. We want to create a situation because China has broken the market for critical minerals and this is not the first time they've tried to use rare earths to control other countries' behavior. Leverage. Exactly right. And so what we are doing with trading partners who have critical minerals or who have manufacturing is we want to create an agreement where among the parties to the agreement we have a market in critical minerals among us and we try to trade at a market-based price. But if you're not in the agreement and you want to trade with one of these parties, you have to do it at a certain level. You have to have a price floor. Because in the past, the Chinese have just dumped into our market and then we have a mine that can't operate anymore because it doesn't make economic sense. And so we're trying to do an agreement with a select group to protect and create a market because it's been broken in the past. Thank you Ambassador Greer, you're doing a great job, and I yield back, Mr. Chairman. Gentleman yields back. Mr. Mrvan. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Thank you Ranking Member. Mr. Greer, thank you for your testimony today. Indiana's first congressional district is home to the nation's largest concentration of steel production. American steelmakers play a critical role in the defense, energy, and automotive construction and industrial manufacturing industries. Therefore, ensuring that domestic producers can compete globally on a level playing field is an economic and national security imperative. To that end, I appreciate the work that you have done to enhance Section 232 steel tariffs, and I hope to continue to work with the administration as co-chair of the Steel Caucus to strengthen our domestic steel industry. On the USMCA, one of the largest looming conversations in the steel industry is the review of the USMCA slated for this summer. While the initial adoption of the USMCA in 2020 made strides with strengthening certain rules of origin provisions that have benefited domestic steelmakers, trade policies in Canada and Mexico have allowed for surge of global steel overcapacity into North America steel markets. The mandatory six-year review of the USMCA presents an opportunity for the USTR to encourage Canada and Mexico to take actionable steps to stem the tide of non-North American steel imports that distort U.S. markets and harm American steel producers and fabricators. What conversations are you having with Canada and Mexican counterparts regarding the USMCA and what steps do you plan to take in order to curb transshipment of heavy subsidized steel from other parts of the world, particularly China? Thank you Congressman and we appreciate your support on this. I look forward to working with you and the rest of the steel caucus on something of just critical national security importance. Over the past year I've had a lot of conversations with my counterparts in Canada and Mexico and I've been clear with them that we cannot really have free trade with those countries in steel unless they are controlling their own borders with respect to steel to get at the transshipment issue you're talking about. For me, the gold standard for rules of origin for steel is melt and pour, which means we want it to be melted and poured in these countries. So that's my view, that's my expectation. When you say my view, you mean also the administration? Yes sir, yep. The President of the United States, the Secretary of Commerce who runs the 232, this is our view. Because we brought in steel from another country for the White House expansion, so I just want to make sure we're on the same page. Well we don't prohibit imports, but we do have a tariff on them, right? I'm not prohibiting it. I just want to make sure that you speak consistently. So please go on. Yeah we don't prohibit it. People can still import, but if they're going to if it's going to be foreign steel that's going to be paid for, it's a 50% tariff. It's the highest tariff any President's ever put on steel using Section 232. So I'm happy to work with you on that. I'm having conversations with the Canadians and the Mexicans about rules of origin on this issue. Okay, thank you for that. Northwest Indiana, largest steel producer in the country, national security along 12 to 14 thousand steelworkers and tradesmen who are in that industry. It's the lifeblood of my economy, so it's something I take very seriously. So the next step national security farmers. With the tariffs and what has happened to the farming industry, I represent a lot of farmers. I specifically want to ask the question that China's soybean market has been closed. That market was filled by Argentina. The administration sent 40 billion, or 40 million, or 40 billion dollars to Argentina while the farmers are suffering here because of the tariffs and the closing of the markets. I'm a believer of E15 and making sure that we do what we can to open those markets, but also with the closing of the Strait of Hormuz, nitrates and fertilizers are increasing by 30-40% consistently and growing season is starting now. And so the administration and you specifically have talked about lowering costs while grocery costs are rising, which is directly related to the input costs of our farmers. Please tell me how that what you would say to the farmers who believe that it was a slap in the face to give 40 billion dollars to Argentina who took over a market that was their market. Thank you Congressman. With respect to Argentina, again this is something China does. Anytime we try to take care of unfair trade practices by the Chinese who are a national security threat, the Chinese response is often to target American farmers. Correct. I think we agree on this. And then the Chinese start importing from Brazil or Argentina or other places. So it was purposely, the tariffs purposely took the soybean market away from the American farmers because you don't want them to have that market? I don't understand. What happens is when we try to take care of unfair trade practices by the Chinese, who are a national security threat, the Chinese response is often to target American farmers. Correct. I think we agree on this. With respect to the 40 billion, that wasn't a gift. I didn't call it a gift. You said you gave it to them. You give a gift. You're being presumptive. The United States forwarded 40 billion dollars to Argentina. Don't put words in my mouth. I did not say gift. The United States of America made a return on that. Very good. So I mean if you don't want us to make a return on our money, we don't have to but I think it's better that we do. They gave you a return on what? When you give a country a loan from a fund that's set up by Congress to give, to help float countries. That's what it's for. So the 40 billion to Argentina was a loan? So Congress, they control the purse, right? And you set up by statute funds at the Treasury Department that they can use to float countries. That's what it's for. So Congress or the executive gave the 40 billion dollars... no I'm sorry, returned 40 billion dollars to Argentina. Because we're losing track that the farmers... you didn't answer the question. The administration gave 40 billion dollars to a country that took their market. What do you say to the farmers who feel like it's a slap in the face that one market was closed and you enhanced the country that took their market away? We have a 25 million metric ton commitment from the Chinese every year for the next three years to buy soybeans. 25 million metric tons a year. That's what we have from the Chinese, that's what they committed to do. That's what we've negotiated. Are they currently doing that? And they they don't buy it all at once, they do it over the year. Because why would the farmers say that the market is closed if you have a commitment? We do have a commitment, but it's not being fulfilled because of the tariffs. Well let's talk about the data. If you look they they had a commitment from our last meetings from the last quarter of 2025 for the selling season and they filled that, and we have 25 million metric tons. I'm going to see with the President of the United States, the Chinese next month and we're going to follow up on this to make sure they fulfill it. Know that my farmers... Time of the gentleman has expired. Yep, I'm concluding. I thank you for the extra time. Thank you. Ms. Dean. Thank you Chairman Rogers, thank you Ranking Member Meng, and thank you Ambassador for being here today in front of us. I'm Madeleine Dean, I serve on both the Appropriations and the Foreign Affairs committees and I am co-founder of the Fentanyl Prevention Caucus. And so I know that you play a very pivotal role in economic policies including tariffs which we are talking a lot about today. So first let me say something that I agree with the President on and I congratulate the President for doing, which is eliminating the de minimis loophole. I say that because eliminating the de minimis loophole is good for U.S. businesses, it is good for individual consumers, and of course it is good in the fight against the transport of fentanyl. So thank the President for that, I have said it openly before. Ambassador Greer, I read with interest in your testimony quote, 'Our most important trade agreement is our Constitution'. Yet a few paragraphs later you state, 'The President's trade program continues despite the Supreme Court's decision in February relating to IEEPA tariffs'. That's puzzling to me. That's ironic. It's a paradox. You ground your work, trade work, in our Constitution. You emphasize that that is what the administration relies upon. And yet the Supreme Court found the President's tariffs to be unconstitutional, to be illegal. The money must be returned. Will you commit to making sure the money is returned in full without request from those who it was taken from? Automatically to those from whom it was taken. Will you commit to work with the Treasury, Customs and Border to return in full automatically? Yes or no, will you commit to that? So Congresswoman, anything the court tells us to do, we'll do, right? And so... Are you going to hide behind the court didn't speak to if we have to return the money or not? So as I was just speaking with Mr. Ivey, Customs and Border Protection has set up a program. Right? We don't just do a willy-nilly grab and just anyone comes and we give them money. They come in through the CBP program that's set up, it's a portal that opens on Monday and... I want to interrupt you. Stop because I'm aware of the program. Well that's how we give the money back. No. Here's how you collected the money. I took a look at this, this is an entry summary for an importer, a small importer. The Form 7501. I'm very familiar with that. I think you're right. Of course you're right, it is 7... very good. Then you know on 7501 IEEPA tariffs are listed here as clear as day to the penny, to the product and to the penny. There should be no application process. Return. Hit the button, return it to those who paid it. Will you commit to saying that the program needs to be changed? It should be automatic. Will you... yes or no, will you commit to that? Well Customs... well I don't control Customs. So I can't commit on behalf of Customs. Would you commit as part of the administration? No, I can't commit on behalf of Customs. As part of the administration and as the Ambassador of Trade. Yes or no? So so there's a manufacturer ID number on that form, and if you take that and you go into the portal and you put it in and you tell them I'm the manufacturer, CBP can look at it and verify. I'm going to reclaim my time. In terms of paying interest on these moneys wrongly, illegally and unconstitutionally gotten by this administration. Interest is owed on it. Do you agree with that? If there's interest owed by statute, it gets paid. That's right. If there is interest or is there interest? I'll defer to CBP. I don't have the specifics. Do you know what the interest rate is? I think it's 6%. I don't think that's true. Okay, what is it? I think it's 4.5%. I'll take six since it was illegally gotten. But the IRS corporate overpayment rate is 4.5%, so if we can check on that. Oh you know what? No, you're right. You're right. Overpayments exceeding $10,000 go to 6%. You're absolutely right. I missed that. Oh, you check again. Let me be clear. Will you commit to making sure that interest is paid on all of these refunds, which should be automatic? If there is an issue with this or your constituent has an issue with this, I'm happy to talk to CBP about it. We shouldn't have to do it by constituent. Time of the gentlewoman has expired. May I just for the record, may I enter something for the record, Mr. Chairman? The gentlelady will respond. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. By the opinion of the United States Court of International Trade, the order written by Richard Eaton, 'These duties must now be refunded with interest and the clock is ticking, further interest is accumulating every day' with approximately six point six hundred and fifty million dollars a month. Thank the gentlelady. We have a series of votes on the floor which will require us to recess the hearing until the last vote in the series of votes on the floor. So the subcommittee will now stand in recess subject to the call of the chair. The plan will be to reconvene after the last floor vote in the series. Order. We will proceed with questions from Mr. Alford. Thank you, Dean and Ranking Member Meng, thank you so much for holding this important hearing. U.S. Trade Representative's work has real consequences for America's farmers, our manufacturers and small businesses. A strong pro-America trade agenda like this administration's focuses on strict enforcement and better deals, helps our producers compete globally and strengthens our supply chains here at home. In Missouri's fourth congressional district, we see the benefits of the President's pro-America trade policies each and every day. When our beef, pork, soybeans, manufactured goods and small business exports face unfair barriers or delayed access, the impact hits our communities immediately. We need USTR fully engaged in breaking down those barriers, enforcing commitments and expanding opportunities abroad. Today I want to understand how this budget request supports stronger trade enforcement, faster market opening efforts, and a more resilient supply chain for American industry. This committee expects transparency with clear priorities, measurable results, and consistent communications with Congress. What I hope to learn today is how USTR plans to deliver for American workers, farmers, manufacturers and small businesses. Ambassador, thank you for being here today. I really want to focus on the ag sector, in particular in Missouri. It's home to more than 80,000 farms, the second highest number of farms of any state in the nation, second to Texas. We have 16,000 farms in my district alone. Our farmers, producers and rural manufacturers continue to face significant pressure from rising input costs. And I want to make this clear, I don't like looking in the rearview mirror all the time, but a lot of those input costs were due to the war on fossil fuels from the previous administration. Farmers are trying to catch up. Demand continues to soar. How is USTR prioritizing trade actions that directly impact the cost and availability of key ag inputs such as fertilizers, chemicals and feedstocks? Thank you, Congressman, and we're always glad to hear about the robust agricultural activity. We've been as you know we've been opening markets around the world and we're cutting down that ag trade deficit that grew up during the Biden administration. When it comes to the input costs, you know we think about things like fertilizer. Just yesterday, I was in a meeting with Secretary Rollins, Secretary Lutnick and a handful of others, and we met with folks all along supply chain, fertilizer manufacturers, traders, state ag commissioners, etc. to talk about this very issue. The President's quite focused on it. On the one hand, the good news is we have a lot of domestic production of fertilizer right here in the United States. There are certain elements like potash that comes in from Canada. We don't have a lot of potash here. That was never subject to any kind of tariff or anything like that. Our sense is that the trade policy is a long-term policy, it's about reshoring, it's about manufacturing. But there are certain markets where we just don't involve oil and gas, certain hydrocarbons, LNG, etc., that's at the heart of some fertilizer manufacturing. Potash we don't have tariffs, other fertilizer we don't have tariffs on that. China continues to dominate key supply chains for inputs like fertilizers, feedstocks and industrial components. How is USTR using tariff tools and trade enforcement to reduce U.S. dependency on China and strengthen domestic production? Thank you. So as I mentioned in my opening comments, the President's tariff program, which again really extends back to his first term when he first imposed tariffs on China, President Biden kept them in place, our trade deficit in goods with China, manufactured goods and everything else, it went down by $130 billion last year. In one year, that's a pretty incredible decrease. And at the same time, it was going down with other countries too. So we pay attention to transshipment and all that, but overall we're going in the right direction, we're diversifying from China, and we're supporting domestic manufacturing and protecting with tariffs where we need to. How much stronger are we now than we were two years ago when it comes to trade in general? You know, I know some small business owners, this de minimis doing away with that has impacted their business, but overall the American economy, how much better off are we? Well, first of all, we've had wages go up. So when we think about trade, we think about exports, and I've already mentioned today, January and February of this year were the highest exports in U.S. history, over $300 billion each month. So we're winning on that front. Wages, especially manufacturing wages, went up by 5% last year. And this is for like non-supervisory, this is for people on the line working. We've seen manufacturing productivity go up, and in the first quarter of this year, we saw manufacturing jobs be positive in the quarter for the first time since 2023. So all of those trends are going in the right direction. Thank you Ambassador and I yield back. Gentleman yields back. Mr. Ivey. Thank you, Mr. Chair, Ranking Member. Ambassador, thank you for being here today. I want to ask you about IEEPA tariffs in the wake of the Learning Resources decision by the Supreme Court. The issue seems to be that I think 166 billion or so was collected with respect to the tariffs, and after the court declared them illegal, the issue became how will the money be refunded to the people who paid the tariffs. And there's sort of two sets of issues that I wanted to touch on. One in particular is what happens to regular people who paid money in the form of higher costs or higher prices? Is there some kind of process that's going to be put in place or that is in place for them to get a refund? The Department of Homeland Security, I guess it was CBP, put in a program that starts on Monday and that's I think it's called CAPE. And the idea there is that entities that are the importer of record or the licensed customs broker can file a request with the department and seek a declaration for repayment. And I think about 2,000 companies or so have sought that already, and there's going to be a process where they can try and recover the funds that they put in. I haven't seen what's going to happen with respect to regular people. And as you move down the supply chain at smaller businesses, I'm going to focus on individual customers here, but smaller businesses are facing sort of similar issues too, some in my district have raised this with me. So is there going to be a process in place or is there one already that will help to make sure that people at the other end of the supply chain, not just the big corporations and the like at the front end of it, but the regular people are going to have a chance to get some sort of refund for money they paid in the form of the tariffs that the Supreme Court said are now illegal. Because if you don't, what will happen is the corporations are going to get sort of double compensation. I think that is an unjust enrichment. They'll get the benefit of having charged the higher prices at the front end and they'll get the benefit of the refund through the process that you all have set up. So is there anything that's going to be done by the administration to try and address that? I see I'm short on time here. The one thing I'd say is Governor Pritzker's Attorney General asked for the money to go to the corporations, so he gets what he lays in the bed that he made. We have asked companies that they should give, if they get a refund, they should give it to their workers as bonuses. It was money in the general Treasury, it was going toward the re-industrialization of America, and the Democratic Attorneys General said no, we want to go back to the companies who import foreign goods made by foreign workers, like that's the law. If I might, I appreciate that comment, but during the course of the federal litigation, the Trump administration stated that if the tariffs were found to be illegal, they would refund the money. So there was a stipulation made in multiple cases as this litigation rose up by the administration for the repayment. What I'm not seeing though is a way to get the money back to as I stated in the predicate to the question, whether it's smaller businesses on the supply chain or regular customers. And I'll add this too, even if it's the case that state AGs only asked for that particular remedy, that doesn't block the administration from trying to find ways to refund the money for people. So for example, you guys got a letter from Governor Pritzker and he proposed $1,700 liquidated damage amount. He only spoke about the people of Illinois, but that's one possibility.
