"The vast majority of people are not seeing yet the way that AI is enriching them. Instead, they’re seeing the way that it’s taking things away, whether that’s employment or resources, or just the threat that it might one day take something away." — Sarah Frier, Bloomberg Managing Editor
"They [companies] have marketed this technology as... 'really, this is so powerful, we need to roll this out slowly, take our time because if we don’t, the results could be disastrous.' It is the strangest public relations strategy of all time." — Sarah Frier, Bloomberg Managing Editor
"If we’re sort of automating away the grunt work or the sort of technical work, then perhaps the work that is more about being a human is going to be much more important." — Julia Rubin, Businessweek Senior Editor
"We are now living in the Mordor economy... an economy that's all about energy. Energy prices get more expensive, so the economy has to make more energy in order to feed the energy needs, and it becomes like a snake swallowing its own tail." — Stacy Vanek Smith, Bloomberg Host
Podcasts, Radio, News. Stacy, there's been so much talk, here and elsewhere, on Wall Street and in the business press, about AI, your favorite subject. It's about saving the world, changing our lives, curing cancer, solving climate change... Talking to whales? Talking to whales, your favorite thing. Almost everyone agrees AI is awesome as far as I can tell, but there is starting to be quite a lot of backlash as well, including some very upsetting incidents. Yes, there's actually been some violence, including against OpenAI CEO Sam Altman recently. And you're right, it definitely seems like a lot of the excitement and optimism around AI has become at least more complicated. There may be an erosion in our ability to learn how to compromise with others, to learn how to collaborate with others—like people who actually give us pushback, shape how we feel, and teach us things. Yeah, this is all existential. We're going to hear about more of that from Bloomberg reporter Sarah Frier. We're talking about what these threats and attempts at harm say about this current moment and the conversation around AI. After that, Max, we are going to talk about the job market. It is tough out there, especially for young job seekers, especially if you just graduated. Businessweek's latest issue is all about that—what it is like to be young and just starting out in your career. College graduates, recent graduates, we see you. And even better, we have Businessweek senior editor Julia Rubin here to break it all down for us. It's very interesting. And this idea of, okay, if we're sort of automating away the grunt work or the technical work, then perhaps the work that is more about being a human is going to be much more important. This is Everybody's Business from Bloomberg Businessweek. I'm Stacey Vanek Smith. And I'm Max Chafkin. Jobs, AI—it is all happening this week and it is a lot, but stick around and, Max, we can just all process this together. This morning, dramatic new surveillance images of a man authorities say tried to kill OpenAI CEO Sam Altman by throwing a Molotov cocktail at his home. Investigators have charged 20-year-old Daniel Moreno Gamma with attempted murder. Stacy, just a few days ago, as we're talking now, there was this attempted arson attack on the home of Sam Altman, the CEO and co-founder of OpenAI. There's a lot to say about this and about the manifesto that this guy wrote, but I think it also maybe says something about the current sentiment—the broader sentiment about AI in general. Yeah, I think so too. This attack, by the way, is not the only one. Last week in Indianapolis, a local councilman who had approved the construction of this big new data center in the area had bullets fired at his front door along with this note that read, "no data centers." It definitely seems like some of the excitement and optimism around AI has turned, or at least gotten a little more complicated. And we've got a great person to talk about this. It is Sarah Frier, Bloomberg managing editor and longtime reporter. She wrote a book all about Facebook and kind of the last time tech was under the microscope. It's called "No Filter: The Inside Story of Instagram." Sarah, welcome to Everybody's Business. Thanks for having me. Alright, so Sarah, I feel like just a couple of weeks ago, we were talking about how the AIs were going to be our friends and they were going to do all our work and they were going to usher in a glorious new future. Now we've got these attacks. We've mentioned two, and there was actually a second attack on Sam Altman's house. When you look at the poll numbers about this, there've been a bunch of polls, and AI is pretty unpopular. I'm wondering, what's your read on this? Did something change or are people just paying attention to something that was there the whole time? Well, I think it's a big change when something goes from a cool new tool you can play with to the reason people think jobs are going away, the reason young people can't find careers when they graduate from college or grad school, and the reason the environment is under stress and energy prices are high. There are these existential things that are tied up in the progress of AI. It may be of help to us for particular tasks or questions or coding projects, but the vast majority of people are not seeing yet the way that AI is enriching them. Instead, they're seeing the way that it's taking things away, whether that's employment or resources, or just the threat that it might one day take something away. And part of that, I think, is the companies' own fault. The way that they have marketed this technology as, like, "really, this is so powerful, we need to roll this out slowly and take our time because if we don't, the results could be disastrous." They've literally been saying our technology might end the world. Sam Altman, including Sam Altman, says it might be as bad as a nuclear weapon and also it very likely will take everyone's jobs. Which is why you should buy it from us because at least we're saying the truth, right? Like, what kind of marketing argument is that? It is the strangest public relations strategy of all time. Honesty's never a good idea in PR. These companies that have spoken about the power and danger and incredible uncontrollableness of AI may actually be stoking real fear in regular people who think they have to do something about it. And of course, no condoning of violence, but you don't have to go too far in the logic to get somebody to that place where they fear AI and think that they're doing the right thing by trying to stop it. Obviously, that's the wrong way to do it. But there are people having these philosophical, even religious debates in Silicon Valley right now about what AI is bringing to change our future and how much of it we should be embracing. And then there's the other side of that, which is like, how much of that is just marketing talk? Sarah, I feel like this is such a similar thing that's happened throughout history. Like back in the 1880s, there were the Luddites and the Saboteurs who threw their shoes into the machines because they saw those machines as threatening their jobs. The Luddites would go with tools and smash the big textile machines apart. Do you see this as like that or is this different? Is this just the human reaction to an existential job threat from a new technology? I think even within the companies, there's a lot of this kind of debate. Anthropic has made this part of their brand—the fact that they're thinking all the time about how do we not just enact AI progress, but do it in a way that takes into account everything that could go wrong. Once we release it on the world, we may not be able to control it, so we have to make sure that what we release is something that is at least understood. And I think that's almost impossible because there's some level of complexity to these models where we don't exactly know how they come to the conclusions that they come to. I mean, they're deriving their conclusions from a lot of different sources, they're building upon the human knowledge that they've ingested, and they're really only as good as how they've been trained. But I think that does get us to an uncomfortable place where, on the one hand, you have people trying to do whatever they can to build as quickly as they can and take advantage of the new technology, and then you have people who are too scared to even touch it. Can I bring up a few sort of data points and some polling here? I alluded to it at the top, but in September of 2025, Pew, the big survey people, released their latest survey about how people feel about AI. The numbers are really stark. And I think you go back to how people felt about smartphones or cloud computing or any of these other recent technology developments, it feels different. In 2021, 18% of people were more excited than concerned about AI, and 37% of the people were more concerned than excited. So there was already a lot of concern about AI. Four years later, there's been a lot more AI and a lot of people have had contact with this technology, and the numbers have gotten way worse. Now, 52% are more concerned than excited, and 10% are more excited than concerned. A huge, huge majority—by far the biggest thing in this survey—is more concerned. And then the second part of this is showing up, of course, in politics. People are getting upset about data centers in their backyard, and Bernie Sanders is saying we need to have a pause on data centers. But the thing that really grabbed me in this survey was not the top line, but then they went and asked people, "Okay, what are you concerned about?" Sarah, you brought up a bunch of things. You brought up the environment and you brought up job loss. There was one other. When I heard about this I thought, okay, yeah, so people are probably worried about... it's probably economic anxiety, right? They've been hearing these stories about job loss, that's gotta be the main thing. But you look at this survey, and they asked the people who are most concerned, and the top problem was not job loss—it was "erodes human abilities and connections." That was 27%. Next was 18% for "negative impact on accuracy of information," followed by "concerns over human control of AI." Job loss is all the way at number five—the fifth most concerned. The environment, which you hear a lot about on the news, is way down there; it's almost at the very bottom at like 2%. And then my favorite is "general dislike," which is just 1%. That's the lowest one. That's you. Exactly, that's me. And the human connection, it's so interesting. I mean, they're like spiritual concerns that people have, right? They are worried about job loss, but they're also worried about these more fundamental questions. It's crazy. Loneliness. And you have people like Mark Zuckerberg who are offering up AI as an antidote to loneliness, while our social media feeds become full of AI slop. One thing I think that is different about this era of new tech compared to the past waves is this is not as clear a game of disrupting the giants as past eras of new tech have been. Normally when you have this, like, big powerful new startup, they're coming in to fight the incumbent. We've just come off of a couple decades of sour feelings about big tech. These AI giants don't exist without big tech. They're collaborating, they're in deep business partnerships and deep financial partnerships; they need each other. And I think that means that a lot of the sentiment and distrust that people have about Facebook, about Google, about all these companies that they've just built up an aversion to over antitrust, over their personal data, over all these things over the past few years, is trickling over to the AI era. People are thinking of these companies sort of in the same boat. Think of if you're like a young person today and you're being told you can't be on social media, or it's dangerous to be on social media, what are you going to do with your time? You're probably going to talk to ChatGPT. I just think that as a substitute for human connection, it's going to be a big enhancement in some ways because it's something that will always respond, will always be there, will always give you an answer. But there may be an erosion in our ability to learn how to compromise with others, to learn how to collaborate with others—like people who actually give us pushback, shape how we feel, teach us things. Yeah, this is all existential. To get a little more practical, though, AI is really... it feels like in many ways kind of holding up our economy right now. Obviously the Magnificent Seven stocks, which are all very deeply invested if not making AI, are really carrying the markets, which has been one of the real bright spots in the economy lately. I mean, how big a part of our economy is AI? Like, even if we're worried about it and there are all these mixed feelings that are surfacing, do we need it? Oh yeah, the top four tech companies are spending $650 billion on data centers this year. Like, that's insane. Not insane if you think about what the capacity needs of AI are; it's just unprecedented. And yeah, that is fueling the economy, but how much of that money is trickling down to everyday people who are going to the grocery store and seeing prices higher than... it's just there's a disconnect right now between the spending and who is receiving the benefits of having an AI economy. And I think that's going to show up in the midterms later this year. Sarah, before we let you go, I'm just curious. You're talking to us from San Francisco, big tech is your beat. We haven't mentioned the name Luigi Mangione, but I remember when the United Healthcare CEO was assassinated, there was a lot of fear. In talking to sources and stuff, they were afraid, and I think are still afraid. And I'm curious whether you're picking up that same level of anxiety. Like, are people aware of the potential for violence, for this kind of generalized anxiety to spill into something scary? I think that there has been a lot of thought put into executive security in the wake of the United Healthcare shooting and even before that. Mark Zuckerberg has millions of dollars each year spent on the security of him and his family. A lot of executives, including Sam Altman, have spoken of building bunkers at their properties, maybe even for more than themselves. Zuckerberg has a bunker on Kauai, I think. Yeah, plenty of them have bunkers. Again, not a great public relations strategy, talking about your bunker. Right, the locals might not love that. And I don't know if the bunkers are to protect from angry citizenry or if they are to protect from the AI apocalypse. I'm not quite sure. Sarah Frier, thanks for being here. Thanks for having me. Max, one of the things I think we've talked about maybe more than almost anything else is jobs—the job market, which has been just the source of a lot of distress and a lot of worry for years now. Yeah, and it's been especially bad for young job seekers, for people who are sort of leaving school and trying to find their first job or their first internship. It's tough out there. It is tough out there. Yeah, for young job seekers, the unemployment rate is almost two times what it is for overall job seekers, and the situation for overall job seekers isn't great either. We wanted to see what it feels like for people who are young and maybe just starting out in their careers or just about to enter the job market. We sent our producer, Jasmine JT Green, out onto the streets of New York to talk to some young people and ask them what their job situation is right now. Young person 1: I think current job market in the US is really limited, especially to international students like me, especially who doesn't have any US citizenship or green card or something like that. Young person 2: I'm a student and what I've heard is it's really difficult to get a job right now. Depends what world you're in, but I'm studying biology, hopefully to be a dentist. And for me, it's been really difficult to find internships, stuff like that, to boost my resume and eventually get the better job. Young person 3: For me, trying to get a job in New York City, like a part-time job, it's really hard and I have been unemployed for a few months now and it's hard to get another part-time job. What would you say is the most difficult part? Young person 2: Having connections and like knowing who to reach out to to find what you want, because I feel like cold emailing a lot of the time doesn't really work. So I feel like having the connections that can really get you a job is definitely the hardest part. Young person 1: English is my second language and kind of, I think it's really hard for me to engage people in the community and stuff like that. Young person 3: I think that there's just a high demand for jobs and there aren't enough, and also... yeah, that's it. High demand for jobs and not enough—that tells the story. Wow. You know what's interesting for me to hear in this, I feel like all the time there are things that give you a leg up in the job market, right? If you're looking for a job in your native language, if you have citizenship in the place where you're looking for a job, if you have connections—all these things are always an advantage. I think when the job market gets tight, it becomes almost really, really hard to get a job if you don't have those things. I mean, they would always be a leg up, but all of a sudden it's like almost impossible to get a job if you don't have every conceivable advantage. Then it becomes impossible. We're talking about this, of course, because in the brand new issue of Bloomberg Businessweek, we have a whole bunch of stories all about first jobs. This is an awesome collection of these combinations of sort of trend stories and first-person interviews. It's all about what things look like right now for young people. What does it mean for their careers, but also for all of us? We have Julia Rubin, she is a Businessweek editor and our colleague, here in the studio with us right now. Julia, welcome to Everybody's Business. Thank you so much. I feel like this is like a real, you know, longtime listener first-time caller situation, so happy to be here. You know her from the credits. Julia helps us each week put together this show and you also put together this awesome issue. Thank you. Yeah, I did. I want to start out really broadly. What does the job market look like for young people right now? What are they up against? It's bad. It's not good. The unemployment numbers for young people are higher than the unemployment numbers for the general population. And we are in a time of overlapping crises right now, and many things feel really bad. But this in particular is not good. There are fewer entry-level jobs and there are more people applying to them. And young people being out of work is really bad for the long-term health of the economy. And not just young people, but particularly young people with college degrees, right? That's one of the big and strange and upsetting trends, I guess. Especially if you're one of these people with a college degree, historically your job prospects are better, and right now that is... that's not true, right, Julia? Yes and no. There is an underemployment crisis, which means that there are more degree-havers than degree-requiring jobs. That said, degree-requiring jobs are still going to make you more money; it is still something that is incredibly desirable. But if you are able to get a job right now and you are young, it's very possible you're getting a job that you did not need your $200,000 education for. What are some examples of this? Did they talk to anybody who was underemployed in the issue? Because I feel like we see numbers for unemployed, and I believe the unemployment numbers for 18 to 24 year olds is high—I think it's like over 8%. But underemployment, the data's a little harder. Yeah, I think the most interesting place and the most kind of salient place to start is like engineers and computer science grads. Because from 2014 to 2024, entry-level openings for those kinds of roles grew about 6%. However, graduates of those fields or into those fields grew by 110%. So there is this huge mismatch between what people studied... they told us to learn to code. I know. And they lied. And you know what? I didn't learn to code. And look, we are not recent college grads, but there you go. But no, totally. And I think this is a field where AI in theory is starting to have some impact, but also there's a lot of AI washing. These companies hired too much during the pandemic and now they're blaming AI for layoffs when, like, that's not really the reason why yet at least that they are slashing jobs. Julia, at the beginning of this issue about first jobs and early career workers, there's this really staggering chart that compares essentially the unemployment rate for recent college graduates—people between the ages of 22 and 27—and everybody else. Until looking at about 2020, for a very long time, 14 years or so, recent college graduates had a much lower unemployment rate than the rest of us. Basically, if you were a recent grad, you were golden compared to the average worker. And it flipped. And that, I think, maybe gets to the core of a lot of the kind of anxiety you hear from young workers as well as conversations around AI. Is this a crisis and how bad is it? I mean, it's totally complicated and no one knows anything yet. There are definitely people saying, okay, AI is going to wipe out the bottom rung of the ladder—the entry-level jobs. The work that entry-level workers are doing, that's the easiest stuff to be automated. There are also people who are like, actually, this is going to create all of these jobs and it's actually just going to sort of change the nature of what these first jobs look like. They'll be way more valuable. There is this class of what we're calling AI natives, though I don't really know if I totally agree with that because people who are graduating the class of 2026 have had access to these large language models their entire college career. They've been cheating on their college classes for years. They are ready to be management consultants. But your children are actually the AI natives, right? Because it's like if you're in elementary school, you are growing up with the technology in the same way that we did with the internet and then social web stuff. That you did. That I did—that I'm very young, I don't know if you can tell. But yeah, so there is some level of AI fluency, but there is also dependency. You are also having these kind of recent grads or sometimes college students going into these internships and they know how to use these tools way better than anyone else in the workplace does, but they know nothing about the subject matter that they are actually using the models for. There's also a lot of soft skill stuff that they may not know. They don't know how to present the slide deck that is being made for them by the AI. Yeah, one of my favorite parts of this issue, there's a story about management consultants. And the funny thing is, it seems like a lot of the anxiety among the people who run management consultancies is not about the entry-level employees so much as the idea that AI slop might be somewhat indistinguishable from the normal output of a management consultancy. And that is manifesting in that they really want to make sure that their junior associates, whatever they're called, have soft skills—that they don't just sound like they're reciting points on a PowerPoint created by a chatbot. And that's why we're hearing too, it's oh, like, "we really want English majors." And that's across the spectrum now, which is also like a joke because when we graduated it was like again, gotta be an engineer, gotta be a business major, whatever. And so a lot of that I think is down to... I'm stealing this but learn to ode. Learn to ode! Man, I didn't even... I wasn't even an English major, I don't know. But yeah, it's very interesting. And this idea of, okay, if we're sort of automating away the grunt work or the sort of technical work, then perhaps, like, the work that is more about being a human is going to be much more important. Julia, a lot of times young people, especially in difficult job markets, get the short end of the stick. So a lot of times you will see unemployment much higher for younger people than it is for the rest of the workforce. Do you see this moment as different from previous downturns in that way? Is this moment harder on young workers than like the housing crisis and the financial crash were, or is this sort of the same? Young workers are always going to be the first impacted. It's like when there is a soft job market, that is who is going to have the most trouble. But what's interesting is unemployment was way higher during 2008, 2009, 2010 when I graduated. But the overall unemployment was higher than for recent grads. And that has flipped now. And I think that is something that is really interesting and really concerning. Because people are not able to enter the market and then they're not able to build their careers. There's also this horrible research that not being able to get a first job is going to impact your salary for the first 10 years, also higher rates of divorce, you're going to have fewer kids, there's like worse health outcomes, there's higher mortality. It's really bad when unemployment for young people is up above the general population. One thing I've wondered about—part of this conversation has made me sometimes a little bit uncomfortable because so much of it seems to be about class and about the expectation that going to college will vault you above people who don't go to college. The fact that that is breaking down feels like a violation of some sort of social contract. In the reporting for these stories, are you picking up anything like that? Any young people being like, not only should I not have learned to code, but maybe I shouldn't have gone to college? Yeah, I mean, when I think about the cost of college, I graduated 16 years ago. I think college is now twice... it cost twice what it was when I was there. I remember even like five years out, it was like 50% more. How could college possibly get any more expensive? So I would say there's definitely a sense of like, how could this possibly be worth it? That said, it doesn't change the kind of jobs that people want, that still are paying more than jobs that don't require degrees. But I do think there is a feeling of like, "I have been failed." And one thing that we did find is that this is an education problem. This is like colleges having majors and class sizes for those majors that are too big. It is schools not working with each other to make sure that each class is going to have X many graduates and across the country in this field where there's not going to be jobs. Like, the education system in this way is actually totally broken, just as the application process for jobs is also totally broken. So there's like these two very broken systems that young people are dealing with. It is still true that the unemployment rate for people with a college degree is lower than people who haven't graduated from college. Even for people with a high school diploma, it's lower than people who don't have a high school diploma. Do you feel like the broken promise is more like a feeling about the future prospects, or is this an underemployment thing that's maybe not being captured in those numbers? You know, one thing to note too is that it is not like the jobs that you're taking when you're underemployed are great either. When we look at trades, I think there's been all this conversation recently about tech leaders saying we're going to need all these electricians and plumbers, but actually there's been this total plateau. I think in the last year there's been a net loss of 150,000 jobs in the trades. And Trump said tariffs, we're going to bring back blue collar jobs, but actually no, it's made manufacturing more expensive, it has made repair work more expensive, and the data just really does not support that these data center builds for AI are going to result in sort of this boom of electricians and plumbers. So looking at the trends as they are now and assuming that AI does indeed become a bigger part of our economy and more jobs are displaced and things like that, where do you see this group of workers and would-be workers heading? What does this look like in the next 10 years? Hard question. I mean, I think people really don't know because I think again there still is this open question on like... is AI gonna decimate the entry-level job market slash the job market period? Is it something that is really going to augment it? We've already seen fewer computer science majors in the last year. I do think what kids are deciding to study is going to change. But we really just kind of don't know. I think one thing Brad Stone, our editor in chief, said is, "should there be sort of like an AI apprenticeship program?" Wait, what does that mean? I was wondering. Does that mean like you learn how to use AI, or are you an apprentice because... oh, or you're just like a new major, you just can't get paid? I think it's like training programs. I think it is like, here is how to use this technology for XYZ line of work. I mean, another example he had is, you know, we or the US subsidizes med school, right? And all around the world there are educational programs that are subsidized and also really pushed, whether they be trade programs or other ways to really kind of like boost this entry-level workforce. So my very unsatisfactory answer is like I don't think anyone knows what is going to happen. This is often cyclical, but AI if it really is this paradigm shifting thing, what will the cycles really look like? Julia, you need to stick around because we're going to talk about paradigm shifts even more when we get to the underrated story. See you then. Stacy, Julia, I mentioned a paradigm shift and whoof, I've got a big one. The underrated story—some people have probably seen this, I'm not even sure if it's underrated but it's just so stupid that I can't not talk about it. Remember Allbirds? Those dorky looking sneakers? Like the hippie shoes. Yeah, I bought them for my dad. I was just going to say they were the sneakers that like your least cool friend was really into. Wait, my dad's cool! Recycled water bottles, cool dad. Or like if you were like a white collar worker working in a business casual workplace, it's the Florsheim of sneakers. Anyway, they were really big for like a hot second. What... I'm trying to remember what the height of Allbirds was, I guess it was like COVID era? Yeah, or like right pre-pandemic. It was also very much like direct-to-consumer boom. Direct-to-consumer and then we all got cozy and then Allbirds spiked and for a brief amount of time it was very valuable. And then this came up last week—it was sold. It had been worth billions of dollars, it had been worth $4 billion, it was sold for $39 million. But what I found out today on bloomberg.com—a great website that people should check out if they haven't—is that actually they did not sell Allbirds, they just sold the shoe part of the business. And the other part of the business is pivoting. Can you guess what the pivot is to, Julia and Stacy? I can. 'Cause you read the story but it's crazy. AI. They're going AI. They're going to do GPUs or something. I don't think anyone involved with this really knows, but it is now called Newbird AI. They got 50 million bucks, and the stock soared. They were always running into the future, but now instead of running with shoes, they're running metaphorically. I think it totally fits. Makes... this idea though that you were like this part of the company. The whole part of the company was shoes. This is not... no no, it's just the name. The name of the company. It's not even the name actually because now it's called Newbird. They were never one of these companies like "we're actually a tech company also." Wasn't their whole thing like "we're sustainable?" And you know what's not sustainable? AI. I never had a pair of Allbirds but I knew many people who did. And I guess the thinking here is that brand—that really strong Allbirds brand—it could be the AI company that those Allbirds enthusiasts patronize. That's my best guess. I don't know. I did learn the term—I mean I don't even know if I can say it out loud—GPUaaS, like SaaS but for GPUs? GPUs as a service. GPUaaS. Pooass. G-pooass. That is correct. That is correct. So you know and like their whole thing is going to be hardware, which I don't know maybe makes sense because it's like they're making a physical product but in every other regard it does not make any sense at all. Yeah, stock's up 373% today, that's all I know. The market is never wrong. We will follow this, I can only imagine this could really... we may have to re-track the early part of the show when we talked about AI just because Allbirds could shake... Newbird AI could shake it up potentially. Stacy, what's your underrated story? Okay. Mine is it's a little bit of a conglomeration of all the news that we've been hearing, but it is this new term that I started to see this week that I found very illuminating. It is from Nate Hagens, a podcaster, and he said we are now living in the Mordor economy. Mordor for the uninitiated is in Lord of the Rings; it is the dystopian kingdom that tries to take over everything. But what he specifically meant, and why I think this is actually really interesting, is that it's an economy that's all about energy. And energy prices get more expensive, so the economy has to make more energy in order to feed the energy needs, and it becomes like a snake swallowing its own tail. I feel like this moment is very much the Mordor economy between electricity prices going nuts because of data centers and also because Canada cut us off, oil prices going nuts, natural gas prices going nuts. Fertilizers are made of a lot of natural gas so that means food prices are going to go up, and our whole economy is becoming about making energy that is then consumed and becomes more expensive—the Mordor economy. I mean, it just feels like something that didn't have to happen. That's my take. Okay. My critique here is more with the term. Is that the only thing going on with Mordor that they're using energy? I feel like this is like a fundamentally confusing analogy. What do you think was going on with Mordor? It's like a totalitarian state they're trying to take over the world and get the One Ring of power. I don't know, I'm not a Lord of the Rings guy but it... so maybe a better analogy than you even thought. Who's Sauron and who are the trees and like where are the hobbits? I just, having written a lot about guys who are really into Lord of the Rings, I'm afraid I may have like... you may be very sensitive to this. This metaphor doesn't line up at all. That is possibly true. But I do think there is something really interesting about an economy where energy starts to get so expensive. There are some really chilling statistics, including that for low-income people, they're now spending about 20% of their income on energy. That's like gas and electricity and everything—20% of their income. And I agree with you, Max, that maybe the finer points of Mordor are lost and that's difficult. The Eye of Sauron is with you too. The Eye of Sauron's our economy was about so much more than energy. Listeners, if you have a Lord of the Rings related take—yes, oh my God—email us at everybodys@bloomberg.net. Just everybodys with an s, everybodys@bloomberg.net. Is it the Mordor economy and if so, who are the orcs? Let us know. This show is produced by Jasmine JT Green and Stacy Wong. Magnus Henrikson is our supervising producer, Sam Rowgic handles engineering, and Dave Purcell fact checks. Special thanks to Jeff Muskas, Julia Rubin, Maria Lyne, Rachel Lewis-Krisky, and Angel Reccheo. If you have a minute, please rate and review the show; it means a lot to us. And if you have a story that should be our business, send us an email at Everybodys@bloomberg.net. That's Everybodys with an s at the end at bloomberg.net. Thank you for listening, and we will see you next week.

LIVE: Howard Lutnick and Lilly CEO David Ricks expected to speak at Semafor summit

Reuters(4.2M) ·103:31 ·1.7K views · 2026-04-17
The U.S.
TL;DR The U.S. is pivoting toward an aggressive "Build in America" industrial policy, targeting 6% GDP growth through massive domestic infrastructure investment and strategic equity stakes in tech companies, while simultaneously racing to lead the "Genesis Mission" of AI-driven...

TL;DR

The U.S. is pivoting toward an aggressive "Build in America" industrial policy, targeting 6% GDP growth through massive domestic infrastructure investment and strategic equity stakes in tech companies, while simultaneously racing to lead the "Genesis Mission" of AI-driven scientific discovery and the commercialization of space and fusion energy.

The New American Industrial Policy

  • GDP Growth Strategy: U.S. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick projects reaching 6% GDP growth by 2027-2028, driven by an $18 trillion "build" initiative.
      • Shovel-Ready Goals: Lutnick argues that getting "shovels in the ground" for chip fabs (like Micron and TSMC) and drug onshoring ($400 billion) will naturally drive growth regardless of traditional economic skepticism.
  • The "Benefit of the Bargain" Model: The government is shifting from pure grants to taking financial stakes in companies to protect taxpayers.
      • Intel Example: The U.S. modified Intel's payout model from a 20% grant to a 4% grant but increased the build commitment to over $500 billion. The government now holds roughly 430 million shares of Intel, turning a potential $10 billion cost into a $30 billion asset.
  • Foreign Investment & Tariffs: The U.S. secured $550 billion from Japan and $200 billion from South Korea for domestic construction projects.
      • Trade Levers: Lutnick revealed that the 25% tariff on Japanese cars was reduced to 15% specifically in exchange for these massive infrastructure investments.
      • USMCA Reimagining: Lutnick expressed skepticism toward the current USMCA, suggesting it may need to be "re-addressed" to prevent it from being used to move American auto plants to Mexico.

AI and the "Genesis Mission" for Science

  • The Genesis Mission: Under Secretary Dario Gil described this flagship initiative to double the productivity of the U.S. R&D ecosystem within a decade by unifying semiconductors, AI supercomputing, and quantum computing.
      • Discovery Acceleration: AI is being used to move science beyond language into mathematics and physics. Gil cited "AlphaFold" as a benchmark, which solved 200 million protein structures in two years—a feat that previously took 50 years to reach just 200,000.
      • Industrial Partnerships: The government has formed a "Genesis Consortium" with over 30 partners, including Nvidia and AMD, to build the world’s largest AI supercomputer cluster for science within the national labs.
  • Information Disintermediation: Cloudflare CEO Matthew Prince warned that AI is a platform change that threatens the business model of the internet.
      • The "Google Problem": Prince criticized Google for bundling AI training with search rankings, essentially forcing publishers to provide content for free or lose visibility.
      • Local Content Value: High-quality, local, and unique data (e.g., local newspapers like the *Park Record*) is becoming more valuable for AI training than generic national news, potentially creating a "golden era" for niche content creators.

The Commercial Space and Fusion Race

  • Commercializing Orbit: Industry leaders are moving toward commercial space stations (e.g., Starlab) to replace the International Space Station (ISS) by the 2030s.
      • Cost Reduction: While the ISS cost $450 billion, commercial stations are projected to cost in the "single-digit billions" while offering larger volumes for manufacturing and microgravity research.
      • Strategic Assets: Space is being viewed as a tool for global economic efficiency (worth $1T to $10T in value creation) and a source of rare materials like Helium-3 from the moon for fusion energy.
  • The Fusion Timeline: Bob Mumgaard (Commonwealth Fusion Systems) expects a demonstration plant to be operational by 2027, with commercial power on the grid in the early 2030s.
      • Competition with China: Mumgaard warned that while the U.S. leads in science, China is outspending the U.S. on fusion industrial policy by an order of magnitude, threatening U.S. leadership in the "fusion race."

Global Shifts: India and the Global South

  • India’s Resilience: Dr. V. Anantha Nageswaran (Chief Economic Advisor of India) stated that India is successfully navigating energy shocks by diversifying into nuclear, renewables, and domestic coal.
      • AI and Labor: Nageswaran downplayed fears of AI-driven job losses, arguing that the replacement of humans in IT and services will be slower than predicted due to legal and compliance requirements.
  • Leapfrogging the Grid: In the Global South, the goal is to skip traditional centralized power grids in favor of AI-managed microgrids.
      • Distributed Energy: Manoj Sinha (Husk Power) argued that AI-powered decentralized resources could solve energy access for 300 million people, treating "green electrons" as the foundation for a local industrial revolution in Africa and India.

***

Notable Quotes

"When you come to my office and the wall is full of shovels, I promise you we’re at six percent GDP growth... That’s 2027, 2028." — Howard Lutnick, U.S. Secretary of Commerce
"What if I told you the balance sheet of the United States of America was worth five hundred trillion dollars?... You put a businessman in the White House, you put a businessman as the Secretary of Commerce, you’re going to make money using our economy." — Howard Lutnick, U.S. Secretary of Commerce
"Everything that’s wrong with the world today is Google’s fault... they begat Facebook which begat TikTok which begat basically us chasing this deity which is traffic. Traffic is a terrible proxy for quality." — Matthew Prince, CEO of Cloudflare
"The U.S. has been a historic leader in fusion science... however, we don’t really have an industrial policy for fusion. China has a program you can photograph from outer space... we are at risk of losing this race." — Bob Mumgaard, CEO of Commonwealth Fusion Systems
I am, because I know the commitments of build in America. That means the factories you have, Micron building a hundred billion dollar fab, right? That’s making the chips, literally making the chips. TSMC currently building a hundred and sixty-five billion dollar—these are factories making the chips. These are not the data centers. Data centers are buying the chips. We’re talking about making the chips, making engines, making cars, making every this, eighteen trillion, making drugs, four hundred billion dollars of onshoring drugs. Finally having an industrial policy that allows us to build in America will drive GDP and it naturally drives it. So I’m not projecting. What I am doing is I’m taking a calculator out that comes free with my phone and putting in the concept. Like think about it this way: if the president says eighteen trillion dollars of build, we have a thirty trillion dollar economy, right? Three trillion dollars of build is ten percent. Okay, three trillion is ten percent. It’s not that hard to hit six percent growth if these shovels get in the ground, right? When they get in the ground is key. But when those shovels hit the ground, you hit six percent GDP growth whether you like it or not, whether you want to agree or disagree—shovels. So if you come to my office, I have two big shovels in my office right now; one was a hundred billion dollar build and one was a thirty-two billion dollar build. When you come to my office and the wall is full of shovels, I promise you we’re at six percent GDP growth. So that’s 2027, 2028. We’ll see. I’ll keep an eye on the wall. If you and I had been talking ten years ago, I think it would have sounded really exotic that you in a Republican administration are advocating for the US government to take stakes in US companies. That is now a model. And I’m curious, do you think that that is, in some of the companies you were just talking about, the government has a financial interest in? Do you think that that is like a one-time emergency measure, or is that kind of the new way that the American government will and should operate in the future? So I have a theory called the benefit of the bargain. Okay? Why would we give Intel a grant of ten billion dollars to assist them to build in America when the company is worth a hundred billion dollars? It’s not supposed to be shareholder Christmas. I mean, that is the American tradition. The answer would have been because they’re an American company. But it’s crazy town. Why wouldn’t you get the benefit of the bargain for the American taxpayer? If we’re going to give, G-I-V-E, money to someone, why don’t we get something for it, right? And so in the scale, so before I walked in the door, the CHIPS Act, which was fifty-two billion dollars, had basically a twenty percent payout model. That means if you were willing to build, you know, a ten billion dollar factory in America, the CHIPS Act gave you two billion dollars, twenty percent. So I walked in the door and I thought that was crazy, right? Who do we give twenty percent? You’re like basically giving the full down payment to someone else to build for them and the American taxpayer is just paying, paying, paying. So we modified that to four percent. Right? So we said I’ll still give you the money, but you have to build twenty-five billion now, right? Thirty-five billion, forty-five billion. So we turned what was a hundred or two hundred billion dollar build into a five hundred and fifty billion dollar build. And when Intel couldn’t build more, right, because they were a dumpster fire, right? When I walked in the door they were a dumpster fire, right? This three hundred, two hundred, a hundred billion dollar. Is the fire out? Are they not a dumpster fire now? The fire’s out. We gave them, right, we invested in them, right, in what was it, August. Not to mention they aren’t even a dumpster fire, they’re doing good. It didn't mean just that they... August, it was twenty dollars a share, right? Yesterday was sixty-five dollars a share. It went from a hundred billion dollar company to a three hundred billion dollar company. We went from giving Intel ten billion dollars—the American taxpayer giving Intel ten billion dollars and getting nothing for it—to owning four hundred and fifty million shares of their company, or four hundred and thirty million shares of their company worth thirty billion dollars for the American taxpayer. It’s a swing of forty billion dollars for the benefit of who? Of us. For our Social Security system, for our government. And that’s a way of thinking that is basically you put a businessman in the White House, you put a businessman as the Secretary of Commerce, and you’re going to make money using our economy. You know the way I say it is we have six and a half trillion of spending when I walked in the door and four and a half trillion of revenue. We lost two trillion dollars a year, our economy. We have a thirty trillion dollar economy and we have thirty-nine trillion of debt; we’re going to hell. Except, what if I told you the balance sheet of the United States of America was worth five hundred trillion dollars? Now, I say it’s worth five hundred trillion. Of course, my boss says a quadrillion because whatever I say he by definition is going to say double, but but because he’s just fundamentally more optimistic. But the fact is, does that sound like a bad... it sounds sensational if you just don’t close... It sounds like a great deal. And I think there’s a lot of, I mean I totally understand the argument, you’re getting a better deal for the taxpayer. I think one of the big criticisms of this system—and you know you’re the chairman of the investment committee overseeing a huge chunk of promised Japanese investment—there’s a Japanese CEO talking to you in the green room just now. There’s obviously a lot of people who see the promise. I would say maybe the central worry is you start doing these one-off deals, these one-off relationships and it just lends itself, whether now or in the future, to a kind of self-dealing crony capitalism. How do you avoid that? Well, you have in this case, so we had five hundred and fifty billion dollars of committed capital by the Japanese and two hundred billion committed capital by the Koreans as part of the trade. And you’re really personally making the decisions, right? You’re the chairman of the investment board. I am the chairman of the investment committee but when we do energy then of course Chris Wright is sitting next to me. When I use—when we use land from Interior—then then Doug Burgum sits next to me and everything gets approved by the president. So the model is we have seven hundred and fifty billion to build what we want to build in America. And what do we want to build? We want to build a hundred gigawatts of power. We need a hundred gigawatts of power and so the Japanese are funding a hundred gigawatts of power. So that’s coming, right? So we’ve announced, we’ve already announced a hundred and five billion dollars of projects, right? Nine point two gigawatts of power in Portsmouth and this is going to rejuvenate, right, and re—basically, if you think about it for just one minute, the industrial policy of the United States of America destroyed basically like Upstate New York had Carrier and all these sort of powers. And where we announced in Portsmouth, that was where Eisenhower built all the nuclear of America. And those communities died with our industrial policy which basically globalized everything—build it somewhere else and we’ll buy it, right? When you turn that back around, these communities have stranded power. They have huge power infrastructure for all those hulking factories. And what’s happening is those those factories are going to get rebuilt, right, with new fabs, with new types of technology. And you’re going to see these communities regrow. And it’s going to be amazing and that’s where that eighteen trillion is going. It’s going to Onondaga County, it’s going to Portsmouth, Ohio. They’re going there because they have stranded power infrastructure and so those old factories are actually a magnet for the new fabs that are coming and these are going to be, this is a hundred billion dollar fab. It’s miles long, twenty, forty thousand construction jobs, twenty-five thousand full-time. I’m sold. People love this, like honestly, this is great policy. I mean as you see, like I don’t think anyone in either party is going to argue with that and they love the fab. What do you say to the notion that whether it’s your administration, whether it’s the Gavin Newsom administration, whatever, Americans get nervous about the idea that a single person might be saying it? That you personally or the president might be saying, you know what, I’ll give this deal to my friend and not to my enemy, that rather than the invisible hand of capitalism, it is some people we elected or appointed doing one-off deals. The worst thing I learned when I went into government is the sucking sound of how many people try to suck off of the US government. I mean it was it’s so demoralizing to watch that stuff. Look, we are here for outcomes and winners. If you give money to a loser, you’re going to have a loser outcome. All right? If you want, I want to be proud of what we accomplish. We’re going to give money to winners who are going to win so that I can be proud and this administration can be proud of the outcomes. We’re going to build a hundred gigawatts of power. We’re going to build it. If we don’t build it, then we won’t have it. Different question. China talks kick off next month. There are some, there are voices in China who want to see Chinese investment in the US and specifically JV BYD factories in the United States. Is that on the table? No. You’ve been that... thank you for that answer. It’s amazing. I have like three more questions I can get in now. Amazing. You’ve been, you’ve been talking to a lot of your counterparts, other countries. I’m curious which countries and which negotiators, which teams do you feel like understand the US? Who has been best to work with, who’s been worst to work with? Well, the Japanese were extraordinary to work with because we have a great cultural divide. And once you understand the cultural divide and were willing to put it on the table. You can’t open the Japanese car market. You you just can’t. There’s ninety-four percent of cars driven in Japan are Japanese. It’s cultural, right? The only American cars sold in Japan are Chevys driven by the Yakuza. So if your kid comes home with a Chevy, it’s not really going to work for you, right? So once you realize that’s not going to open, then we had to come at it with a completely different model, right? The tariff was going to be twenty-five percent or we’ve got to come up with a different model. And so they came up with a model that said we will invest with you in America. And this is buying down the tariff in some sense? Not in some sense. They literally... we reduced the tariff from twenty-five percent to fifteen percent, right? In exchange for that they gave us five hundred and fifty billion dollars to do construction projects that are absolutely going to get them their money back. Who else has been, who else gets it from your perspective? The British, the South Koreans, who’s who’s? Well, it was it was hard for the Europeans, but they got there. You know the big the big deals, the Japanese, the Koreans, the Europeans, and and the UK, they got there. Right? They got there and they understood. Didn’t mean it wasn’t a lot of talking but obviously even in this brief time we have together you realize I’m not really shy about talking. Speaking of our closer neighbors, is President Trump committed to extending the USMCA ahead of the July deadline? I think he thinks it’s a bad deal. Right? He thinks making Mexico and Canada be treated economically like Georgia and Alabama without them actually being committed. You think it might lapse? I think it needs to be re re-considered and re-imagined correctly. There are parts of Mexico that are fundamental to us, there are parts of of Canada, you know, their energy and other things that that are important to us. But the but the concept of taking an auto plant out of Ohio and Michigan and putting it in Mexico to break the union and to break our people is nuts. It was a bad industrial policy, it harmed America. President Trump is going to fix it, he’s bringing it back. You’re going to see it get brought back. And I think it needs to be re-imagined and needs to be re-addressed. There’s plenty of good in it but there’s a huge amount of bad in it and it needs to be re-considered for the benefit of America. The former top Canadian trade negotiator said the other day that time is on... Who? Chrystia Freeland who’s like the worst... No, no, a guy, I’m blanking on his name, but that... Howard Lutnick, Secretary of Commerce, US Department of Commerce. I like to hear you talk about your passion for Onondaga County, you’re a New Yorker. Ever consider running for governor of New York? I’ve never considered it but you know, whatever I’m done with two years and nine months, then I’ll worry about the rest of my life. But maybe you’re not done with public life having set a foot into it? I think I’ve made enough money in my life. This is the most fun I’ve ever had. All I care about is America. And we can do amazing things in America, right? The we are going, right, the platinum card is going to make America a trillion dollars. A trillion dollars, right? We will make the Japan and Korea deals will make America a trillion dollars. You’ve never had a commerce secretary make trillions. But if you want a platinum card talk to the secretariat afterward. You got it. Thank you. This was fun. Thank you. Reimagining Global Economic Levers. Semafor. Citadel | Securities. Please welcome Under Secretary Dario Gil of the US Department of Energy and Tim McDonnell of Semafor. Thank you. Good morning everyone. Thank you so much. We’re going to speak with Under Secretary Gil now about some of the basic science, the the hardware that goes into the innovation that Secretary Lutnick was just speaking about. There’s this thing called the Genesis mission. Tell us a little bit about that. What’s our, unpack a little bit our our plan for beating China on quantum computing and these other critical technologies. Well good morning everybody. The Genesis mission is the flagship science and technology initiative of the administration. It was launched as an executive order the Monday of Thanksgiving of last year and it has a very straightforward thesis which is we’re in the midst of a computing revolution that is going to transform the practice of science and engineering in America. We seek to double the productivity and impact of America’s trillion dollar a year R&D ecosystem within a decade. This computing revolution is powered by three legs. It is built on the decades of progress of semiconductors and high-performance computing, it is built on AI and AI supercomputing, and it is built on quantum computing. And what we seek to do is to bring those three modalities together and on top of it, we’re building, in partnership with the private sector, a platform to accelerate scientific and engineering workflows. And what that means is that those three modalities will be unified by an AI agentic framework that is designed to go through the whole flow of hypothesis generation, experimentation, analysis, the scientific method. And on top of that, we’re also going to get AI beyond its ability to do language and coding and into its ability to do mathematics and physics and chemistry and materials and biology and engineering of every type. And we’re also going to connect that intelligence platform with our exquisite scientific instruments and laboratories in a closed-loop system. Think about it as an internet of science of form that you can execute this complex workflows. So that’s part one of the Genesis mission, the platform to accelerate discovery. Part two of the Genesis mission is the construction and execution of a national science and technology portfolio of grand challenges. And these grand challenges, we’re beginning with the scope of the Department of Energy, so they involve challenges in energy, like in nuclear and grid and fusion. They involve challenges in discovery science in the physical sciences. Imagine the next generation of materials and chemistry and biology innovation. And then they also involve a portfolio in national security. And we’re going to expand this in partnership with other agencies like NIH and NASA and the National Science Foundation etc. So when you look at it all combined, think about a portfolio to solve some of the grandest and most impactful science and technology challenges in our country with a change in methodology because we can accelerate the time to solution on how we actually drive breakthrough. There’s there’s a sort of exponential progress that’s happening in in a lot of these technologies. If if, you know, if we have this conversation again a year from now on the stage, how how would you define success? What what are you looking for in the the sort of short term to know that this is actually working? Yeah, so let me give you an example and what I what we aim to do, which is a hundred of those examples, and I’ll give, which I’m sure many of you are aware, the best known example perhaps of AI for science which is what happened with protein folding and you know and and what happened with AlphaFold. But let me just connect the dots and see how you’re going to scale that we’re going to scale that up. So in 1971, at Brookhaven National Laboratory, one of our seventeen national laboratories that we manage in the Department of Energy, they started a project to catalog proteins and proteins are the building blocks of life, right, are fundamental to the world of biology and medicine and drug discovery and so on. And at the time, they used one of the X-ray sources that we have there, we have these amazing user facilities and they have a synchrotron and they were able to prepare a sample with the proteins and they had seven samples. And you illuminate the sample with the X-rays and it forms a diffraction pattern and you reconstruct the three-dimensional structure of the protein. So fast forward fifty years of effort of the community, fifty years of efforts of using synchrtrons and NMR and simulation, the community was able to build two hundred thousand proteins for which we know their structure. And then in two years with the advent of modern AI techniques applied to those data sets on a project called AlphaFold that Google DeepMind did leveraging those investments, we went from two hundred thousand proteins to two hundred million, all known proteins, in two years. Yeah. That’s an example of a discontinuity. So success looks like a hundred of those stories across every field of science and engineering and that is what we’re executing. Let’s talk a bit more about the funding stream here. The the latest White House budget proposal cuts the budget for the Office of Science by more than a billion dollars, reduced the budget for National Lab of the Rockies by half, NSF in half, ARPA-E by forty-three percent. Isn’t this sort of the opposite of what we we should be doing here? I mean China I think is taking the the exact opposite tack and and putting much more money into these efforts. Yeah, so again the central thesis of what Genesis is is also to transform how we practice science and engineering. In the end, as everybody knows, the budgets will be determined you know, this is part of the process, it will be determined by Congress and what allocates. What I can tell you is, for example, if you look at in the Office of Science on a year on year basis, our budget was up, right, by two percent. Compare that in the President’s budget also, you will see in the context of Genesis that it calls for a, you know, one point two billion dollar investment on building the next generation of supercomputers both at Oak Ridge and Argonne National Laboratories. And one of the unique things that we’re doing in the Genesis mission is this: this is a whole-of-nation endeavor. And what it means is that we are innovating in how we are going to enable the co-investment of federal dollars, state dollars, private sector dollars, and philanthropy. And and this has real meaning because I’ll tell you as an example, one of the issues we have to solve in the USG is we simply don’t have enough AI supercomputing capacity. We have world-class high-performance computing, but we don’t have enough AI supercomputing capacity. So we are having partners invest actually billions of dollars to build supercomputing capacity at the national laboratories to be able to enable the success of the Genesis mission. But but can can we do both though? I mean why why not have more private sector participation and invest more federal dollars into this effort at the same time? Yeah yeah, the we absolutely are going to see growing investments into this Genesis mission effort. There is no doubt about it, right, because because again it is rooted on a fundamental from from the public sector. Yes, there will be, there will be, there is no doubt that the Genesis mission portfolio and investments is going to grow on a year on year basis without a doubt and quite significantly in partnership with other agencies. Does that come at the cost of other basic science that the US is doing? Is it I mean as I, you know, as I just said, there’s the the most recent budget cuts a lot of the funding for some of these programs. Yeah again, let’s see what happens at the end of the process, right? This is part of the process on on like budget submissions. So let’s see what happens. I think what is important for the community of, you know, in science and engineering is to see what happens at the end of the process when budgets are actually appropriated and allocated to the to the to the agencies. And but but no, it does not come at the cost of it. Because if you look at fundamental biology, I mean the example I was giving with protein folding, the change of methodology only made biology better. It wasn’t at the cost of it. That was one of the grand challenges that people were trying to solve for decades. And this is going to be true, I mean there are so many examples. Let me give you another one on fusion, right? So we’re very passionate about fusion, we want to see commercial fusion power plants realized in the early 2030s. We’re going to see demonstrations on a number of private sector actors where, you know, Q greater than one, more energy out than you put in, happens in the next, you know, year or two. And it was decades of investments in the Office of Science that enabled the match between the unique conditions of plasma fusion, right, where you have to get things at hundreds of millions of degrees, coupled with simulation codes that allows us to predict what happened. Okay, what was the issue? The issue is that those simulations that allows us to design what a fusion reactor looks like takes weeks, months of computing time to get to an answer. Now come AI and there’s a subset of simulation on something called surrogate models where you basically take the output of the simulation codes and you use that as training examples for a neural network based model to issue predictions. Well, the acceleration of these things is by a factor of ten thousand X. So all of a sudden, the thing that took you three months to do, now you can do in hours. And if you can do that, now you can iterate the design of your tokamak or your stellarator in different ways. So this is and I could give you a hundred examples of area after area, on the grid, on nuclear, on high-energy physics, in cosmology, in national security. This is a fundamental transformation of science and engineering. When you look at the the portfolio, you just described all of these different concentration areas that you’re focused on. Are there areas of basic research where you think we should be pulling back or that are sort of well covered enough already that we should be doing less of? Well I mean fields... climate science for example is not is not among the things that that you listed. Fields evolve. I I don’t tell like people what not to study, right? Like you know, my my role on this is to enable and support the community in a discipline-based approach like what we support. The Office of Science is the largest federal sponsor of of physical sciences research in the nation. We support physics and chemistry and biology and I don’t tell scientists like don't think about it like I want the best science ecosystem in America, we want to support them and around that what we are insisting within the context of Genesis is to equip them with the modern instruments of our time. And the analogy I give is just like telescopes allowed us to see what’s far away and microscopes the small, these new instruments, AI supercomputers and quantum systems, allow us to see the very complex. So they are the new instruments of our time. We have to give it to them. They don’t have our community in universities and in the national labs, they don’t have the state-of-the-art AI supercomputing capabilities. Genesis mission is going to solve that problem for them and with that, in this administration and in the future, we will equip the nation to accelerate discovery no matter what priorities anybody sets. So for me what I’m passionate about is to leave behind a platform of discovery that overlays on top of our national laboratories, on top of our university ecosystem, and allow them to achieve what no one else could do before. You were we were speaking backstage about partnership with NVIDIA. What what other conversations are you having with with tech companies? Are there future deals that we can watch for? Can you maybe break some news on stage or what what should we be watching for in these partnerships? Look, I’ll tell you a a couple of things. So so one, we’re innovating in how to partner, so we created the Genesis consortium, we have over thirty industrial partners that are investing collectively billions of dollars into the Genesis mission. So we will be standing up as an example two AI supercomputers that from the time Secretary Wright, you know, was shaking hands and we were together with Jensen Huang and with Lisa Su from NVIDIA and AMD respectively, from that time to the supercomputers built in the labs was nine months. Unprecedented. There’s never been history that we’ve been able to execute projects like that. These are massive investments. Next year with NVIDIA we’re working towards building a hundred thousand GPU cluster, be the largest cluster for science in the world that we’re creating around that. But I’ll give you one more example of breaking a little bit like what is to anticipate. We were talking about the platform, but the part I’m most excited about is the teams that are responding to the twenty-six national science and technology challenges that we’ve issued. The news kind of I will I will sort of break is an anticipation is like we are fully anticipating that when these proposals are due which is just in a couple of weeks, it’s going to break every record in terms of the number of proposals that we have ever gotten in the history of the Department of Energy. And we’ll share that like when that happens around that but the amount of enthusiasm in our university ecosystem, in our national laboratories about this transformation is without equal on any program we have ever created. So I’m very excited about that aspect of it and if you talk to industry partners and and so on on this topic, you will see that I think they will say flat out this is the most exciting science and technology initiative that of the administration that they want to be part of. So this is not something about forcing people, everybody is rallying to do it. My my stress is urgency and can we give it as per your question enough fuel to be successful in a commensurate way with the talent and capability we have as a country. That’ll be so interesting to watch, Under Secretary. Thank you so much for your time. Thank you. Reimagining Global Economic Levers. Semafor. Citadel | Securities. Please welcome Matthew Prince of Cloudflare and Reed Albergotti of Semafor. Thanks for being here. Hey, thanks for having me. Thank you all for coming. I I just want to let you know that I forgive you for missing the breakfast that you skipped this morning because you were you got in at 3 AM after giving a TED talk on the future of journalism and how AI is you know going to destroy journalism or what have you. It’s this has become your your pet project. So can you just kind of lay out what the problem is right now that you’re trying to solve? Sure. So so again Secretary I apologize for missing the breakfast. And this is a lot less stressful than a TED talk. Like I’d never done a TED talk before but we’ve all sort of seen the world’s perfect TED talk and so you feel like you have to give that and I did not but it was you know check that box. So thanks for thanks for still having me. So you know I think that what people don’t appreciate about AI is that it’s a platform change. The same way that we went from browsers on desktops and laptops to social media to mobile, those were all platform changes but AI is a platform change as well and the way that you access information, the way that you do commerce increasingly is going to be through some sort of agent that you have and we’re all doing this already. Even if you use something like Google, type something into Google, the Google of old doesn’t exist anymore. Increasingly people are just getting the AI overview at the top of the page for whatever they’re looking for and for consumers that is a much better experience. But the problem is for anyone who’s in media and and by the way eventually for everyone who’s in in in commerce as well that’s disintermediating the relationship between your customer, in the case of media your readers, and and you. And that means that if your business in the past was selling ads or subscriptions or things, now increasingly that’s that’s you’re not getting that traffic, you’re not seeing those eyeballs. And so that’s going to force a pretty substantial change to the business model of the internet which has been very stable for the last thirty years and I don’t totally know what that what that change is going to look like but I think that we have to be very intentional and talk about what we want that to have come out the other side. And and so I’ve been I’ve been you know meeting with journalists and and AI companies and Cloudflare because of sort of the nature of where we sit kind of sits between those two things and I’m trying to say okay what do we all want, what do we what is the future that we want. The future that I want is not one where there are you know three AI companies but one where there are three hundred thousand AI companies. Not one where there’s you know very few content creators and journalists and academics and researchers but where everyone has the ability to be reporting on what’s going on and and that the best ideas are surfaced and rewarded. And then in the business perspective like I worry that AI is this massive consolidating force and so we have to figure out how do we give especially small businesses the tools to be able to not just exist but thrive in this new world which is which is coming, which is coming. Yeah and and one of your ideas is like you already you have actually done a lot on this already. Like this isn’t just talk. I mean you you’ve created a mechanism so that the AI crawlers that go out and crawl the websites, get the answers that we now can just you know have at our fingertips through ChatGPT or Gemini or whatever, they can’t do that unless the publishing companies give them permission. Of course they’re finding ways around etc but that’s the idea, right? Yeah I mean so so I I am confident we can stop the AI crawlers from getting content. The the problem that we have right now is we’re kind of at a we’re kind of at a loggerhead where there’s a challenge which is going on and there’s one company that has created kind of a mess here. And that’s Google. Google was the superhero of the last twenty-seven years of the internet. They built the system yes to sort of sort through the chaos that was the internet but they also importantly built the business model that allowed you and in you know almost all the ad tech and subscription tech and everything that’s there you know comes back to Google and Google powers a ton of it and so again they should have an enormous amount of credit for what they’ve done. But in the process of doing that they cut a bunch of deals to get access to a huge portion of the web that that other people don’t see. So if you look at you know how much more of the web does Google see than OpenAI? The answer is for every page that OpenAI sees, Google sees three. For every page that Microsoft sees, Google sees four. For every page that Anthropic sees, Google sees five. For every page that xAI sees, Google sees twenty-two. Right? And we we see that data, we can actually see that that’s there and Google is the one that’s sort of said we aren’t willing to actually compensate publishers for what they’re doing and in fact they’ve gone even further. They’ve said if you try to block us from using your content for AI, we’re going to drop you out of search. Right? They’ve bundled those two things together. And I think that’s incredibly problematic if we take what kind of was the the monopoly player of yesterday and you let them use that leverage to create a monopoly for tomorrow. That that’s enormously challenging. And so what’s been hard for us is we can hard block all the AI companies except Google because then you lose search. But if we do that we’re just handing the game to Google. And so what we’re trying to do is figure out how can we make it so either Google has to play by the same rules as everyone else or how can we bring everybody else to the same level as Google. And that’s that I think is a is a big a big struggle and one of the things that you know we talk with if if you want again a world where there are three hundred thousand AI companies what you’ve got to do is make sure that there isn’t one that essentially is getting one of the key resources that they need for free where everyone else has to pay. I mean you’re in Washington. There’s a bunch of policy people here. What do you think the government should do? Is it is it an antitrust issue? Well again I think that we we’ve got to figure out some way that either everybody gets the same access that Google has or that Google has to play by the same rules as everyone else. And and again I don’t know what the right answer is but if you allowed that it would be much easier to start to restrict you know in very firm ways what all the AI companies have access to. Right now we kind of sort of give people... it’s sort of like putting a no trespassing sign in somebody’s yard. You know, yeah, stop some people but you’re right, there are people who are going around that. We could we could put armed guards at the at the edge of every single content creator that’s out there but again what I’m worried about is how do we do that in a way which doesn’t you know again just completely hand the game to Google. Yeah you know one of the things as you’ve pointed out Google the Google system didn’t exactly incentivize the best journalism all the time right because you’re sort of optimizing for traffic for click, you know it incentivized clickbait. Terribly I mean everything that’s wrong with the world today is Google’s fault. That’s that’s too strong but only slightly too strong because Google begat Facebook which begat TikTok which begat basically us chasing this deity which is traffic. Traffic is a terrible proxy for quality. It actually I mean if you think about like what are the things that sort of attract traffic? It’s usually the things that either make you very angry or or you know stimulate some sort of cortisol response or something else. And so isn’t it a good thing though that like okay so now we have to find new business models and do creative things like this? Like look at all you know I mean this is what we’re doing. Is this a new business model? It sort of looks like West but ooh. All right this is going to be a tough interview from here on. There you go. So so I think that I think that I am actually optimistic that for the first time in human history we can actually measure mathematically what we as humans know.
