And also what we don’t know and then also when a new piece of information comes out we can actually measure how much does that contribute to knowledge. And so if I were running an AI company today I’d launch the equivalent of the Nobel Prize or the or the Academy Awards for content. And every year look at, you know across a number of different fields, who added the most information that actually furthered humanity’s understanding of a particular topic and give awards out, right, celebrate that, reward that, compensate those content creators. And the good news is if you talk to Sam Altman, if you talk to Dario, if you talk to you know Sundar, and you say what are you really looking for, they want that new information which is there. And so I think I believe two things simultaneously about media. I believe that a whole bunch of existing media companies are about to just get crushed and I believe that we are on the at the on the verge of the age of the next golden era of content creation. And the reason why is I think that things that are local, unique, differentiated are going to be much more valuable. I’ll give you an example. My wife and I we live most of the year in Park City, Utah. There’s Park City has a small newspaper called the *Park Record*. It was dying like most small newspapers. We believe that local media is incredibly important and you know we’ve been fortunate so we bought the local newspaper and we did not buy it to be a great business. In fact we the KPIs, the the ways we measured how successful we were, we took no financial KPIs, the only KPI we looked at was voter turnout which in when the the election before we bought the paper was at twenty-three percent and this last election, a non-presidential election, we got it to sixty-two percent. So like and I think that’s I like if you’re going to measure how much good you’ve done in a community, I think that’s actually a pretty good way of measuring it, so we were happy about that. But what’s crazy is it’s actually kind of also turned into a pretty good business and we’ll make more money this year I think off licensing our content to AI companies than we do off digital media. Why? Because if you’re going on vacation to Park City, Utah and you want to know what’s the hot new restaurant, the only place that information exists is in the *Park Record*. And so that’s actually incredibly valuable information. Compare that with kind of what was the winning media strategy of the last ten years. And it was like write the same story about what’s going on at 1600 Pennsylvania Avenue, but tell it to your own tribe and write the most incendiary headline you could possibly have on top of it. That media doesn’t have a place in a world that is intermediated by AI in the future. Your AI’s going to do that for you. But you know it would be amazing if the Washington Post covered like you know not only what’s the best hotel in DC but what’s the best hotel room in DC. Let’s get super micro granular to that and and in and also you know the the nature of journalism has been you have a certain number of column inches, you do all this work, and then it has to get reduced to this tiny amount. Like all those reporters' notes, all that other information, I mean that’s the most valuable stuff that you could be feeding into these things and obviously there are things you have to work through, but I think there’s an amazing way that we can actually again be on the cusp of of what is much better media going forward. With the clock ticking out I want to get to one other project that you’re passionate about which is skiing. You also want to buy the local ski area from Vail. Yes. Is that going to happen? They’ve had a tough year. Well I think the one thing we can all agree on is that Vail has destroyed skiing. And and that they are they are a terrible capital allocator. I mean they’re worth like four billion dollars sitting on like thirty billion dollars of assets. So something’s going to change there and I you know I grew up in Park City, I feel like I owe the community a lot and and I think that that there’s no reason that we can’t really invest in that mountain and give back to it. I don’t need to make any money off of it but but you know I as I try to think about like what are the things that I can do to give back to the community, sure the local newspaper is great but being able to support that community and make a great ski resort would be even better. Does the bad season that we just had skiing have any effect? I am the only person that was happy about that bad season because I was like huh makes it slightly more likely that they’ll be willing to sell. Thank you. Thank you so much for the time. It's been great. Thank you. Reimagining Global Economic Levers. Semafor. Citadel | Securities. Please welcome Phil Frank of Rendezvous Robotics, Dylan Taylor of Voyager Technologies, and Gina Chua of Semafor. It’s always nice to get the applause before we say anything. All downhill from here. Phil, Dylan, thanks so much for being here. So of course we are having a space moment right now. You know the the images from space amazing and they give everybody a great sense of wonder. We do have a bit of a business focused, bottom line focus group here. Why does space matter? Why should we care about space? Who, well I’m one of the few people who had the privilege of actually going to space myself. I did a suborbital flight in December of 2021. So I saw the earth from space. Totally changed my life. It’s something you can’t unsee or unfeel. So I think the meta point is I think space is a unique perspective shifter. And I think in a world where we’re trying to evaluate what our next step forward is, I think space can be a tool for transformation in many respects. Two other points I would make: lots of scientific innovation, microgravity research, which I know we’ll talk about, space stations, commercial space stations. Microgravity research has really unlocked a lot of innovations in biopharma, space manufacturing and the like. That benefits life directly here on Earth. And final point I would make is inspiration. You know I think Apollo inspired an entire generation of engineers and innovators. I think the Artemis generation centered around this initiative we now have to get back to the moon has a similar promise. Not only for STEM education but also just what’s possible. I think we’re all captivated by this Artemis mission around the moon and manned at four new heroes. Yep. So I think I think those are the three reasons: scientific innovation, perspective shift, and inspiration. Four new heroes and two new craters. Phil, plus also the socks, that’s a really great... Yeah I have to rep NASA here. If you can’t see the socks you should see the socks. No I would repeat almost verbatim what Dylan said. I think it’s really cool this year you know the the national pride, the inspiration that Artemis really kind of reinvigorated that we haven’t seen I at least within kids in a long long time. You know going back and remember watching a lot when you were young really inspired you know a lot of us to be in this industry. The I mean there’s not much cooler things on this planet than watching a rocket launch live, like actually there. That is a pretty amazing thing and you got a lot of kids actually watching YouTube SpaceX launches all the time. So it’s not just Artemis. You know there is there is a lot that I think is really accelerating which is cool. But now let’s get the brass tacks, right? What is the business in space? Right? I mean so so SpaceX and Starlink have proven there’s a business. Starlink obviously sells to a ton of commercial, you know, consumers on Earth. SpaceX is highly dependent on government contracts. So what is the real business? I mean you’re looking at building a commercial space station. You’ve got a technology that can assemble larger structures in space than we can possibly do now. What’s the real business here? Especially if you factor out the government business which, you know, is important. Yeah. Well it’s a it’s a great question. I mean first of all fundamentally space is a denominator in every global business you can think of, right? Everyone’s phone is attached to global GPS. Most business plans created nowadays rely on satellite-based technology. So I think the fundamental answer is ones and zeros. Now that we’ve built the reliable reusable elevator to space if you will, and I credit Elon and others for that, we can now get more hardware to orbit. We can get more hardware to orbit to collect more data and of course with machine learning and AI we can generate new insights. So one thought experiment I like to use is we have roughly a seventy trillion dollar global economy, round numbers. Could we make the global economy two percent more efficient by having perfect real-time information about the earth? I think the answer is absolutely, we could probably make it ten percent more efficient. And if that’s true you’re talking about, you know, a trillion to ten trillion of value creation just from space-based assets. So I think that’s one easy answer. And then the longer term answer are things like the moon, if we think about technologies like nuclear fusion that relies a lot on Helium-3. The earth is running out of Helium-3, the moon has a ton, literally and figuratively, of Helium-3. So that’s an interesting business model as well. Orbital data centers—which data centers here on earth not only take a lot of power but they take a lot of water. Right. And there’s some regulatory not-in-my-backyard considerations with data centers. So I think orbital data centers is another key business model. So I think generally speaking you’re going to have the launchers, you’re going to have the infrastructure creators, and you’re going to have the picks and shovel suppliers. I think those are the three general categories. Yeah, I mean there is so much that is immature at the stage. I mean maturing fast. I think launch is probably the furthest along from a commercial standpoint. Not a balanced supply and demand however, so that does need to improve. I always look back at the analogy when I first started in the wireless industry which was a long time ago and at that point in time everybody asked us why... who’s going to use these things? You know who’s going to use a mobile phone and walk around with it? You know fast forward twenty-five years later we built a trillion dollar economy on the back of it and it really changed everybody’s life around the world. It took a few things to get there. It took global standards and cooperation. And where the space industry is right now I think there is there’s great cooperation which is one of the cool things about the industry. It’s one of the things that we need to do in terms of working together but if Elon has built the the yellow brick road to space or FedEx for space you know somebody’s going to build the Emerald City. And I think it’s in humanity’s interest that you know we do this. Economically speaking there is a tremendous amount of data up there and there’s a tremendous amount of discoveries that we’ve already made, you know, in small batches, but at scale is really going to drive I think, you know, an economy that’s going to benefit all humanity but it’s also going to be massive from a financial standpoint. But a lot of this kind of does require government money to get it kick-started, right? Because if you don’t have that, you know, as with telecoms, you needed something to push it along. NASA’s obviously you know put out the the... I’m not sure what the right word is but, you know, put out the requirements for an independent space station and then a couple of weeks ago they seemed to perhaps change their mind where they say maybe the ISS will be there for a little longer and they want commercial modules. Doesn’t that sort of at least throw in a huge amount of uncertainty into the business planning and don’t you need a little more firm direction from government if you want to invest billions of dollars into this? I think that’s been traditionally true, like each administration would have their own kind of Mars or moon or their pet projects. I think we now have seventy-five global space agencies around the world. Space has become a global phenomenon. I think everyone understands that the long-term future for low earth orbit is commercial space stations. I don’t think anyone disagrees with that. How we actually bridge from the current model International Space Station to commercial space stations, I think that’s still trying to be worked out. But I don’t think anybody disagrees on that future. China of course has a brand-new space station on orbit. Which is not commercial. It’s not commercial. In fact all the astronauts who have been on the Chinese space station are active-duty military. Right. So it gives you a sense for how they’re posturing their space-based assets. But I think commercial is the future, and the main reason is because it’s much less expensive and it’s much more innovative. So to give you just some facts and figures—round numbers—the International Space Station, when you factor in all the shuttle launches and everything to assemble that, it’s around four hundred and fifty billion dollars, round numbers. I think it’s the best thing humans have ever built. I think it deserves a Nobel Peace Prize. It’s the most complicated machine humans have ever assembled and it’s flying in space. We’ve had humans living continuously for twenty-five years, so it’s amazing. But we don’t have four hundred and fifty billion dollars to replace it. Commercial space stations are on the order of single-digit billions, you know, somewhere between two and six billion, and you’re going to get modern technology and much larger volume. So I think everybody wins. It’s a classic public-private partnership where NASA benefits, the taxpayers benefit, and humanity benefits. So I think that’s where we’re headed. I don’t see any disruption with that future. I think the transition is what we’re trying to figure out right now. Phil, how much can you drive that one-to-six billion dollar price down? Well, you know the ISS is the size of a four-bedroom house, right? And living in a tin can is not the most appealing thing. If we’re ever going to really accelerate people living, working, and playing in space, we’re going to have to get to much more human-scale and human-grade infrastructure. But the ISS is phenomenal. I mean, our technology wouldn’t exist without the ISS. It was born and experimented on on the ISS. I do look forward to commercial stations very soon because there is so much technical development that could happen in zero gravity. We’re very focused on being able to move beyond that and be able to build things that are much more human-scale and much larger for missions that require that, and they will grow over time. But I can’t emphasize enough that a human presence in orbit, a continued human presence in orbit, is really critical for the industry. So let me ask you to take the other side of the argument. You talked about China; it’s continuously crewed. It’s non-commercial. They don’t have to turn a profit. They are doing things in the interest of their national interests. Clearly, as you say, SpaceX has proven you can get rockets up much cheaper than the government can. What does China gain from having a non-commercial model? I mean, and to the extent that that maps onto America, what does America lose by not having a non-state initiative? I think the fact is, you know, China’s vertically integrated. I think that’s one way to put it, so I think they can move quicker. They’re not particularly innovative. So if you look at most of their space-based technologies, it’s more of a copy-paste of what we have done. So I think you lose on innovation. I also think perhaps you lose this great middle that we have. So I liken it to Apple and Android. Think of two different ecosystems we’re building in space. One is a Western-led ecosystem dominated by the International Space Station consortium. Russia is complicated because they’re part of it, but Western Europe, Canada, Japan—you have that ecosystem. And then you have the Chinese-led ecosystem. I think what we’re in competition for are the hearts and minds of that great middle: Turkey, India, Gulf states, South America, Brazil. These are countries that have space-faring ambitions who haven’t really picked a platform yet. If they’re looking at innovation, if they’re looking at the future of space technology, and they’re looking at an expression of presumably their values, I think the Western-led ecosystem is going to prevail. But I think that’s what we’re talking about. And China could also just be a really fast follower, right? Whether or not that’s stealing your IP or that’s riffing off of your business, I think it’s a very good debate. I mean, open source has done wonders to accelerate innovation in certain areas, and global standards in others. It took a lot of work to be able to get there in the wireless industry. We have that debate right now. If you have innovative IP, is it a trade secret or do you patent it? Do you try and hide it? And, not to get too geopolitical, but I’ve lived through those battles with national security and Huawei and others in the past. So I think to Dylan’s point, there is a great need to be able to kind of bring folks together around this industry. And frankly, I think the US has to win from a standards perspective because that gives us not just the national security elevation but the ability to actually then bring everybody into standards more efficiently. But let’s talk about national security for a while. Again, China owns their entire stack and here we gain the advantages of commercial innovation. But as we’ve seen with Starlink and the war in Ukraine, one person has the ability to sort of flip a switch and choose which side has access. Surely that’s a national security concern as well, right? I think it’s an important innovation, Starlink. I think having heavy-lift rocket capability is an important innovation as well. I think there are laws that we have in this country regarding antitrust and other considerations that I assume the regulators are regulating. So I leave it to them to make those judgments. But I think a world without Starlink would be materially inferior to the world we have today for all the reasons we’ve talked about. Is it perfect? Do we have it all figured out? No. But I think that innovation in and of itself has the potential to lift potentially more people out of poverty than just about anything else. There are entire swaths of the earth that have no internet access at all. So yeah, I don’t know how to answer your question directly other than to say I trust that the people who are charged with evaluating that question are evaluating it. You know, we’ve been working on communications constellations since the mid-nineties, you know, McCaw’s Teledesic with Bill Gates and whatnot. So I don’t think Starlink is the end. There will be private networks that can be segregated and controlled for national security purposes. There’s going to be a lot of constellations if everybody carries through on what they say they’re going to do right now. It is 80% of the economic value that’s generated in LEO today; it just is a fact. And it’s going to get better, it’s going to get bigger, it’s going to be higher bandwidth, and it’s going to fundamentally change the economy on the earth. Like if you follow it all the way to the end—disintermediation—sorry AT&T, I used to work there—disintermediation of terrestrial communications providers is not inconceivable. But back to your point, I do think, you know, let the regulators regulate. But I have a belief in the market economics associated with plurality that will eventually come. I mean, if I’m going to paraphrase a little bit, essentially when you guys are successful there will be competition and one man will not hold all that. But just in the short time that I have—why you don’t have to say it, I can say it—that same person also controls the heavy lift capacity. If he were to compete with either of you, that would be presumably a challenge as well, would it not? Yeah, I mean, a couple things. First of all, our space station Starlab will launch on Starship. We’re one of the first commercial customers for Starship. So SpaceX is an important partner of ours. I don’t see them as a direct competitor on the space station. That being said, if SpaceX chose to focus on any part of the space economy and said that’s what we want to do, I have no doubt they could figure it out. It would be formidable. But I think they’re very focused on what they’re focused on, which is Starlink, orbital data centers, lunar, and Starship. So I think I’m comfortable that the parts of the industry we’re working on are not in direct conflict with what they’re trying to achieve. I think collaboration and competition always happens. Owning your own rocket obviously is a great value. It changes the economic math for you for anything else you try to do. I thank SpaceX and I thank Elon. He’s trained a whole generation of engineers, many of whom now work in my company doing the next cool thing. And yes, I think there will be plurality in launch. You know, it’s coming. It’s not going to be a cornered market. And I think in many ways he’s shown us how to do things really well, and that talent is moving around. All right, well that’s all the time we have for. Thank you very much for coming and you know, the final frontier. Thank you. Reimagining Global Economic Levers. Semafor. Citadel | Securities. Please welcome Chief Economic Advisor of India Dr. V. Anantha Nageswaran and Andy Browne of Semafor. Dr. Nageswaran, thank you for taking the time. You’re welcome. Let’s start with the war in Iran. How is India coping with the energy shock? I think India has been coping quite well so far. We have diversified our sources of crude oil. We have adequate fertilizer stock going into the agriculture sowing season and natural gas availability is doing fine. Cooking gas has been a challenge, but I think the government has been meeting that challenge quite well so far. And also I noticed that the high-frequency indicators we track, such as the e-way bill, movement of goods inside the country, which is what leads to the goods and services tax, I see that the first fortnight of April is actually running at a higher rate than March and February. So far so good. How does it in any way affect your thinking about the balance between coal and renewables? Well, I mean, that’s a different issue altogether. India has been diversifying its energy sources from coal to oil to natural gas to renewables. Very recently we opened up the nuclear sector for both private sector and foreign participation, and in the budget for the current financial year the government in fact completely exempted from customs duty capital goods equipment that go into nuclear power generation, etc. We have also incentivized carbon capture and storage, battery technologies, and also tried to provide the supply chain for renewable energy. This includes things such as the rare earth corridor and critical minerals mission, so we are approaching this as a long-term challenge. We have to diversify our energy sources while at the same time, in the short run, we do have to maximize our existing coal resources as well. This is a multi-year balancing act that India has been pursuing. Let me point out one thing: in the G20 with respect to the nationally determined contribution on emission reduction, India has been one of the leading countries. You talk about supply chains. Right now, green supply chains overwhelmingly run through China. Can India achieve autonomy or independence? Can India achieve what you might call "China out"? Well, that is the challenge that many countries in the world face and it is not a challenge that is unique to India. Of course, there are technologies available elsewhere but maybe at a higher price. I think the attempt to find processing capabilities on critical minerals and rare earths, and also identify alternative chemicals for battery technology, is a challenge that many countries are working towards. Perhaps the challenge of meeting China’s dominance in the global supply chain in many of these areas is going to provide a great spur for domestic R&D and innovation. I mean, necessity is the mother of invention and I’m sure that just as we managed to claw our way back in the active pharma ingredient space, we should be able to insulate ourselves from dependence on one single source. Oh, you mentioned China. China doesn't shed industries and China doesn't share technology. To what extent is that an obstacle? Some of these—not in all spaces I agree—but there are spaces where competing technologies are available even if it is slightly more expensive. But then if you take into account supply chain disruptions and export licensing, I think companies are better off investing in their own R&D efforts—which India has traditionally not done so—but also being able to create a much different set of global supply chain ecosystems even if it is slightly more costly. That is what will stand India and other middle powers in a good state in the long run. Let’s turn to AI, which is a big topic in this event. India needs to create a hundred and fifteen million jobs by 2030. Does AI put that goal out of reach? Not really, because AI’s impact is on a very narrow space of IT-related jobs, either in the IT sector or in other sectors which have an IT component to it. Even there, I think right now across the world, and India is no exception, fear and sort of wild guesses dominate more recent and calmer analysis of the potential speed of displacement of humans by AI and the extent to which humans can be displaced. There is a lot of panic and fear more than recent analysis. When you transfer some of these benchmark laboratory experiments to real-life situations, I don’t think the speed can be matched because you have a legal responsibility, liability, and tasks associated with compliance, cybersecurity, system integration, and bug-fixing. All of these things require human interface. So of course, companies may initially let go of people and reflect back on it later and re-hire them. So we feel that even in traditional simple coding jobs, the replacement of humans will not be to the extent currently feared. That’s number one. Second, on the global capability centers which India has really blossomed in, with eighteen hundred of them located in India which are mainly owned by the Western multinationals. They are using them not as back offices; they are using them as operational nerve centers and that will continue. I think in that space AI can actually be a huge productivity-enhancing tool. Whether or not it leads to job losses, it may slow down the job creation in that space also. In any case, GCCs were not labor-intensive as much as technology and capital intensive. But the real challenge for India is therefore outside the AI space, which is how to create jobs in services and manufacturing or trade skills which are AI insulated. For example, the care economy—children and elder care—culinary skills, hospitality, medical and health professionals, and the creative economy. These are all the areas in which we have to get our youth to focus on to make some of these traditional skills like plumbing, carpentry, and electrical work acceptable, respectable, and fashionable among the youth. That is a big challenge, and that is completely unrelated to AI. I was quite surprised to hear you say that AI will have effect in limited areas. I always think about the Indian economy, the software part of the economy, outsourcing, and call centers. These are towering strengths of your economy. Those are precisely the areas that are going to be most affected by AI, no? I don’t deny that. All I’m saying is from the perspective of the total number of jobs that need to be created, these jobs are still relatively a smaller fraction, although they are in the formal sector and they have driven India's service sector boom over the last thirty years. Those numbers will not change that much as much as there being a slight slowdown and recalibration of the labor content. I don’t deny that. But I don’t see AI actually upending the service sector progress that we have made over the last three decades. Net net, does AI create jobs, does it destroy jobs? Is it Brahma, is it Shiva? No, that judgment is still out in the open. I mean, I think it’s still too early to tell. In the end we may find that it is neither such a super productivity tool nor is it such a major disruptor in the negative sense. It may end up being an incremental improvement over the existing industrial revolutions up to 3.0. Right now there is far more hype than a calmer analysis in this space. You talked about the imperative for India to grow services. Can India become a global manufacturing superpower? The option still exists; the door is still open. I think it is not easy. I can see that because the context in which India is trying to do that is much more challenging than what East Asia could do back in the 1960s to 80s. So I don’t want to trivialize the hurdles and the obstacles that stand in the way. But if anything, as I said in the earlier case of the energy crisis that we are facing today, that should also actually spur more focused action and we are at it. The national manufacturing mission has been launched. Over the last six years or so, the government has done these production-linked incentive schemes in consumer electronics, white goods, textiles, and in the pharma space. We have managed to create a domestic manufacturing ecosystem and, more importantly, ultimately the success or failure of India’s manufacturing aspiration will lie in creating this huge skill ecosystem among the Indian youth. That is where we are focused on. If we do that along with the various trade agreements we have arrived at in the last one year or so, which opens up markets for Indian goods—both labor-intensive goods and services—I think the pathways for India to become a reasonably important player in the manufacturing space exist. The challenge of China's astounding success in this space should not be underestimated, but that should in fact spur us and act as a motivator for us to try and emulate at least parts of it if not the entirety of it. Oh, you mentioned China again. China doesn't shed industries and China doesn't share technology. To what extent is that an issue? Some of the, see, not in all spaces I agree, but there are spaces where competing technologies are available even if it is slightly more expensive. But then if you take into account supply chain disruptions and export licensing, I think companies are better off investing in their own R&D efforts which India has traditionally not done so. But also being able to create a much different set of global supply chain ecosystems, even if it is slightly more costly, because that is what will stand India and other middle powers in a good state in the long run. When you go through Silicon Valley and you go through the startups, they’re full of talented Indians. Why aren’t there more Indian startups in India in the tech space? Oh, I don’t think you have come to India in the recent past. We have a huge startup ecosystem even in tier-two and tier-three cities. Most engineering and management institutions now have incubators and startups there. And I think we have had many more unicorns in the last ten years compared to the previous seventy years or so. The startup ecosystem is vibrant and second only to Silicon Valley in the world. And that is what India has and it is not confined to the top tier metros like Bengaluru, Hyderabad, and Chennai. It has spread to tier-two and tier-three cities. Can India be an AI power? Definitely. In a frugal space India can be an AI power not only for itself, but also setting the model for the global south as we have done in the case of the digital public infrastructure in the last six years. On that note, I think we are time out. Thank you so much. Appreciate it. You’re welcome. Thank you. Thank you. Reimagining Global Economic Levers. Semafor. Citadel | Securities. Please welcome Bob Mumgaard of Commonwealth Fusion Systems, Manoj Sinha of Husk Power, and Tim McDonnell of Semafor. Great. Thank thank thank you, thank you, thank you. Thank you so much guys for being here with us for our last panel of this day actually, which is kind of sad and exciting also. We have two companies that are both taking different angles on improving energy access around the world with very exciting new technologies, especially Bob. So, is fusion here? It’s closer than people think. You know, what fusion power is is the power of the stars. It’s the thing that powers solar, just closer. And what you do is you build machines that make the reactions happen inside of them that then capture the energy. That process now is well understood scientifically. There’s now the first generation of power plants on the drawing board and getting ready to go into the dirt. What’s the timeline for your commercial plant that’s coming? You said breaking ground next year, right? We’re the largest private fusion company in the world, most advanced, and we have a demonstration plant outside of Boston. It is one of the largest and most advanced energy projects in the United States and globally, and that plant’s about seventy-five percent constructed. So it’s turning on systems now, and it’ll operate in 2027. And then the commercial plant after that would be in the early 2030s. That’s already been sited in Virginia, so just south of here, Chesterfield County, in partnership with Dominion. That puts the timeline in the early thirties. Other companies are working on similar goals, if a little bit maybe a few years behind that, and then nations have stated that their goal is the mid thirties. What did you guys at Commonwealth do differently from other fusion startups in the US that allowed you to kind of get ahead of the pack on this? We come out of MIT; we come out of a long line of scientific research, well-published, very close to public-private partnerships, universities, and national labs. So building on top of the science that’s been done before. And then there was a key technical innovation that really happened about ten years ago that we were able to build an entirely new class of magnets. It’s that marrying of the proven science plus a technical unlock in a type of magnet plus the fact that we need a lot more energy globally and that capital had moved back into the energy space. That’s really the combination. Manoj, in emerging markets, one of the sustainable development goals is to have universal energy access by 2030, but we're probably going to miss that by hundreds of millions of people. What’s the most important step that we’re not taking now to actually close the energy access gap in developing countries? You’re right. So I do sit on the World Bank private sector council and I do think that we will miss electrifying three hundred million people by 2030. It has to be three things that have to come together to make it happen in the next five to ten years. Those three things are capital flowing in the Global South, not in tens of billions of dollars—this has to be hundreds of billions of dollars annually. The technology is already there. You have artificial intelligence that can take care of it; in our case, we have artificial intelligence-powered distributed energy resources that can power tens of thousands of villages in the most efficient way. So technology is there. The policy is evolving thanks to the World Bank, and energy compacts have been signed by many countries including Nigeria and others. So policy, capital, and technology. Technology is there. We need a lot of capital and we need policies that can give a return expectation to investors to attract that capital. My main challenge right now is access to billions of dollars that has to flow in these markets to electrify people in a sustainable way for the next hundred years. I mean, what’s the pitch that you make to Wall Street to get that capital flowing? What needs to change to unlock that? So two things in my opinion. The first: is the market big enough for them to even pay attention to that market? If you look at the India market alone, we are looking to invest two point four trillion dollars in power infra over the next ten to twenty years. If you look at the Africa market, the quantum is similar. Now can you generate eighteen to twenty percent return on those asset classes or not? Wall Street needs to figure that out, if it is getting sufficient return on the investments and if the risk is not big enough. In my opinion there’s a difference between a perceived risk and actual risk when you invest in infrastructure in these countries. These countries have one of the most forward-looking—when I say these countries, India and Nigeria—forward-looking energy policies that can give you a viewpoint of the next hundred years. I don’t know why you would not put money in those markets where you can generate more than twenty percent return when the risk actually has been mitigated by the policy change. There is a catalyst that the World Bank is bringing through grant funding programs to bridge the gap that exists in this market. So in my opinion, Wall Street needs to pay attention to the Global South because the return is there, the risk is not that high, and policies are there to generate return on a long-term basis. And you think with the off-take price and the cost of capital that the rate of return is possible? There is a need for a catalyst, like I said, the World Bank grant funding program for Nigeria is a catalyst for the next five to ten years. Do we need that catalyst for the next hundred years for markets like Nigeria? The answer is no. We do have pieces in place to generate a decent return on investment with a long-term horizon and the likes of the World Bank program is needed as an interim method to catalyze that capital. Technology—solar prices have come down ninety percent. Battery prices have come down more than fifty percent. If you look at the levelized cost of energy of these asset classes, it’s very competitive in the Global South market as well. Bob, you got the world’s first corporate PPA for fusion with Google. Can you tell us a little bit about how that deal came together? What’s the pitch that you make to tech companies or data centers or hyperscalers to take a bit of a risk on this technology? When you think about what fusion is, fusion is energy that’s effectively unlimited. It’s energy that’s unconstrained by natural resources; it’s not about consumption. It’s not about what’s under people’s feet or access to a pipeline or a shipping lane. Instead it’s energy as technology. If you know how to build a fusion power plant and you build it, then you have energy for as long as you want that plant to be there. And so that model of a technology shift to unlock a new way to get energy is something that technology companies understand. When you think about what AI has done, it’s intelligence in a new way that’s effectively unlimited. Of course, those two technologies have a link where energy is needed to power AI, and actually AI makes fusion better. So we have had a long-time partnership with Google around using AI in fusion and it was natural to say, well, let’s actually power it. That relationship—which was from a shareholder standpoint but also an intellectual and research standpoint—naturally has moved into a customer and a power provider standpoint. Do you have other PPA deals like that under negotiation now? We have announced another one with ENI, a large Italian energy company and long-time supporter. They are looking at where the energy transition is going to go, and we have others that aren’t yet announced. You think about what it takes to pull a new technology forward: it takes people that look and say, "Hey I can understand this from a first principles; I can underwrite this from first principles," versus a five-year trailing average of plants that have been built for thirty years. Manoj, last word for you here, also thinking about the future. I mean, when you picture energy access and grids in emerging markets in 2040, how does that look different from today? What’s in your crystal ball? In my crystal ball I see not just thousands but millions of decentralized energy resources close to the customers, close to households, and close to the industrial hubs. They are managed automatically almost by AI that can do demand response and spur economic activity around those areas. That’s how I see the world evolving in terms of prosperity in the Global South. Bob, we just had the DOE Under Secretary on stage just a few minutes ago. We were talking a bit about their strategy for fusion. What should the US government be doing differently to accelerate fusion in the US, especially in competition with China? The US has been a historic leader in fusion in terms of the science. The reason that today you can build facilities like ours is because the US government has had long-term research programs that have laid the foundation. However, that’s now shifting into a much more deployment-type mode where we need to get plants built. We need to get the first plants built, and that’s an area where we don’t really have an industrial policy for fusion in the United States. So we don’t have an equivalent of the policies that helped solar and wind, or that are today doing small modular reactors and nuclear fission. The first step is to put those in place. In fact, they’re already enabled by law; they’re just not yet funded. So there’s about a ten billion dollar gap in federal funding for what would be really required to put the fusion that’s been developed in the United States into a globally competitive mix. Meanwhile, we have large investments being made in Germany, in the UK, and in Japan that are also significantly more aggressive in terms of building plants, building supply chains, and moving it to power on the grid than they are in the United States. And China too, right? In China, what you have is a very large program—a program that you can photograph from outer space—that has very large facilities. They are somewhat behind technologically but making up for it in capital and speed at a rate that we’ve never seen in this field. And so, you can tell where countries are going to end up in this energy source by where the investments are going today. We’ve got investments in Massachusetts, private companies like ours—in our case, we’re at three billion dollars. In China’s case, it’s an order of magnitude more than that and we need to catch up. Are we at risk of losing this fusion race to China, you think? Actually, there was a Strategic Competitive Studies Project—a bipartisan Senate commission—I served on that commission and that was one of the conclusions. Not only are we at risk, it is actively happening. Wow, that’s concerning. Manoj, it’s a private company. Am I correct that you’re still planning an IPO in the next three years? Not next year, in the next three years. Within the next three years? What’s the strategy behind that? You know, there’s been some recent history of energy companies going public; it hasn’t always worked out for all of them. What’s the advantage that you want to get out of that? The story that we’re trying to tell to Wall Street—whether it is Wall Street in the United States or Dalal Street in Mumbai—we don’t know which will buy our story yet. But we are open to capital markets in India, capital markets in Nigeria, and obviously capital markets in the United States. The story that we are telling is that green electrons is probably the easier thing to deliver. What we are really creating is an ecosystem of these local manufacturing and industrialization efforts that will spur the economic activities of these thousands of communities in India, Nigeria, and many other countries in Africa. We are creating a new economic system by bringing green electrons sustainably and reliably to people at an affordable price point that opens up massive economic opportunity not just for us, but for other companies as well. To me that story is super exciting. It’s not about delivering electrons. In my opinion that is very boring. What is exciting is the industrial revolution. Yeah. Okay, great. Manoj, can you say a little bit about supply chain constraints? What kind of work are you doing with suppliers in China for example? Where are the bottlenecks? So in the current environment, of course, the supply chain has become much more risky. When we do these decentralized assets in India, our supply chain is largely in India, so we are not as affected by events that are going on around the world except for prices that have increased. But when we are doing business in Africa, in particular Nigeria, most of the things are imported, so we are exposed to the macro events that are currently ongoing. In my opinion, and connecting back to what I was saying, we are not just bringing electricity to these communities in Nigeria or other countries in Africa; we are really creating a local industrial revolution. We are creating industrialization that they have never seen—that the Global North has already seen or even India has seen now. When you create local industrialization, the solar panels will be manufactured in Nigeria. You don’t have to go to China to purchase your solar panels or lithium. I mean, lithium mines are right there in DRC. You can also process hopefully in countries like Nigeria and procure locally. The local manufacturing is getting enabled by electrification and that ecosystem will solve the supply chain problem in the long run. That’s really interesting—kind of actually bringing more of it home. Do you think that in countries like rural parts of India or in Nigeria, the places where you’re building these, are we ever going to have a grid that resembles the one we have here in the US with long-distance transmission? Or have they sort of leapfrogged that approach entirely? What I was saying earlier is the electrification program in the Global South doesn’t have to copy-paste what happened in the Global North, because it happened 100 to 150 years ago when some of these technologies including solar batteries and AI were not available. The future of energy in countries like India and Nigeria looks like a central grid and distributed energy resources coming together through the policy framework and managed globally in those countries using AI. Demand response is possible now at the individual level that was not possible five years ago because you now have AI and you now have data. Future of energy for India and Nigeria is very different. We don’t have to replicate the central grid model which is not as capital efficient and is very susceptible to climate change. So you bring both assets together—centralized and decentralized—drive it with AI and better policy, and that’s a much more resilient model. You don’t have to use 65% coal on your grid. You can do only 10% coal on the grid if that is necessary. So it’s a very different equation of solving energy access and simultaneous transition problems in the Global South. Exciting times. Bob and Manoj, thank you so much. Thank you. Reimagining Global Economic Levers. Semafor. Citadel | Securities. Please welcome Semafor's Ben Smith. Wow. I think that is literally never happened to me before, but maybe it’s because like half the room is Semafor employees, I'm not sure. But I just—thanks, thank you all so much for joining us at Semafor World Economy. One of the best parts of this has been just how high quality the audience here has been and how thoughtful and engaged you’ve all been in conversation in the hallways and over-hearing your conversations in the hallways and in the feedback we’ve got from our speakers. We’ve been very, very grateful to have you here. We just got the numbers: we did 380 interviews this week including half the cabinet, a quarter of the Senate, and we had 500 CEOs through. But honestly I think I’m proudest of, and a lot of the feedback I’ve gotten is from, folks who work on this sort of event a lot and said, "You know, we were really surprised that you asked these folks such tough and uncomfortable questions." I think we get a lot of—since we launched Semafor as a news business that has events very close to its heart—we’ve gotten a lot of people ask, "How does that work?" And the answer is it’s journalism and we’ve been so thrilled to see my colleagues use these stages just to break so much news. This thing has been a festival of news and we’re just really grateful to you all for being part of it. So thank you and we hope to see you here next year. Semafor World Economy. April 13-17, 2026. Washington, DC. Live Agenda. This live event has ended.

Demis Hassabis Wants to Build AGI. Should We Trust Him?

The Next Big Idea Podcast(33.7K) ·69:18 ·256 views · 2026-04-17
Demis Hassabis (AI Chief, Google) Sebastian Mallaby (Journalist)
Demis Hassabis, the Nobel-winning founder of DeepMind, is driven by a unique blend of scientific rigor and "spiritual" ambition to build AGI, balancing a high-stakes rivalry with OpenAI against his desire to remain a "good" steward of humanity's most powerful invention.
TL;DR Demis Hassabis, the Nobel-winning founder of DeepMind, is driven by a unique blend of scientific rigor and "spiritual" ambition to build AGI, balancing a high-stakes rivalry with OpenAI against his desire to remain a "good" steward of humanity's most powerful invention. A...

TL;DR

Demis Hassabis, the Nobel-winning founder of DeepMind, is driven by a unique blend of scientific rigor and "spiritual" ambition to build AGI, balancing a high-stakes rivalry with OpenAI against his desire to remain a "good" steward of humanity's most powerful invention.

The Evolution of a "Jedi" Polymath

  • A Diverse Background: Hassabis’s journey to the top of Google’s AI efforts began as a child chess prodigy (ranking 2nd in the world for his age) and a video game designer of the hit *Theme Park* at age 17.
      • Parental Influence: His character is defined by a tension between his mother’s Christian values of compassion and his father’s "win-at-all-costs" competitive drive.
      • Academic Range: Unlike many Silicon Valley leaders, Hassabis is a deep scientist, holding a PhD in neuroscience which he used to model artificial intelligence after the human brain’s "action and perception" mechanisms.
  • Charisma and Control: Journalist Sebastian Mallaby describes Hassabis as having a "Jedi" quality—an ability to stare people in the eye and make them believe his vision for the future.
      • The "Good vs. Great" Conflict: While Hassabis strives to be a moral leader, Mallaby notes a "blind spot" regarding his controlling nature, evidenced by the 2019 departure of co-founder Mustafa Suleyman.

Scientific Breakthroughs: From Atari to the Nobel Prize

  • The Core Innovation: DeepMind’s early success was built on fusing two distinct AI traditions:
      • Deep Learning: Identifying patterns in massive datasets.
      • Reinforcement Learning: Learning through experience and feedback (trial and error).
  • AlphaGo to AlphaFold: After defeating the world champion in the game of Go, Hassabis immediately pivoted to biology, recognizing that the "combinatorial space" of games was the perfect training ground for scientific discovery.
      • AlphaFold Success: By solving the 50-year-old "protein folding" problem, DeepMind provided a tool to the global scientific community that is now accelerating drug discovery and medicine.
      • Algorithmic Invention: Mallaby argues this proves AI labs are doing deep "algorithmic invention," not just scaling up data centers.
  • Open Science: In a show of "virtue ethics," Hassabis made the AlphaFold database open-source and free for all scientists worldwide.

The OpenAI Rivalry and the LLM Blind Spot

  • Missing the Language Wave: Despite DeepMind being the "OG" of AI labs, they were caught off guard by ChatGPT.
      • The Gaia Project: Hassabis was focused on "Gaia," a simulation of the natural world, believing AGI required an agent to act in physical space rather than just processing "symbols" (language).
      • Shift in Strategy: The release of GPT-3 in 2020 forced Hassabis to pivot DeepMind's resources toward Large Language Models (LLMs) to catch up with OpenAI.
  • Perceptions of Rivals: According to Mallaby, Hassabis views his competitors through specific lenses:
      • Sam Altman: Driven primarily by the pursuit of power.
      • Mark Zuckerberg: Driven by shareholder value and product addiction.
      • Elon Musk: Seen as a "hypocritical" figure who oscillates between "doomsterism" and aggressive acceleration.

Existential Risk and the "Spiritual" Quest

  • AGI as Faith: Hassabis views the quest for superintelligence as a spiritual endeavor akin to "the God of Spinoza"—the idea that understanding nature through science brings one closer to the divine.
  • The Probability of Doom (P-Doom): While Hassabis remains "cautiously optimistic," the briefing highlights a growing concern regarding AI safety.
      • The Survival Instinct: Geoffrey Hinton argues that we may accidentally grant AI a survival instinct by tasking it with defending itself against cyberattacks.
      • The "Race Condition": There is a persistent tension between Hassabis's safety principles and the market pressure to release "open-weight" models (like Google’s Gemma) to compete with rivals.
  • Conclusion on Character: Mallaby concludes that Hassabis is "both great and good," but acknowledges that the "greatness" of the task often requires a pragmatism that can conflict with pure moral idealism.

Notable Quotes

"If you're right... it follows that you as the creator of AI will be one of the most important people in the history of humanity, which means you don't have a choice. Somebody's going to write a book about you." — Sebastian Mallaby (recalling his pitch to Hassabis)
"He [Hassabis] is the only person who was really at the origin of the science and also a lab leader... Sam Altman, not a scientist, dropped out of Stanford, very different kettle of fish." — Sebastian Mallaby
"We are defined by the desire to invent as human beings and he [Hassabis] is part of that and he is leading us on a frightening ride, but it's kind of human destiny to go there." — Sebastian Mallaby
"You put those three things together: superintelligence, survival instinct, and devious obfuscation. It feels like analytically I can't be confident that we're not going to be attacked." — Sebastian Mallaby (on the risks of AGI)
I'm Caleb Bissinger. And I'm Rufus Griscom. And this is the Next Big Idea. Today, Demis Hassabis wants to build superintelligence. Should we trust him? For three years, two men met regularly at a pub in a leafy neighborhood in North London. They'd climb the rickety wooden stairs to the second floor, and there, in a room that was always empty, they'd sink into leather chairs beneath a tarnished chandelier and talk for hours. One of the men was journalist Sebastian Mallaby. The other was a local boy, a chess prodigy who'd grown up to be a video game designer, a neuroscientist, a Nobel laureate in chemistry, and one of the most important and powerful figures in the world of artificial intelligence. His name is Demis Hassabis. Those conversations, along with interviews with more than 100 people in Demis's orbit, are the grist of Sebastian's new book, *The Infinity Machine*. It does all the standard biography stuff, charting Demis's improbable journey to becoming the guy in charge of Google's AI. But what really got me interested in Sebastian's book is something he writes in the introduction. A new kind of intelligence, he says, is being willed into the world by a remarkably small number of people. Each of them is driven by a particular mix of curiosity and hubris, vanity and avarice, idealism and craving. And the sobering reality is that for better or worse, the quality of their characters will affect society. So, what motivates Demis? What are his talents, his flaws? What does he think of his rivals? What does he think the world will look like if he succeeds? And if he really believes there's a chance AI could destroy us, why keep building it? These questions affect all of us, and we'll try to answer them right after the break. And then Rufus, why don't you come back at the end of the show and you and I can talk about what this all means. Yeah, let's do that. I mean, I can't get over this notion that there are half a dozen people leading the top AI labs in the world, and how they think and what drives them arguably could not be more important. Sebastian, welcome to the Next Big Idea. So great to be with you. I have this post-it note stuck to my notes for this interview here, and it's a quote from Henry Luce, the founder of *Time* Magazine. He believed that what journalists should do—and this is what I've written on the post-it note—is tell the history of our time through the people who make it. And I think that's what you've done with your new book, *The Infinity Machine*, about Demis Hassabis. And I think that's what we're going to do over the next hour on the show: try to tell the history of our time through the people who make it. And so I want to begin by asking how you chose your particular history maker, how you chose Demis. He is not as kind of messianic as Elon Musk, he's not as Machiavellian as Sam Altman, he's not as pugnacious as Dario Amodei, he's not as Pollyannaish as Reid Hoffman. So what is it that drew you to him? Why write a book about him? Partly it was that he was the OG, the first guy to get into this game. You know, he set up DeepMind in 2010, four or five years before OpenAI was founded by Elon and Sam. And even before 2010, he had been dreaming about building AI since he went to college in the mid-1990s at Cambridge University in England. So that was one thing. Through his life story, you can tell the making of modern AI because he does stretch back to the origins. And then he led all of the stuff that went on really until ChatGPT came out. You know, his lab, DeepMind in London, was clearly the top AI lab in the world. And then there was this exciting fight in the last part of the story which made it even better because, you know, ChatGPT came out and Google and DeepMind—Demis Hassabis's team—were kind of behind. And so there's an exciting catch-up story at the end of the book. We'll get into that. And part of what made it, I think, particularly painful for Demis and the team at Google was that a lot of what allowed ChatGPT to work was building on research and breakthroughs that Google had made themselves, that DeepMind had made themselves. But I think actually, you know, that gets to a second good reason to choose Demis as the focus of a story about modern AI, which is that, you know, he led the science. He is the only person who was really at the origin of the science and also a lab leader, right? You've got people like Geoffrey Hinton in Toronto and, you know, Yann LeCun and Yoshua Bengio, who are sort of great scientists who laid the foundations. But in terms of people who actually founded an organization to build it at scale, Demis was the leader. And, you know, he has a Nobel Prize to prove it. Sam Altman, not a scientist, dropped out of Stanford. He is a very different kettle of fish. So for almost three years, you and Demis met regularly at a pub in North London where he's from, where he lives. And you guys would sip cappuccinos and talk. I think you say that you did something like 30 hours of interviews with him and then you spoke to at least 100 people in his orbit. And I was really surprised. Like, he came and spoke to you when things were going really well at DeepMind, and he came and spoke to you when things were not going very well at DeepMind. And, you know, this is an era where I think we've seen tech CEOs be rather skittish around journalists, right? Like, I think Mark Zuckerberg would much rather go on a comedian's podcast and talk about martial arts than spend 30 hours with a journalist who's going to ask him difficult questions about the company that he's building. And so what is it about Demis that led him into this project and what do you think it is about you that made him feel comfortable opening up? Well, you know, I went and pitched him. I was very lucky. I went and pitched him in November 2022 on the idea of spending super long amounts of time with me so I could write a book about him. And I thought that he was fascinating. I'd known him for a few years at conferences and stuff. I also thought AI was fascinating, would maybe be a bit niche, but was going to break out at some point. And then a week after I pitched Demis, ChatGPT dropped and it went from the fringe to the mainstream way faster than I expected. But what I said to him in the pitch was really two things. First is, all of his lectures had been saying for a long time that artificial intelligence would be the most important invention in the history of humanity. So I said to him quite simply, look, if you're right, it follows that you as the creator of AI will be one of the most important people in the history of humanity. Which means you don't have a choice. Somebody's going to write a book about you. You may be able to decide which particular writer you spend time with, but you can't negate the reality that there will be books, plural, about you. So let's just get real about that. Second thing I said is, look, if you are going to disrupt human life on the scale that you say—with, you know, people bringing up their kids in a new way because why would they learn to write if chatbots can do it for them, people thinking of themselves as humans in a different way because we used to define ourselves as the most intelligent species in the world and now there's this machine intelligence that might surpass us... If you're going to disrupt people from head to toe, you owe them an explanation of why you're doing it. What motivates you? Why do something this dangerous? And unless you provide that explanation, nobody in society's going to accept it. And, you know, I presented myself as somebody who had written other books. I try to understand the people I write about. It's independent, but I'm not out to, you know, knuckle people gratuitously because I've been around the block. I'm 61, I've done five other books. I know that all humans are flawed, including me. And so, you know, I'm going to expose all sides of you, but I'm not going to be vicious and judgmental in excess because I think the world is negative enough anyway and that's just not my shtick. And so he went with that. And that idea that all humans are flawed, I feel like, is kind of the animating principle of this book. Like the way you set it up in the early pages, you almost frame it around a question of virtue ethics. Demis does seem to be a good person who has good principles, who is pursuing this for good reasons. And yet his character, his temperament, his integrity, his choice of where to stand in moments of challenge—those things are going to matter not just for him and for his company, but for humanity. And that there's this tension constantly in him between his desire for discovery and his desire for scientific breakthrough. He has his desire to be good, and then there are the pressures of the market, and the pressures of the AI world, and the pressures of being perhaps the person who invents the last technology humanity will ever invent. Speak to that. Anything you want to add there? Yeah, I mean, you're right. That question of whether you can be both great and good is kind of at the core of the book. I think in history, if you look back at people who are termed "great," whether it's Alexander the Great or Catherine the Great or Peter the Great, you know, these are all people who basically led armies and killed a lot of people. So they weren't necessarily good. In the 20th century, maybe a new tradition began where scientists like Einstein were called great. Maybe other intellectuals, Solzhenitsyn or whatever. Winners of Nobel Peace Prizes were called great. But in Demis, we have somebody who not only is a scientist, but he's also a really powerful actor in the world who leads this major lab that's actually making things that change our lives. So it's a bit, not quite back to the military leader, but it's a bit back to the agent, the agentic thinker. And can you be good and great? This is the big question. And I think he struggles with it. And you could in the end judge him harshly and say, well, he knows it's dangerous, but he's still building it, he's inflicting it upon us, we can't forgive him for that. Or you could say, well, he represents a version of all of us because we all see technology as both frightening and exciting, and yet we take that trade. We move forward with it, we accept it, because if we didn't, we would still be living in caves. So to some extent, I think understanding Demis is kind of therapy for the rest of us who are going to live through AI. Because when you understand him deeply, you see we are defined by the desire to invent as human beings, and he is part of that. He is leading us on a frightening ride, but it's kind of human destiny to go there. My wife thinks I have an online shopping habit. She would say addiction. I think that's a strong word. It's true I've been taken lately with beautiful spoons and spatulas made of wood, running shoes, colorful backpacks. We all have our weaknesses. The frustration of course comes when you find the perfect item and then you see the dreaded credit card entry page. Such a chore. Then you sometimes see the purple pay button that has all your information saved, making checkout as simple as a tap on your screen. Shopify makes checkout easy. It's the commerce platform behind millions of businesses around the world and 10% of all e-commerce in the US, from household names like Allbirds, Cotopaxi, and Gymshark to brands just getting started. Shopify is your e-commerce expert with world-class expertise in everything from managing inventory to international shipping to processing returns and beyond. See less cars go abandoned and more sales go with Shopify and their Shop Pay button. Sign up for your $1 per month trial today at shopify.com/nbi. Go to shopify.com/nbi. That's shopify.com/nbi. Here's my personal approach to insurance. I don't need it for small stuff like electronics or flights. Over time the math doesn't work. But it does make sense for the big stuff: health, home, and life insurance. Life insurance isn't something you buy for yourself. You buy it for the people you love and who rely on you so they're not scrambling. They're covered, they have less worry about in an already difficult unexpected time. Fabric by Gerber Life makes it easy to give them that. Fabric by Gerber Life is term life insurance you can get done today. Made for busy parents like you, all online, on your schedule, right from your couch. You could be covered in 10 minutes, often with no health exam required. If you've got kids, especially if you're young and healthy, the time to lock in low rates is now. There's no risk, there's a 30-day money-back guarantee, and you can cancel at any time. Fabric also has free digital wills, tools to invest in your kid's future, and more, all right from your phone. Join the thousands of parents who trust Fabric to help protect their family. Apply today in just minutes at meetfabric.com/nbi. Meetfabric.com/nbi. And use my link so they know I sent you. meetfabric.com/nbi. Policies issued by Western-Southern Life Assurance Company, not available in certain states. Prices subject to underwriting and health questions. Well, let's take a whirlwind tour through his background. I don't want to spend a ton of time on sort of his youth and young manhood, but I want to just sort of tease out some of the elements of his early life that shaped him and that we can still sort of see alive in his character today. So he's born in North London in 1976. He comes, to my surprise, from incredibly modest, humble background, right? Talk a little bit just about his parents. I mean, his mother was, I believe, an orphan. She lived in Singapore growing up. She's Chinese-Singaporean, and she was an orphan for a bit, and I think she was rescued off the streets by a benevolent relative. And she later made her way to London, trained in nursing, and that's where she met a Greek Cypriot Londoner, and that was Demis's father. So you have a kind of classic melting pot, you know, striving lower-middle-class story. And it's kind of interestingly American, right? You think of these people who come out of nowhere with immigrant backgrounds as kind of a Silicon Valley profile. But in fact, London has that too. In fact, four-fifths of the workers, people with jobs in London, were not born in Britain. I mean, it's really a melting pot kind of place. And Demis's co-founder, by the way, Mustafa Suleyman, was also of immigrant extraction in North London, went to the same school at some point. You know, very Islamic parents, no books in the home, no music, father couldn't... was pretty much illiterate in English. So, yeah, these are pretty striking origins for somebody who's going to go and change the world. My reading of your book is that there's sort of two things that he gets from his parents that we see in him to this day. From his mother, who's a devout Christian, he gets this sort of a compassion, empathy, and friendliness. And from his father, Demis was a... you can talk about this... he was an extremely elite and competitive chess player from the age of four into his teens. And I think at one point he was the second-best ranked chess player in the world for his age when he was like 13. And his father is sort of his... not his coach, but sort of his chief cheerleader and also his chief critic during those chess years. And from his dad, he gets this unbelievable ferocious drive, intensity, and competitiveness. And those are kind of at odds with each other in my mind: the sort of wholesome, compassionate, empathetic friendliness and the win-at-all-costs ferocity. Do you see those at war within him, and what's winning? Yeah, I mean, you said that very beautifully. I mean, you're quite right. His mother would tell him it's a bad thing to control other human beings; don't try to control other human beings. And his father was sort of urging him on to great glory in chess. And although I think Demis outgrew his father's influence pretty young, like by 15 or so, because Demis was just so clever and so independent, so much an autodidact, and his father wasn't in that league. Nevertheless, that early chess circuit clearly shaped his character. There were wooden boards underneath the table so that one kid couldn't kick the other viciously as that other kid was trying to think about the next move. I mean, it was bitterly competitive. Children bursting out into tears when they lost games and burning out on the chess circuit, going to alcohol. Really, eerily intense. You ask the question: how did Demis reconcile those two sides? And I think the answer is, you know, he hasn't really. I mean, right at the end of the project, the deal I had with him was look, this is an independent book, but you're allowed to check the quotes because this is on background. And when I do that deal with people, I always give them a lot of context around the quotes because otherwise, how can they possibly judge what they feel about the quotes? And because Demis is so quotable, I quoted him all over the place. I gave him the whole thing. I just said, look, I'm not promising to change a comma outside the quotes, but I'll listen to any comments you have. And so one of the fights we had in a series of exchanges, which culminated in a three-hour meeting in that pub in London, you know, where we really hammered it out on the points of disagreement... But there was one particularly pointed argument where, you know, I'd written that he was controlling. And he said, "No, no, no, I always told you I didn't—I didn't want to control people." And I said, "Yeah, I wrote that you didn't want to control people, but you know, you're running a big company, you are controlling. You're the smartest guy in the room, you win every argument, you are controlling." And he kind of didn't want to accept that. And that's the tension. By their nature, charismatic people are both going to control others in arguments, and they're probably going to create a kind of desperation on the part of people around them to kind of be in their good books. Because the affirmation you get from a charismatic person when they say something good to you is the biggest high emotionally you can get. And at the same time, the suffering you feel as one of his entourage if he criticizes you or argues with you or disagrees with you—he's so powerful at doing that, whether he likes it or not, that you suffer. And so David Silver, one of the leading reinforcement learning AI scientists in the world who was Demis's friend and worked with him in his first startup as well as at DeepMind, he basically had a nervous breakdown working with Demis. He had to go off and spend a year in France somewhere in the countryside recuperating after the experience of doing a startup with Demis. The controlling thing is so interesting to me. It's almost as if maybe that's like a shadow part of his personality that he knows is maybe there, and he's trying desperately to tamp it down and not let it surface. Well, you know, that's why the figure of his sort of doppelganger Mustafa Suleyman—his co-founder who he eventually fired in 2019—is so interesting. Mustafa went off and, you know, ultimately joined Microsoft and for a while was the top AI person at Microsoft, although I think just recently maybe he's been slightly made to share that pedestal. But you know, Mustafa Suleyman is a strong leader in AI in his own right and had this super close relationship with Demis when they were co-founders. They would talk every night for like an hour or two on the phone and in the early hours of the morning. Almost big brother little brother it felt to... was that wrong in my reading of that? No, you know, you're quite right. I mean, Mustafa was eight years younger and he had not completed college. He was very smart, but he dropped out of Oxford because he was a man of action who wanted to actually go do stuff, and he founded a thing called the Muslim Youth Helpline, driven by that Islamic background which I talked about before. So, you know, there he was, he was not a trained scientist. He joined DeepMind as kind of the business person and looked up to Demis because Demis was older, Demis had already made some money, Demis was a PhD scientist, and Demis is Demis. I mean, he's quite an extraordinary guy. So, yeah, big brother is right. And ultimately Mustafa, you know, wants to throw his weight around and be a leader because that's sort of who he is as well, and there wasn't space in the organization for both of them, and Demis ended up pushing him out. Now, Demis would never accept pushing him out. He would argue forever if he was on this podcast with you: "No, no, no, I didn't, you know, it was just a process, the lawyers led it, my chief of staff, whatever," you know. And it's like, dude, you know, you ran the company. Everything that happened in the company was happening because you'd okayed it. And Mustafa got pushed out, so own it. I mean, come on, you know, it's not unusual for startup founders to part ways after nine years. No harm no foul, it's okay. Just say fine, I get it, I accept I did that. No, no, he won't. Demis seems to want to be the good guy. Having pushed out your co-founder and your little brother, that's not good for the good guy image. Yeah, no, I think you're hitting it spot on. I mean, he does have this desire to be good and, you know, that's where we started our conversation, and yet in practice, you can't always be good if you're wielding power. You have to make tough decisions and sometimes you fire someone. I wouldn't say that Demis has very many blind spots at all. I think most of the time he's incredibly honest and lucid about both what's going on, but also what his own motivations and thinking processes are, and it—and it all makes total sense. I mean, when it came to why did you get overtaken by OpenAI product-wise, you know, he was very honest, very forthcoming and very interesting. But just on this specific thing about control, I would say that's one blind spot. Let's just rewind a little bit and put a button on sort of how he ends up where he ends up. So he's this chess prodigy, that sort of leads him to a fascination with computers because he's interested in modeling games of chess on computers. And that in turn leads him to an obsession with games, with gaming, with video games, and then ultimately to AI and the interaction between all those things. I think we sometimes feel like, because ChatGPT exploded and now AI is everywhere, we sort of forget that people were working on AI for a really long time before it hit the mainstream. And Demis is one of those people. What drew him to artificial intelligence in the first place? Well, as you said, it was gaming right at the beginning in the mid-1990s. Around 1993 or so, he was admitted to Cambridge University, but he was very young—he was only 16 or so. And so the university said, "Look, you're intellectually and academically completely ready, but you are very young and you won't have a good time socially, so why don't you just do something else for a bit and then come in a year." So he occupied that year by joining a game design studio and coding up a game called Theme Park, which proceeded to sell more than 5 million copies and made him both rich and a bit of a celebrity. In fact, the boss of the game design studio was called Peter Molyneux, kind of an OG legend of game design in Britain. And this guy Peter Molyneux wrote a check for more than a million dollars in today's money and said to Demis, "Hey, I want you to do the next game with me, skip going to college, stay with the game design studio and here's your bonus." And remarkably, Demis didn't cash the check, right? More than a million bucks, you come from a poor background, you're only 17 or something at the time. Wow. So he went off, preferring science to more money, and studied computer science at Cambridge. But what happened at the gaming studio was partly that there was this culture of, you know, staying up late coding and then kind of meeting for spaghetti in the kitchen at sort of four in the morning and talking. And the talk would be about AI. And Demis, you know, I think was seized by the idea that if a human brain is essentially a computer with electrical signals and synapses and 1 and 0 signals rather like a silicon computer, whatever the brain can do in terms of being intelligent is something that computers will be able to do one day. And that was sort of the bedrock faith that he developed even before he went to college. Therefore he thought AI would be possible and therefore he was determined to be the guy who brought it into the world and unlocked all the mysteries of science, particularly physics, which he was passionate about.
