And so, you know, even when he was 18-19 at college, he had a piece of paper in his dorm room with his kind of plan for AGI. I don't think he used the term AGI then, but his plan for super intelligence. It was just really, really early and you've got to remember AI systems couldn't recognize the photograph of a cat until ImageNet in 2012. So we're talking like 15-17 years before that, and Demis is already committed to that path. You just said he kind of thought he could be the guy to solve this. And we were talking earlier about his charisma. So many other people seem to think he's going to be the guy to solve this. So he goes off, gets a PhD at University College London, and there he meets Shane Legg, who will be one of his co-founders at DeepMind along with Mustafa Suleyman, who we just mentioned earlier. They start this company in 2010. And before long, Mark Zuckerberg is trying to buy DeepMind, Elon Musk wants to buy DeepMind and fold it into Tesla, and Google ultimately prevails and buys DeepMind. And at that point, DeepMind is not... they don't have a killer product, they don't have tons of research breakthroughs under the hood. Like, it's kind of just their brains that these companies are bidding for. And yet, all of these people are convinced like these might be the guys who are finally going to crack the code on AGI. What was it about him and his co-founders that had convinced these people from Elon to Zuckerberg to the folks at Google—this is the guy? So I think a lot of people who meet Demis Hassabis think this is the guy. He just has that magic. Did you think that when you met him? I did. I thought he was remarkable. I didn't realize the full depth of how interesting he is, how he can switch from discussion of philosophy to movies to theoretical physics to neuroscience, you know, and do it all seeming totally intelligible, approachable, non-arrogant, friendly. He's like the guy next door, you know, chatting about "hey I think it's time to wash up the dishes after lunch," except that he's talking about these mind-blowing concepts. It's quite a remarkable mixture. So I think that's the first thing. You know, his colleagues in his first startup used to joke that he was the Jedi because he would stare you in the eye and say, "You will believe these things," and then people did believe them. So he has a kind of magic. But in addition to that, more seriously and scientifically, what DeepMind did very early was to fuse the two central traditions in artificial intelligence research. The first one is deep learning, which is essentially learning from enormous quantities of data and trying to find patterns in that data. And that's what underpins the early ChatGPT versions, you know, much later on. But the second kind of artificial intelligence is called reinforcement learning, and that is learning from experience. The system tries an action, it gets a feedback signal from its environment. So it might be playing a game, for example, and it plays a game, plays move, opponent plays a move and at the end it either wins or loses and that's a feedback signal which helps it to understand if its moves that it tried were good. And by repeating millions and millions of games, it gets a lot of feedback signals, and then that is how it learns stuff from trial and error and feedback. So these were two separate traditions historically in AI; they were embodied by Geoffrey Hinton at Toronto and then Richard Sutton, the sort of father of reinforcement learning who was based in Edmonton in Canada. So two British scientists, both of them operating out of Canadian colleges. And there was a scientist called Vlad Mnih of Ukrainian extraction, a Canadian-Ukrainian, and he had done graduate work with both of these two traditions, and DeepMind hired him. And because of Vlad and because of others also who were minded to try and bring together the two traditions, that's what they pulled off with the Atari game-playing system which they did in 2012. That was a breakthrough. I mean, it was kind of ImageNet, which was, you know, the ability to recognize photographs. ImageNet was pure deep learning, and what DeepMind contributed was an ImageNet equivalent but for agentic systems which were using reinforcement learning in addition to deep learning. I should say as an aside there's this really funny moment in the book where Demis is starting DeepMind and he's trying to raise money and one of his investors says you have to come to my house and you have to pitch me and my wife and my 14-year-old son and we all get a vote and you have to convince three of us if I'm going to write a check into your business. And Demis is like, oh my God I have to explain this to a 14-year-old kid. I am that 14-year-old kid. So when you're talking about the Atari system, it's basically like they're using deep learning in order to allow this AI to see and understand the gameplay and then they're using reinforcement learning for it actually to play and through trial and error figure out what are good moves and what are bad moves? Is that how those two overlap? Yeah, I mean that's the start of the explanation. I mean Demis had, you know, done his PhD interestingly not in computer science but in neuroscience. And his reason for that was he wanted to understand how human intelligence worked because that was the existence proof for artificial intelligence being possible. One of the big ideas he got from doing neuroscience is that intelligence consists of many different components inside the brain, right? So you have the hippocampus and the cerebellum and so forth and so forth. And in layman's terms, there are different bits of the brain which some of which do planning, some of which do memory, some of which do imagination and so forth. And what's really magical is when you put them all together and they interact really cleverly, that produces real intelligence. And so with Atari they were applying that intuition. And so for example, one of the things that they did was that the system would play many, many games to try to get feedback signals about whether moves were good or bad. So you've got all these moves and you've got all these feedback signals, and then you want to learn from those pairs of action-reward what moves will lead you to victory in the future. And for complicated reasons I won't get into, the system won't learn well if it tries to derive conclusions in real-time with each feedback signal. Because it will over-index on sort of one micro part of the game where your paddle is stuck in the top right-hand corner, it's doing these repetitive shots in the top right and that will kind of mislead it. What you do is you collect like a million different pairs of action-reward, you put them in a memory bank, and then you have a bunch of data in the memory bank and then you can do deep learning as if it was ImageNet recognizing a photograph on top of all those stored experiences. It's quite a subtle series of ways of combining the idea of reinforcement learning to collect win-loss feedback and then deep learning on top of a memory bank of those results to produce a game system that wins. You know just a minute ago you were saying it's an almost dizzying experience spending time with Demis because he'll be talking about a movie and then he's talking about theoretical physics and then he's talking about biology, like he just sees this all as part of this stew that he lives and thinks in. I'm thinking about that because you're also now saying that you have these oil and water camps, right: deep learning, reinforcement learning. They're completely separate tribes. And Demis says these can be united, these can overlap and these can intermingle, much as he does in conversation, you know, connecting threads from all... Do you think there's—does that say something about the way he thinks, the way he sees the world? 100%. I think he's Mr. Anti-Silo, right? He's super cross-disciplinary. And actually over the holidays at the end of last year I was having a break between finishing this book and then needing to come on podcasts and talk about it. And I read a book called *Range* by David Epstein. And, you know, it makes the point that a lot of the people who do the best, whether it's in sports or in music or in lots of fields, they have range. They don't just specialize in one thing and do whatever it is, you know, 3,000 hours of practice and then they're great. That's the kind of Malcolm Gladwell learning and maybe I got the number wrong, but the point is dogged specialization wins. No, no, no it doesn't, actually. Experimentation, allowing you to bring inspiration from one subject into another one, tends to do better if you're really going for excellence. And Demis embodies that. Tell me if this has ever happened to you, because I can tell you it's happened to me. You leave a meeting and you think, "Wow, that was productive, we accomplished so much, we set all these goals." And then 10 minutes later, half hour, you stop and you realize, "Wait, what am I supposed to be doing?" You look back at your notes and they're just kind of a jumble of random keywords that don't make any sense. It's a mess. But Granola fixes that. Granola is an AI-powered notepad built for the way real people actually meet. You take rough notes just like you normally would, but in the background Granola securely transcribes the meeting and it turns everything into clean, structured, actually useful notes when the meeting ends. And the best part is that it works through your device's audio, which means it integrates seamlessly into the video conferencing tools you already use. So there's no setup and none of those awkward bots that you end up just kicking out of the meeting. We've been using Granola for months now, and not only does it let all of us just be a little more present in meetings because we're not distractedly trying to take our own notes, but it also creates this incredible living archive of everything we've ever discussed as a team or with external partners. And by the way, it's a beautiful interface. If meetings are eating up your day, Granola is a no-brainer. You can try it totally free for three months. Just head to granola.ai/idea. That's granola.ai/idea to get your time back. Get three months free at granola.ai/idea. So if we're thinking about the breakthroughs and the innovations that have made DeepMind sort of the cornerstone of modern AI, one is uniting deep learning and reinforcement learning, as you just described. Another is embracing the transformer, embracing transformer architecture, which is something that emerges out of Google I think in 2017 and DeepMind embraces. Describe what that technology is, why it's so significant to today's AI. Sure. So with ImageNet, which was sort of the big breakthrough for images, what the system was really doing was understanding that each pixel in a photograph is probably going to be related to the pixel right next to it. Because, you know, they're both part of the cat's tail or something. And that intuition that there is a relationship between different bits of the puzzle you're trying to solve is helpful in narrowing down the search space that you have to conquer when you're figuring out what the picture is. Now, in sequences, sequential data—think about, you know, a text, lots of words. Different words in the sentence will relate to each other in different ways. It's not the same as a picture where you kind of have clusters of related pixels. Because also in a sentence you might have a reference back to something that happened in the previous sentence. If I say, "I dropped my keys," and then the second sentence is, "I can't get into my house without them." The 'them' is the pronoun that makes no sense unless you can relate it back to the first sentence. And to unravel the meaning of sequential data—and that could also be not just text, it could be the clips in a movie, it could be the ticker numbers in a stock market... Any sequential data, you need to figure out the relationship between the different bits in the sequence. And to make a long story short, the point about the transformer innovation was that it was the first time you could really solve the meaning of big amounts of sequential data. You could put a whole book into a transformer and it would understand all of the interrelationships in that sequence. You could put, you know, a whole movie in, etc. And so really that was the significance of the 2017 paper. And it brings speed to the process, right? Because it doesn't have to understand things token by token, word by word, because that would be an incredibly slow and time-consuming and laborious process for an AI to go about understanding a book. If it's taking it token by token, the transformer allows it to sort of see it almost in multi-dimensional space and therefore understand it and engage with it much, much, much faster. Correct. And you know, the funny thing was that the GPU chips that were being used by this point, you know, ubiquitously for AI, had this feature that they could parallelize operations. And yet that ability to parallelize things hadn't been fully exploited until the transformer model came along. And then the transformer architecture, you know, ironically tackled sequences without paying attention to the order in which these sequences played out. They were able to feed enormous amounts of text; kind of different bits of that text simultaneously would be processed and you didn't have to do it sequentially. That liberation, as you're suggesting, was why you could suddenly deal with way more data much, much faster because you weren't doing it ploddingly, you know, one token after the other. How did all this coalesce to allow Demis and DeepMind to solve the problem of protein folding? Well, that was very interesting because the original impetus for taking on protein folding, aside from the fact that Demis had been personally fascinated by it since he was at college... Because a friend of his had mentioned the protein folding problem back when he was at college... like while they were playing like table football, right? Like one night as a stray comment at a bar. Yeah, that's right. And that's how—kind of how Demis operates, right: he's a magpie, he collects these bits of interesting challenge or information, puts them in the back of his mind and then bang, something happens later. Could be a long time later—and he thinks, "Oh, I remember, yeah this is now the right time to take on that thing I heard about in the college bar when I was 19." And so the moment this 'aha' moment came that this was the right time was when DeepMind had defeated the South Korean Go champion in a kind of exhibition match in Seoul. The system called AlphaGo had defeated the human and Demis was walking out of the exhibition hall where the game took place with his friend David Silver. Coming down the street in Seoul—and we know this because coincidentally there was a camera with a microphone kind of following them and this was picked up—Demis says, "We've done that, we've done that, we've solved Go, so the next thing is we can solve protein folding." And what he meant was that Go had this immense combinatorial space, right? You have 19 by 19 on the board, that means there's 361 squares. That means the first move there's 361 possibilities and then the second there's 360 and so forth. And very soon you multiply all these options together: move, counter move, move, counter move, and you get to this impossibly large number of combinations. To solve that game had been a grand challenge in computer science precisely because of the huge combinatorial complexity. And the point about protein folding was that with protein folding you have this strip of amino acid and on it there's the DNA code. And the DNA code governs how it's going to do a self-executing origami shape. The strand of amino acid folds itself up into a super complex, intricate shape. And then these are the building blocks of nature. And to predict the shape just from the amino acids means predicting a shape that could be one of billions, trillions, you know, a massive number of possible shapes because each little atom can twist either way. And so it's—it's just enormously complicated. But Demis's point was "Hey, if we solved Go, which was a big combinatorial space, the next thing is obviously AlphaFold." And there you see precisely how you can't pigeonhole him, you know: it goes from games to structural biology in the same breath. So he sets DeepMind to work on this. And the first idea is, you know, David Silver having conquered Go with reinforcement learning, will apply reinforcement learning to AlphaFold, to the idea of protein folding. And they would have the same recipe. And after not very long, like six months or so, they quickly understand that that's not going to work. And they basically go through a series of experiments where they pivot and they pivot, and another thing doesn't work and they pivot again. And along the way, there emerges this young scientist called John Jumper, who had been obsessed with protein folding forever. He brought in the transformer model. I guess this was sort of 2018-2019 when he brought it in, and so it had been invented a year or a year and a half before. And he decided that the sequences of the DNA on the amino acid strands which make up protein could be subjected to a transformer-style sequential modeling tool. He used the transformer and he threw out the reinforcement learning. There was no reinforcement learning in AlphaFold. And so, you know, when I think it's interesting about this story and all the pivots that went into it and finally, you know, solving all of the shapes in nature and getting a Nobel Prize for it, is that this achievement completely belies the nonsense that some people say where they say, "Well, the labs aren't doing algorithmic invention, they are merely scaling." "They're just obsessed with building bigger and bigger data centers, there's no real invention," you know. Nonsense. Nonsense. You know, this was exactly the opposite. There was enormous algorithmic experimentation, pivoting and innovation. This is also an area where what will flow from it will be profound for human beings, right? It's not just creating funny videos; it's going to unlock drug discovery, advance medicine... So the consequences for humanity of figuring out protein folding are remarkable and not to be underestimated. You've alluded to this already in the conversation but there's this sort of, you know, DeepMind's kind of riding high, they've figured out protein folding, and yet it's OpenAI, a lab started by Sam Altman and Elon Musk, in part because they didn't trust Google and Demis to be the only people building and potentially controlling AGI. And it's OpenAI using some of the breakthroughs that DeepMind had made to create and launch ChatGPT, something that, you know, DeepMind was not even really particularly focused on building a large language model, an LLM, at the time that ChatGPT launched. What was going on? Was Demis just asleep at the wheel? Like how did they—I mean, in retrospect it looks like a pretty big blind spot. Right, right. And I would ask him this, you know, repeatedly when we were up there on the top floor of that pub, in the kind of secret room that nobody knew about and there was just the two of us and we had two hours together each month. And the most interesting part of the answer as to why he missed this and let OpenAI get ahead went back to his studies of neuroscience. Because in neuroscience there is an idea called action and perception, which basically says that intelligence requires the intelligent agent to act in the world. You won't know what gravity is until you've felt it, until you've dropped an object and seen it fall to the floor. You wouldn't know the difference between liquid and solid unless you had a glass of water and you tipped it and then the liquid fell out and splashed. You need to understand the physical world, and that's how humans do a lot of their learning and therefore by extension artificial intelligence will either need to be robotic and act in the world or at the minimum act in game simulations of the real world. And so Demis was pursuing that vision of how you get to super intelligence, and he had a fascinating project called Gaia. The idea was to build a simulation of the natural environment, which is way more irregular and complex to act in than a man-made environment. You know, there's no right angles in nature, you get these enormous trees and tiny little blades of grass; it's super irregular and therefore very hard to impose categories on it. And that's something which is part of what intelligent beings do: they kind of classify objects to understand the world. So the idea was to build Gaia and get to super intelligence that way. And then another project he had going on was an agent that played the real-time multiplayer game StarCraft 2. And the point again was that this is a super complicated game which requires you to plan over long horizons, you can't see the whole landscape, you have to therefore act in a fog of uncertainty—can an AI agent handle that? was the question. And in the meantime what he was not doing so much was focusing on language. And his view was, "Look, language is a system of symbols, and you can understand symbols—great, but we know from action and perception and neuroscience that that ain't enough, that won't get you to super intelligence." And he basically persisted in this conviction until 2020, when GPT-3 came out. And that was really quite a strong model. And suddenly Demis said, "Wow, okay, I was wrong. We need to catch up. We're going to do language big time." And he threw a ton of resources and started to try to catch up. In that effort to catch up, it does seem like Demis had to betray some of his principles about how AI should scale. Right, he's always been aware that if things go right with this technology it will be wonderful for humanity, but there are very real risks that it goes wrong and if it does it will be catastrophic. And so he's always been concerned about safety, speed, and avoiding a "race condition" where you have multiple labs racing each other and taking big risks. And all of a sudden he finds himself thrust into this world where, oh my gosh, we're on our back foot, we gotta catch ChatGPT, and now they have another new model coming out and it's better than ours, and oh my gosh Anthropic just released this model and it's incredible, and oh my gosh the Chinese just released this lightweight model and it's maybe better than... Like all of a sudden he has to—you know, we've been talking since the beginning—can you be good and can you also be great? That tension is right there, right? He has to decide: do I get into this race because that's what the company wants and that's what the company needs, but does that mean sacrificing some of my principles around safety and speed? Yeah. And we saw an example of that actually just last week, when DeepMind released a new suite of its open-weight light models called Gemma. And the reason that's revealing is that, you know, Demis would say, has said to me, look, open-source models can be very dangerous because people can download them, modify them, do whatever they want with them and you can't control that. Whereas if you serve them a proprietary model, A, they can't mess around with the safeguards you've put on it, and B, if they start to use it for a cyber attack or something you can switch it off. And so you give up those things with open-weight, and yet he's going ahead with open-weight. So that really illustrates how he's doing stuff that he himself would recognize to be sub-optimal for safety. And, you know, the big I think interesting, exciting, terrifying thing to be watching in this AI race right now is how all of the different lab leaders process this position that they are in because they're all in it. And we saw Dario Amodei from Anthropic pick a fight with the Pentagon and try and assert some principles about how AI is used for mass surveillance or for autonomous lethal weapons. Basically he got rolled, indeed attacked by the Pentagon, and he's having to fight law cases to protect the understanding of whether American labs can work with him or not. So, you know, you could say he made a principled stand, but you could also say not clear it changed the world for the better and it might have damaged his company, although I think hopefully he'll be fine. I think, you know, Sam Altman on this very day that we're recording, Monday, has just put out a big essay about how to manage the kind of labor market disruption and inequality increases that will come from AI. So he's making a public stand about it. Demis believes, I think, at least as strongly as either of them, and probably a lot—a lot more strongly than Sam does—that he wants to be good and that he would like AI to be beneficial for society. He built AlphaFold and, by the way, gave it to the world. It's totally open-source; any scientist in the world can use it for free. He does this because that is AI for the good. It's going to advance medicine for the good, you know, and he's very proud of that and rightly so. I don't know if you, Sebastian, watch a lot of reality TV. Something tells me you probably don't. I don't. There's something they do on these shows called a confessional, where they take one cast member and they put them in a room with the cameras and they ask them to sort of say what they really think about the other cast members on the show. If we were to do that with Demis and we said, "What do you really think about your rivals Dario, Sam, Elon? What do you think he would say?" Well, I've done that with him. And, you know, what he says is that Sam wants power. Sam thought of running for Governor of California; there's rumors that he even wanted to run for President. You know, his objective function is power, and that's how he sees Sam. And that's not something he says with respect. He doesn't like people who are trying to maximize power. I think about Mark Zuckerberg he would say that guy wants money. He wants to maximize the shareholder value. He wants Meta to roll out even more addictive products with the help of AI. I think, you know, Elon is an interesting one because Elon's record on this is so double standards and hypocritical, right? He was very, very outspoken at various times about the existential risk posed by AI. And yet he's also very, very determined to race and have xAI be a leading player in the field. He's both; he's an accelerationist and a doomster at once. I think for whatever reason Demis doesn't like to criticize Elon, partly because maybe Elon has such a megaphone that poking the bear is unwise. I'm speculating there, but you know there have been times when Elon would go around saying, "Well, Demis Hassabis is an evil genius and he's going to destroy us all and that's why we need to have OpenAI as a counterweight to DeepMind." Demis doesn't want to reignite that kind of talk. So, you know, he's a bit tactful about—about Elon. One of the things that's interesting to me with Demis is the way that he talks about this technology. It is different than those guys, different from Sam or Elon, which is that it's infused with a kind of religiosity. It's infused with a kind of faith, and he says to you that his pursuit of AGI is a spiritual endeavor, that it's about trying to get close to God, I think is the word he uses. Does that draw from—from the faith that he grew up with with his mother? Is that where does that come from? To me that's highly unusual, this sense that he's driven not by power, not by wealth, but by faith. Yeah, I mean it's true he did grow up with a religious mother. He did pray in the evenings as a child, he did sing in the choir at the church, and he has that tradition which probably makes it more natural for him to reach for religious terms, spiritual terms to describe what he feels about his scientific quest to understand nature. I mean, we talked about this at some length and sometimes he would try to discourage me from seeing him as spiritual, although he confessed that he did feel it was spiritual. But he didn't want me to overdo that because what he—what he was saying was look, you know, I—I'm a scientist primarily. I want to understand nature. And I choose to sometimes describe that incredibly intense urge to understand nature as being a kind of quest for quasi-spiritual understanding. And I'm not the only one to do this. Demis would carry on, he said you know Einstein talked about the God of Spinoza. Spinoza being the 17th-century European philosopher who—who said that, you know, nature is God's creation and to understand nature as a scientist will get you closer to understanding God. And Demis feels that. And I have scientific friends who have nothing to do with this book, but who when I describe this to them say, "Oh yeah, I feel the same." So I think it's not that unusual for scientists, particularly who are doing things like theoretical physics, to be so passionate as to be spiritual about it. And I think the last thing to say about this is that what we're skirting around here is the topic of how do human beings process the enormity of AGI, of—of being the one to discover AGI? And, you know, some of the time what I observed was people had weird reactions. So Shane Legg, the co-founder of DeepMind, gave a famous lecture which was where he actually bonded with Demis Hassabis for the first time, and this was in 2009 and it was about AI risk. Back in 2009 it was about the possibility that AI would become sufficiently powerful to threaten human existence. And as Shane is delivering this lecture, and I could watch it on a—on a video which exists on the internet, you know you could see him smiling and his eyes are kind of blowing and he kind of giggles. And as he describes more intense existential risk, that giggle gets more prominent. And you think, what is going on? Why is it funny to talk about potential human annihilation? And the fact is that when we humans contemplate our own annihilation, it feels absurd, and the absurd is a close cousin of humor, and so you laugh. That came up a bit in my research. And then another way of processing the enormity of what's going on is to sort of reach for religious symbols and language. And so there's this moment when Ilya Sutskever is talking to his fellow scientists at OpenAI at a company retreat. There's a fire pit and it's the evening, and Ilya produces a kind of effigy and puts it in the fire and said, "This stands for the evil AGI, and when the evil AGI comes we have to burn it." And they burn the effigy. And it was sort of super kind of culty religious feeling thing, yeah you know. And so I think the symbolism of religion is somehow what we turn to to describe the ineffable nature of a super intelligence that's super hard to get your head around otherwise. Despite the ineffable nature of—of AGI, Demis remains cautiously optimistic. That's the phrase he used with you. Why do you think that is? Like how do you think he's managed to justify that position given—given what he knows about the potential risks and dangers here? You know, I think some of it is that you have to say you're optimistic if you're going to carry on. You have to justify yourself, come up with a rationale for—for what you're doing. But I think also there is a Rorschach test which goes on with the prospect of AGI when people compare their so-called probability of doom, the P. How likely is it that humans would be wiped out or partially wiped out by—by AGI? And I think where people land on that spectrum of P depends on their temperament. And, you know, someone like Geoffrey Hinton, the father of deep learning, is very, very doomsterish. But I know former PhD students of his who say that he was doomsterish about the prospect of bioterrorism before AI became powerful enough to worry about. He always had something that he was terrified of. That just rings true to me. When I get up every morning, you know, although analytically I see the case for being very worried about the robots attacking us, and we can go into why I think that, but I really do think there's a non-zero risk that this could happen... I can't feel upset on a daily basis. I just get up, I like the world, I like my job, I like my friends, I have a great time. I feel optimistic and so I'm optimistic still. To parrot Demis. And—and maybe he's just he's got that optimism which is kind of a requirement for an entrepreneur and when you have that, of course you're optimistic still. You can't tease us with that. What is your P, how worried are you about this circumstance where the robots rise up against us? And then maybe also on the flip side, I mean Demis is quite convincing in this book about what the beautiful, abundant, post-AGI world could look like if we get it right. So maybe play those two against each other. Sure. I mean I think the upside abundance I broadly agree with at least in—in the longer term. So, you know, I think AGI or even just powerful AI is going to accelerate scientific discovery. That's going to be terrific for medicine. It's going to unlock a lot of new learning and information for lots of people. It's going to maybe help material science which could in turn lead to inventions that reduce climate change. I mean, for example, nuclear fusion as a source of fuel has made strides in recent years, not least because of AI and the way that both AI can help you to invent new materials that are good for fusion reactions and also can help you to suspend the nuclear plasma in the reactor magnetically. You need a—a very sophisticated magnetic control function and reinforcement learning turns out to be a way of creating one. So I think the sort of abundance story is certainly true for scientific progress. And I think largely probably true for just general human lives. Because I think that we will as humans invent new things to be passionate about, to give our lives meaning, we will compete at games, we will do art, we will—we will occupy ourselves productively and stimulate ourselves. And so I don't buy the story that, you know, when we have no jobs we decline into depression. I think we'll figure that out because of just our—the way we are wired. Now on the downside, I spent a lot of time during my project. I spent, you know, more than three and a half years doing this. And for most of that time I was wandering around saying yeah, sure, the machines will be more intelligent than humans but they're not wired to compete with us. I mean, humans evolved over millennia to survive, to fear risks, and to fight to survive to pass on our DNA. And that was our most basic objective function. And machines don't have that. They're not evolved and they don't necessarily want to survive, and so why would they pick a fight with us? And in fact, Demis had told me about this series of books he read, that science fiction books called the Culture series by Iain Banks, where in an AGI future you have all these AGIs that are, you know, doing their own stuff. They—they have their own political intrigues, they play their own chess matches, they do their own space exploration and the humans live alongside them without being bothered by them and it's all perfectly amicable. I kind of thought, well, you know, I don't—I don't want to anchor my view on science fiction but I do see that. And then I went to Toronto one day and I sat in Geoffrey Hinton's kitchen for two hours and we debated why Geoffrey Hinton, having invented deep learning academically, is so pessimistic about what that's going to do to us today. And I came armed with this knowledge that, you know, on the Rorschach test I mentioned earlier he had always been super pessimistic. So, you know, maybe he was just overdoing it. And we had this debate where he basically explained to me why AI is going to want to survive and therefore I shouldn't be so optimistic. And what he said was look, supposing you create a very powerful AI and you're worried that it's going to be subject to a cyber attack by an enemy AI. You need to defend your own AI, but a human brain is not fast enough to protect against an AI-generated cyber attack. So you're going to have to empower your own AI to defend itself against the attacking AI. And you're going to have to tell your own AI, you need to survive here. Bang, you just did it. You gave it a survival instinct. And then there are all these experiments, by the way, that come out of the red teaming of models before they're released. And, you know, I think both Anthropic and OpenAI have released descriptions of some of these cases where not only does the AI want to survive, not only is it more intelligent than us in many ways, but it also lies to us. It's very capable of obfuscation and dissembling. You put those three things together: superintelligence, survival instinct, and devious obfuscation. It feels like analytically I can't be confident that we're not going to be attacked. That's certainly a grim place, an important message but a grim message to land on. Why don't we close with this: after three-plus years of working on this book, are you any closer to an answer on the question of whether it is possible to be both good and great? I'd say the answer depends on how you judge people. If you want them to be utterly pure and constant in their principles and never controlling of any one, then it's impossible to be both great and good. If you're willing to say, well in the real world people need to adjust their principles, they need to be pragmatic about when you take a principled stand—for example on safety and how you do it—don't use up your capital by taking a stand that wouldn't actually change outcomes in the real world, which I personally find compelling. Because I think judging somebody in the abstract, sort of separated from how the real world functions, is just not useful. I mean, we surely ought to be judged as human beings in the context of the world in which we live. And if you do that, then I think you can be sort of good on a relative basis, which ends up just being good. And so I'm willing to say that Demis Hassabis is both great and good. The book is The Infinity Machine. Sebastian, it's been really wonderful talking with you today. Thank you so much. I've enjoyed it so much. Great, thank you. Thank you.

Why the A.I. Backlash Turned Violent in America

Hard Fork(56.9K) ·60:31 ·2.5K views · 2026-04-17
Sam Altman (CEO, OpenAI)
Public sentiment toward AI is shifting toward radicalization and violence, driven by deep economic anxiety, a perceived lack of democratic oversight, and rising local opposition to massive data center infrastructure.
TL;DR Public sentiment toward AI is shifting toward radicalization and violence, driven by deep economic anxiety, a perceived lack of democratic oversight, and rising local opposition to massive data center infrastructure. Incidents of Physical Violence: Recent events suggest a...

TL;DR

Public sentiment toward AI is shifting toward radicalization and violence, driven by deep economic anxiety, a perceived lack of democratic oversight, and rising local opposition to massive data center infrastructure.

Radicalization and Violent Backlash

  • Incidents of Physical Violence: Recent events suggest a dangerous escalation in anti-AI sentiment from online discourse to physical attacks.
      • Sam Altman Attack: A 20-year-old suspect allegedly targeted the OpenAI CEO’s San Francisco home with a Molotov cocktail; the suspect possessed a manifesto opposing AI and a "hit list" of other tech executives and investors.
      • Indianapolis Shooting: More than a dozen gunshots were fired at the home of Councilman Ron Gibson following his vote to approve a new data center. A note left at the scene read "no data centers."
  • Trust Deficit: According to a Stanford AI Index report, public trust in the technology is plummeting.
      • Only 31% of Americans trust their government to regulate AI responsibly, compared to a global average of 54%.
  • The Rhetoric Bind: Host Casey Newton argues that AI CEOs have "inflamed" the situation by claiming their products pose an "existential threat" to humanity while simultaneously lobbying against safety regulations (such as California’s transparency bills).

The "Data Center NIMBY" Movement

  • Legislative Moratoriums: Local and federal politicians are increasingly moving to block the physical infrastructure required for AI.
      • Maine: Passed a temporary moratorium on data centers larger than 20 megawatts until 2027.
      • Wisconsin: Voters in Port Washington recently restricted the building of future data centers.
      • Federal Level: Bernie Sanders and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez have proposed a national moratorium on data center construction.
  • Economic vs. Environmental Fears: While some view AI as an economic engine, local opposition is focusing on rising home energy costs, environmental degradation, and noise pollution.
  • Lever for Change: Host Kevin Roose notes that blocking data centers is the only "lever" average citizens feel they have left to slow a top-down technological revolution they did not ask for.

Silicon Valley’s Obsession with Longevity

  • Kara Swisher’s Critique: In an interview regarding her new documentary, *Kara Swisher Wants to Live Forever*, the veteran journalist characterizes the tech elite’s quest for immortality as a "narcissistic" distraction from systemic issues.
      • "The Wealth Gap": Swisher argues that the most effective way to increase longevity is universal healthcare, yet many tech leaders support political movements that reduce public health funding.
      • Stunts vs. Science: Swisher dismisses hyperbaric chambers and "biohacking" as "ridiculous" stunts for the rich, while identifying mRNA technology and CRISPR gene editing as the truly promising frontiers of medicine.
  • Healthspan vs. Lifespan: A central theme of the longevity movement is "collapsing" the period of decline at the end of life so that individuals remain healthy until shortly before death.

The Automation of the CEO

  • The "Zuck-Bot": Meta is reportedly building an AI version of Mark Zuckerberg to interact with staff.
      • Training Data: The bot is being trained on Zuckerberg’s specific mannerisms, tone, and strategic thinking to help employees feel "connected" to the founder.
      • C-Suite Utility: Hosts suggest that AI clones could eventually handle repetitive CEO tasks, such as testifying before foreign parliaments, conducting "pre-calls" for events, or answering routine internal inquiries.
  • Synthetic Media Shift: The project mirrors a broader shift within Meta platforms (Instagram, Facebook) away from human connection and toward synthetic, AI-generated content.

Notable Quotes

"Words have power too... someone said to me yesterday they thought [an article] was coming at a time of great anxiety about AI and that it made things more dangerous for me." — Sam Altman (via blog post)
"No one is going to stop the march of AI with a few stray bullets... but it is the CEOs themselves who have been inflaming the rhetoric... telling us that superintelligence is imminent." — Casey Newton
"Death acceptance makes you live longer. Death denial makes you hateful, tiny, and dying quicker. Scientific." — Kara Swisher
"I think the fear and the expectation among the leaders of these companies is that it will get much worse if and when AI does actually start to cause mass disruption to the labor market." — Kevin Roose
I’m Kevin Roose, a tech columnist at The New York Times. I’m Casey Newton from Platformer. And this is Hard Fork. This week, the AI backlash has turned violent. We’ll debate what’s making it so unpopular. Then, Kara Swisher returns to the show to discuss her new documentary on Silicon Valley’s obsession with living longer. And finally, can CEOs replace themselves with AI? Mark Zuckerberg may be giving it a shot. And we wish him the best. All right, Kevin. Well, some very serious news to start. I think you and I both over the past few months have seen public sentiment really begin to turn against AI and this week, unfortunately, we saw that sentiment spill over into violence. Yeah, so most of our listeners have probably heard by now that late last week there was an attempted attack on Sam Altman at his house in San Francisco. A 20-year-old man allegedly threw a Molotov cocktail at the gate of Sam's home. No one was hurt, but according to the criminal complaint against the suspect, this was someone who had a document that identified views opposed to artificial intelligence. He also had a list of names and addresses of other AI executives, investors, and board members. This is someone who was very clearly concerned about the existential risk that AI posed in his opinion, and so decided to take matters into his own hands and go try to attack Sam Altman. From there, he made his way over to OpenAI headquarters to try to commit some violence there as well. And as you say, fortunately no one there was hurt. This incident came just a few days after another really worrisome incident in Indiana. In that incident, an Indianapolis City Councilman named Ron Gibson and his son woke up to more than a dozen gunshots being fired at their front door and a note tucked under their doormat that read "no data centers." This was someone who had been a supporter of a proposed data center in his district in Indiana and had voted to approve rezoning for the project the week before. And I think this is just part of what I am worried is a growing trend of anti-AI radicalization and violence. We should just say like up front, that we are not fans of violence. We do not encourage violence. No one should be doing this. This is very bad for society and even for the sort of proposed policy outcomes of the groups that are most worried about AI. Yeah, I mean in addition to just the moral reasons why it is bad to try to hurt people to achieve a political objective, it also is just very ineffective. No one is going to stop the march of AI with a few stray bullets. So, before we talk about the AI backlash let’s make our AI disclosures. I work for The New York Times which is suing OpenAI, Microsoft, and Perplexity. And my fiance works at Anthropic. You bring up the data center connection in the Indiana case and I want you to lay out some of this backlash that we're seeing to data centers around the country. Data centers are necessary for AI companies to deliver the services that they are building now. Some of the companies right now have a big sort of crunch in trying to deliver as much service as there is currently demand for. But increasingly, we are just seeing people across the country rise up and say, literally, "not in my backyard." Yeah, so I think the data centers and the violence or attempted violence against AI executives share sort of a common fury and outrage. They are obviously very different tactics. I think data centers are kind of the most visible symbol of the AI boom and I think there are a lot of fears and worries and concerns about data center construction out there. Some of them are based on more sound reasons than others, but we have seen not just sort of individual threats against people who support data centers, but also just that there is a lot of political resistance forming in opposition to these data centers. These are people who I think think that this sort of boom is bad, or at least that they don't want it taking place in their neighborhoods. So, describe some of this resistance. The state of Maine recently passed a temporary moratorium that would ban data centers larger than 20 megawatts until November 2027. There’s a suburb of Milwaukee, Wisconsin, Port Washington, which is going to be the home of one of these big OpenAI-Oracle-Stargate data centers. That town recently voted overwhelmingly in favor of restricting the building of future data centers. Basically, you have to get voter approval before you do any of these things. Then there’s also similar efforts going on in places like Ohio, Missouri, Indiana, Georgia, and North Carolina. And there’s also this big federal data center moratorium that has been proposed by Bernie Sanders in the Senate and AOC in the House that would basically put a national moratorium on the construction of data centers. So I think data centers are kind of where this is becoming hottest fastest, but I do think we are seeing also these individual threats on the executives and leaders of these big AI companies by people who just do not think this is headed in a good direction. Yes, and on that point, I want you to describe a little bit some of the broader backdrop here because over the past several weeks, I have seen survey after survey that just says, in one way or another, Americans do not believe that AI is likely to be a positive in their lives. So tell us about some of that data. What you see is kind of a slow turn against the AI industry over the course of really the last year or two. There’s a new report out from Stanford this week, the 2026 version of their AI Index, which sort of catalogs various trends in the AI industry. And basically, their takeaway was that, in the US, people have very low trust in not only AI but on the question of whether their own government can regulate AI in a responsible way. The global average on that question was 54% of like, "do you trust your own government to responsibly regulate AI?" In the US, that's only 31%. The data is a little fuzzier in some other studies. There was a Pew study earlier this year that showed that people's attitudes in the US are more negative than positive when it comes to data centers' impact on the environment, home energy costs, and quality of life nearby. However, more Americans view the economic effects of AI as being potentially more positive. So it's a little fuzzy, sort of depending on how you slice the data and how you ask the questions, but I think it's fair to say that most polls and surveys of public sentiment around AI have shown that people are getting more concerned as these systems get more powerful. Yeah. So let's talk about why we think this turn has happened so dramatically. I think we have a few possible explanations and I want to start by one that was offered by Sam Altman, who wrote a very personal post on his blog. He put as a lead image a photo of his husband and his baby and in this post, Sam talks about the story that was in The New Yorker the week previously, which we discussed on the most recent episode of Hard Fork. One of the things that Sam writes is: "Words have power too. There was an incendiary article about me a few days ago. Someone said to me yesterday they thought it was coming at a time of great anxiety about AI and that it made things more dangerous for me. I brushed it aside. Now I'm awake in the middle of the night and pissed." So what do you make of the idea, Kevin, that a reason for the sort of negative sentiment against the AI companies and the industry at large is being driven by investigative journalism? Well, I don't think that it is the New Yorker's fault that someone showed up at Sam Altman's house and threw a Molotov cocktail at it. This person, the suspect, appears to have had a longer history of engaging with these sort of anti-AI communities on the internet, and I don't think we should stop scrutinizing these powerful people and companies. That said, I do worry that this is going to get worse before it gets better. One thing that I've been thinking about over the past few days is that this is happening at a time when unemployment is below 5% and the S&P 500 is near a record high. So if all of this is starting to happen when things are relatively good, economically speaking, in this country, I think the fear and the expectation among the leaders of these companies is that it will get much worse if and when AI does actually start to cause mass disruption to the labor market. Absolutely, and they have essentially all but promised us that for the past several years. I read Sam's post and thought, "this is sort of right and wrong at the same time." I think that he is right that the rhetoric around AI is really extreme and that some people do take it seriously. One of the people who took it seriously appears to have been the suspect in this case, right? Where I think it's wrong, though, is that it was the CEOs themselves who have been inflaming the rhetoric, right? There’s a 2015 blog post where Sam writes "Development of superhuman machine intelligence is probably the greatest threat to the continued existence of humanity." And the other AI CEOs have said things along very similar lines. So I think to come along now at a moment where the systems are more powerful than ever and the CEOs themselves are telling us that superintelligence is imminent and to say, "well, now we need to tamp down the rhetoric," that just seems sort of crazy to me. At this point, we're not even really talking about the rhetoric, we're talking about the actual technology and the material effects it's already starting to have on people's lives. Yeah, see, I want to ask you about this because I feel like there is a certain bind here that these companies and their leaders are in when it comes to talking about some of the scarier possible outcomes of AI. I think a lot of them watched the social media CEOs claim that their technologies during the last decade would produce nothing but good for the world, right? It was going to connect everyone; it was going to be this glorious utopia. I think a lot of them took the lesson from that that we have to be up front. If we think the thing that we're building has some risk attached to it, like we should be open and honest about that and not sugarcoat it. So I see them as kind of being stuck here because if they did what you are suggesting maybe they should have done and try to sort of de-escalate the rhetoric or sketch a more positive vision, they would have been accused of sugarcoating. But if they talk about the risks that they see and they're honest about their fears, then they're accused of being doomers who only want to escalate the rhetoric and stir things up. How do you think they should square that circle? So I think that there is a third path forward here which is to essentially just try to work with the governments and put more pressure on them to put into place systems that would regulate the companies to mitigate the harms caused by their products. And so far, we have actually seen the opposite, particularly in the case of OpenAI, right? As we have seen more and more regulations get proposed in statehouses around the country, OpenAI is going around trying to prevent those bills from being passed into law. So I think this is really, really important because the way that we solve problems caused by companies in a democracy is that we regulate them. And when the companies themselves are out there saying, "well, we want regulation but no no no no no no not like that, like you can't—you'll harm innovation, you'll prevent us from defeating China," you're just sort of like creating a double bind and that is just going to make voters more and more infuriated. Yeah. All right, so we don't seem to think that journalism is the reason that people are so upset with the AI companies. Let me propose a second answer: economic worries. I feel like when you and I are out there talking to most folks, to the extent that people have a concrete near-term worry about AI, it is that it is either going to totally replace their job or it is just going to make the job that they have now horrible. How does that sound as an explanation to you? Yeah, I am sort of more in line with this view of things that, sort of in the same way that all politics is local, I think that all AI politics or most AI politics ultimately come down to people just sort of looking at a technology and thinking "what will this do to me and my ability to continue to live my life and support my family and, you know, retire comfortably?" Like I think people have a lot of fears about this stuff. And that is, I think, a bigger part of the sort of data center opposition. I don't think a lot of the people opposing data centers are worried about existential risk; I think they're mostly focused on like, "this thing seems super annoying and maybe it's going to take my job and pollute my environment." And I think people are saying, "Wait a minute, we're supposed to be rooting for this? Like, why would I root for something that might make it harder for me to put food on the table?" I really do think that is the skeleton key that unlocks some vast portion of the entire AI debate, right? And it doesn't seem to be something that so far any of the AI companies have really had an answer for. Now, if you sort of, you know, keep them up late at night and have one of these like dorm room conversations, they will describe for you visions of fully automated luxury communism where you don't have to have a job anymore and neither does anyone in your family. And they say all of your meals will be 3D printed for you and it won't cost you a thing. I think that just seems so implausible to people that it's impossible to build any kind of political constituency around it. I wonder if we will see AI companies trying to sell that case a little bit harder in the future, right? Yeah, I mean I think this is the biggest cultural disconnect between the San Francisco-Silicon Valley AI bubble and the rest of the country. I think people here, many of the people I talk to, they are excited about a period of rapid technological change. That is what excites them, they're motivated by making it happen, they think ultimately this will be a good thing for society. I think most people don't think like that. They don't think, "I want to live through a period of unprecedented technological change in which the world becomes unrecognizable to me." I think there are a lot of people trying to send that message by opposing data centers, but I don't think it's really sunk in at the AI companies or to the people running them that most people want stability in their lives. They want to be able to plan for their futures. And when people from Silicon Valley show up and say "hey, we've got this amazing new technology and by the way it might take away your job and there's nothing you can do about it," I think that naturally breeds some fear and resentment. So this gets into the third explanation for what I think is going on here which I want to put under the broad label of anti-elitism, right? This AI moment that we're living through is a top-down moment. It did not rise up from the grassroots, from a bunch of nerds getting together in their garages and training frontier models. It was a small group of really smart people who were able to get access to massive amounts of capital from the elites in our society and they are now mounting this effort to build it very quickly, deploy it very quickly without a lot of guardrails. And so I think when the average person looks at this, they think, "not only did I not ask for this, but I have no meaningful control over it, right?" I think that is a big reason that you're seeing people so furious because, particularly on the left, this just looks like a mostly right-wing elite project that is being championed by President Trump and the many venture capitalists that are in his administration. If you're already worried that it's going to take your job and you don't feel like you have any control over it, well of course you're going to hate it. I don't think this is some like elite right-wing plot but it is definitely an elitist project that is being undertaken by a very small handful of people who are not elected, who don't have all that much accountability. And I think that is in part by design. I think these people have seen that when you do give the public a right to weigh in on how technology is deployed, they mostly vote to stop it, right? As we're seeing now with these data centers, as we're seeing around the country with the backlash against Waymo, which we haven't even talked about but this has been truly surprising to me. Waymo has now a technology in its self-driving cars that is demonstrably safer than human drivers and you would think that that would be greeted as kind of an unambiguous good. Yet, in a lot of places where they're trying to expand, people are saying "no no no no no no, think of all the jobs we'll lose if this technology comes in." Now I think in the tech industry, that attitude is mostly sort of mocked and dismissed as like "oh, like we're literally showing up here with a life-saving technology and you're saying 'what about the taxi drivers?'" And I think there’s a cohort of people in Silicon Valley, many of whom we talk to and know, who just think like this technology is too important to be left to the masses. And I think that is like a misguided attitude, but it is definitely an attitude that is out there. Yeah. I mean I do think it is really misguided because it's one thing to say, "well, we cannot trust the public to vote correctly about how new technologies could be deployed." It is another thing to actively fight against accountability measures or even transparency measures. And I just want to name this as another reason why I think that the public is growing increasingly anxious or even furious. You know, if you just look at OpenAI's lobbying efforts over the past couple of years, they lobbied against one of the first big AI transparency bills here in California and successfully killed it. They have been sending subpoenas to people who work at nonprofits who were in favor of AI regulations trying to insinuate that maybe they were the puppets of Elon Musk. That doesn't really feel like a very pro-democratic move. Most recently, OpenAI backed a bill in Illinois that would shield it from liability in cases where its models are used to cause serious harm so long as they did not recklessly or intentionally cause it and publish some safety reports. So to me, that is not just "hey, like let's embrace the spirit of permissionless innovation and see what kind of cool stuff we can do." It's saying "we've told you we're creating something that could be an existential risk to humanity and we're going to lobby for a bill that prevents us from being held liable." So to me, when I say this technology is elitist and anti-democratic, that is what I am talking about. They're fighting against the mechanisms of accountability and so I understand why members of the public are upset about that. Yeah, I don't think it's that simple. Like I think a lot of these companies have been very open to regulation of some kind. Now they have fought, you're right, they have fought specifically some of these bills, but the people who are building this stuff do believe that it should be regulated. I don't think any of them think that it should be a total laissez-faire free-for-all. I think, you know, they just want there to be smart people making smart policies. Yeah, we—there’s a perfect regulation out there and they can't seem to really name it or describe it or get it passed, but if they hear it someday they promise they're going to line up behind it with their full support. Well I want to just put a little bit of meat on that claim that I just made because they are actually proposing policies, right? The companies—OpenAI last week released this document called "Industrial Policy for the Intelligence Age: Ideas to Keep People First." It's sort of a white paper about some of their ideas for how policy and regulation might need to change in a world of very powerful AI systems. They say we should create a public wealth fund, similar to things that happen in Alaska for oil where every citizen would get a stake in the economic upside of AI, improved safety nets for workers, establishing new public-private partnerships to accelerate energy production. Nothing like truly crazy, but it is just a slate of stuff that like I would be happy if one member of Congress was reading this and thinking "this stuff looks like a good idea." So I actually think it is pretty crazy. Like when you think about corporate policy papers that you've read in your time as a reporter, I'm guessing that you haven't read many that cause for a massive redistribution of wealth. That's essentially what this is and when you look at what OpenAI is proposing and then you look at their political donations and lobbying, they just seem like they're at complete cross-purposes, right? OpenAI is backing a lot of Republican candidates who I'm guessing are not going to support a massive expansion of the welfare state. So there something is going on here that I think at the very least leaves room for critics to say, "Are y'all even serious about this?" Yeah, I think some of what's going on right now at all of the leading AI companies is that they are trying to sort of plan for two worlds. One of them is a world of extreme acceleration in AI capabilities during the Trump term, right? Before 2028. And in that world, it really matters to have good relationships with Republican lawmakers and the White House. There's another world in which they are having to plan for a new president in 2029 and maybe that's a Democrat, maybe it's a Republican, but maybe this stuff all takes until 2029 or so to get really crazy and in that world, you actually want to start planting seeds with people in various different factions and coalitions. So like I think they are trying to kind of spread the bets a little bit. Yeah, and look, I will be the first to stipulate that for the most part, it should not be the job of the private corporations to figure out how America should be governed. But we're in a situation where the government we have has been all too happy to take their lobbying dollars and then do almost whatever the companies are asking the government to do. And so that has just led to a world where again AI just looks like a top-down elitist project that the average person has no control over except in the one dimension where you always have control over American life and that is in saying no to a project being built in your neighborhood. For some reason, this is how we have decided to massively empower Americans: if there's anything that you don't want to see built, you probably actually can make that happen as an average citizen. So that's obviously very inspiring, but I do wish we had other levers that we could pull. Yeah, in part because I don't think this is going to work, right? Like if you vote the data center project out of your town, they're just going to go to another state or to Canada. They'll put the data centers in space, you know? They’ve got options here and I don't think this is going to meaningfully slow down or stop anything. When I hear you talk about this, my sense is that your real objection to the sort of data center NIMBYism in particular is just that you think this technology is going to be really, really good for people and you think people need to get out of the way and let the future happen. No, that is not what I’m saying at all. Like I have real concerns about AI. I have real concerns about the kinds of job displacement that we may see as a result of this technology. But I think my concern is that people are identifying the wrong levers for change. I do not think that stopping a data center from being built in your town has any marginal effect on the speed of AI progress or the proliferation of AI throughout society. I think a lot of it is just what you said is basically true: that people think that this is the area where they can actually change things, just as the thing that people thought they could do in the 1980s to block new construction in their neighborhoods was to throw up a bunch of environmental reviews. Did that help the individual homeowners in that area who didn't want apartment buildings going up? Yes, it kept their views unobstructed. But it also created a massive housing shortage in this state in particular, that directly stems from this kind of NIMBY politics and I just worry that the sort of data center NIMBYism that we're seeing will have some unforeseen consequences down the road. Well, so then what are the right levers to pull if you are worried about all of the harms and joblessness that AI seems likely to create? I think there are real policy proposals out there that people have been putting forward. One of them is actually in this OpenAI paper which is for this kind of, you know, these more nimble and flexible social safety nets that could, for example, catch workers who become displaced by AI and pay for them to be retrained for some other skill. We saw things like this in the past when it came to manufacturing automation where in some countries they have these like job councils where you get laid off because a robot takes that job, but they sort of pay you for a period of time and retrain you to do something else, so that you get to stay employed and keep your standard of living while you're being displaced. Like that kind of thing feels like a better answer to me than just saying "no data centers." It also seems to require America to transform into Europe overnight, which seems somewhat unlikely to me, but Inshallah, my friend. So, what should the AI industry do? I mean, this is the question on a lot of people's minds right now is like what can they do to increase the public acceptance of or favorability toward the thing that they are building, or is this just kind of going to be a project that they have to just push through regardless of public opposition? So I really struggle here because I could tell you a bunch of messaging changes that they could make that might, you know, affect public perception of AI at the margins. This is not really a messaging challenge. The problem is not that they're talking about it in the wrong way. The problem is that they're saying that they're about to create a massive disruption in Americans' lives and they do not have a plan for what comes after that disruption. They've said "your government is going to figure it out" and I think particularly if you're an American right now and you're looking at the government that we have, it's very hard to believe that they're going to sort of adeptly navigate through that level of disruption. So, I think in short, Kevin, we have a governance problem and that while the problem is being driven by the decisions of these like unelected AI leaders, it is ultimately the governments who are going to have to give us an answer to these questions. Well that’s a punt, but... Is it though? It's a punt but it feels like the only honest answer. Like what am I supposed to say, like "well, you know, GPT-6 should—blah-blah-blah-blah-blah"? Like, come on. Yeah, I guess I agree that the ball is in government's court on this one but I've just become so pessimistic about our government's ability to address even a technology that we understand very well. This stuff is moving very, very fast and it's very, very hard for even the most plugged-in lawmakers to get a handle on what the hell they're supposed to be doing about this technology but I would love for there to be a handful of people in Congress thinking hard about proposals that may sound extreme right now, like extreme wealth redistribution, like a token tax... Yeah, well I like what you just said because I do think this feels like a wide-open lane in American politics which is like "I am extremely nervous about AI and if it is going to advance at this pace, I am determined to make sure that it goes well for the average person and I'm going to insist on it and I have a policy plan to make that happen." That’s not really what we're seeing right now. My hope is that there are some power-hungry individuals out there who are looking to run for office and they want to avoid the just sort of easy "this company sucks" stuff and lean into the harder "we have to build policies that harness this stuff for the greater good" because that is where glory lies. If you can figure that out, they will write your name in history and I want to believe that there are some solid American politicians there who are up for the challenge, Kevin. Yeah, they will put you on Mount Rushmore if you figure out how to make this go well. That is our promise to you and at Hard Fork, we will do everything we can to get you on Mount Rushmore. So, we'll make some calls. Well, Casey, we’ve been saying for weeks now that we need to get some of our enemies on the show and today we’ve got one. We really do. Kara Swisher is here. She was able to muscle past security in the lobby and she said she would not leave until we talked to her about her new show on CNN. Yes, Kara Swisher, of course the iconic tech journalist, mentor and friend to both of us, and now podcast mogul, who is out with a new docuseries on CNN about her attempts to live forever, at least to explore the idea of living forever. It is called "Kara Swisher Wants to Live Forever." Whether she does in fact seek immortality is a point of contention, as you will hear in the interview, but, during this series, Kevin and I were able to watch the first two episodes and in it, she tries a lot of the things that the rich and powerful are trying as part of their quest to become immortal. So, this is a big topic. Obviously people in tech are very interested in longevity and living forever and hacking their bodies, as they would hack a software program. And who better to be our Virgil to this strange health underworld than Kara Swisher? I think you'll find that she is rather skeptical about many of the things that she tried, but we do try to sort of press her on what you might do if you were interested in, maybe extending your life for a few years. I have a secret theory, which is that Kara Swisher actually really likes tech and it's just kind of like a nagging thing that's going on. I think it’s a very complicated relationship as they used to say on Facebook. All right, let’s bring her in. Kara Swisher. Kara Swisher, welcome back to Hard Fork. Thank you, I'm so un-glad to be here. Well, last time you came on the show, you insulted our bosses, accused us of stealing your podcast feed, and dropped so many f-bombs that we had to fight with our standards department and record a separate content warning. So, Kara Swisher, welcome back. We’re delighted to have you. Accused means it wasn't accurate and in fact it was accurate. If we want to re-litigate it, I'm happy to. I've moved past it now. I've moved past these things. Yeah. Because I'm bigger than ever. I built a new one. Try to steal my new one. That's right, New York Times, I'm bigger than your little boys there. So, whatever. I could do this all night. Well, speaking of doing this for a long time, your new project is longevity and Kevin and I recently had a chance to watch the first two, highly entertaining episodes of your new show on CNN, "Kara Swisher Wants to Live Forever." Kara, though, my impression from the first two episodes is that the title is a bit of a lie. You do not actually want to live forever. I do not. It is a joke. I know it's lost on someone who's not as sophisticated a viewer, but it is a tongue-in-cheek situation which is I want to go on this journey. It's sort of like a Bourdain except with all this longevity stuff but then actually go into the stuff that's going to help us live a very long and much better life. Yes. Well, so tell us a bit how you got into this. You know, I've known you for a long time, we have talked about mortality a lot. You talk about this app WeCroak in the show, which I have seen the WeCroak notifications going off on your phone sort of sending you messages about mortality. So, how did you get from your interest in mortality to this question of longevity and all of these folks in Silicon Valley who are trying to extend their lives? Well, you know, they're linked together and as you both know, I did one of—I think I did the last public interview of Steve Jobs before he died, about a year before he died at Code. And, he had given a speech at Stanford, which I found amazingly moving, and which was about mortality and about death being a motivator for him. And I liked that and then everybody sort of shifted. At first it started with intermittent fasting, they started talking about that, or Soylent if you recall. Or all manner of body hacking and then it was psychedelics and then they were talking about brain hacking and then Elon started talking about—like—he was an expert on COVID for a while there, and you know, it just morphed into this thing. Then you saw these investments, whether it was Sam Altman, Larry Ellison's probably the most active and earliest around anti-aging. I had a lunch with this woman who kept talking about ending senescent cells, which was odd. It was backed by Peter Thiel. And so I just started seeing it more and more. And then you had all these incredible things like CRISPR and mRNA vaccines and AI, looking at gene folding. So there was all this real stuff and all this really ridiculous stuff. Right. And so you said sort of like "I'm seeing a lot of stuff that seems obviously wrong, but some stuff that seems actually promising." So I want to spend some time and see if I can sort of separate the wheat from the chaff. Right. And I also need to do the stunts because it's funny, right? Like doing a sound bath with Scott Galloway or getting in a hyperbaric chamber. Everything I did was stuff that tech people told me I had to do, an absolute... Now they're on peptides at this moment. But they were always fiddling with themselves. And and and it seemed narcissistic to me. Of course. Let's talk about some of the things that you did as part of your journey on this show. And I'm just going to rattle a few of them off: you did sound therapy, a hyperbaric chamber, you improved your max VO2 by running with this like Hannibal Lecter-like monitor, you did red light therapy, sleep therapy. Of all of these things, which was the most enjoyable and which was the most excruciating? Excruciating... You know, VO2 max is really interesting, actually. I thought that was some real stuff I could use and it really—I have improved my efficiency and stuff by running and taking those tests. I don't think you need to do it the way I did it, there's VO2 max stuff on your wrist and your earbuds now and that was helpful, I would say helpful. I mean, the hyperbaric chamber's fucking ridiculous. Although I enjoyed it, right? It was kind of fun to be in there. Although I don't like small spaces, but it was so stupid, it's so stupid to have all these people insist that this is the way to go and I was like "it's really not, you do know that, like..." What is supposed to be happening to you while you're in a hyperbaric chamber? Oh, people who take it, they're like going on a long trip and they think they feel better because like if you have oxygen out here, double the oxygen in there is better. I mean, that's their mentality. I think—if—or if you have the bends or a wound, it's a great place to be. But otherwise it's just one of these things they sell the rich people and make them feel superior and it's a waste of money. Kara, I have to ask you about your ketamine experience because this was one of the early moments in the show. You did ketamine which is—which is not a life extension thing, we should say it's like a depression thing and people...
